question:
Through which of the following marked sites does SARS-COVID 19 spread to the brain?

[Anatomy-INI-CET-2021-11-image]
explanation:
The given image shows various skull foramina's. Infection through the cribriform plate and
olfactory bulb (A) and dissemination through trans-synaptic transfer are some of the mechanisms
proposed for the SARS-COV2 virus to reach the brain.
Encephalitis, demyelination, neuropathy, and stroke are the neurological sequelae to severe
COVID-19 infection. The neurological invasion of the virus on the medullary cardiorespiratory
center can lead to refractory respiratory failure in patients infected with COVID-19.
205

subject: Anatomy
question:
A boy with a history of a fall from a tree presents with a lurching gait and dragging of his feet.
Which of the following structures are involved?

[Anatomy-INI-CET-2021-12-image]
explanation:
The structure involved in causing lurching gait in the child is gluteus medius (marked as 2).
The gluteus medius originates from the outer surface of the ilium between the anterior and
posterior gluteal lines. Insertion of the muscle is at the lateral surface of the greater trochanter. It
is supplied by the superior gluteal nerve.
Gluteus medius and minimus are powerful abductors of the hip joint. Mechanism of actions of
gluteus medius and minimus:
• When the leg on one side is lifted off the ground, the gluteus medius and minimus muscles, on
the opposite supported side, exert traction on the hip bone by acting from the femur. This action
results in a slight raising of the pelvis on the unsupported side, which helps maintain the trunk in
the upright position.
• The actions of these muscles are affected when there is an injury to the superior gluteal nerve,
neck of the femur, or loss of normal angulation of the shaft of the femur. This occurs in conditions
like paralysis of the glutei, developmental dysplasia of the hip, etc.
• When the gluteus medius and minimus are paralyzed, the pelvis sinks down on the unsupported
(normal) side, when the patient tries to stand on the affected limb. This leads to a positive
Trendelenburg's sign with a characteristic lurching gait - the patient lurches towards the affected
side with contralateral pelvis drop.
206

[Anatomy-INI-CET-2021-12-exp-image]
Other options
1 - Gluteus maximus - involved in the extension of flexed thigh, abduction (only upper fibres), and
lateral rotation at the hip joint. Insertion is at the iliotibial tract.
3 - Piriformis - involved in lateral rotation and assists abduction at the hip joint. It is inserted into
the medial side of greater trochanter of the femur. Entrapment of the sciatic nerve in the
piriformis can lead to piriformis syndrome.
4 - Sciatic nerve - The nerve passes over the obturator internus, gemellus superior, gemellus
inferior and quadratus femoris. It supplies knee flexors and muscles below the knee. Paralysis of
this nerve results in flail foot and severe difficulty in walking.

[Anatomy-INI-CET-2021-12-exp-image]

subject: Anatomy
question:
A patient after a road traffic accident presents to the emergency room with difficulty in
swallowing and slurred speech. Investigations reveal fractures in the occipitotemporal region.
Which of the following areas should be tested in order to find the nerve which is involved?
182

[Anatomy-INI-CET-2021-13-image]
explanation:
207
The given clinical scenario is suggestive of the involvement of 9th and 10th cranial nerves due
to occipitotemporal fracture and they are examined by testing the uvula which is marked as 2 in
the given image.
The jugular foramen is located between the lower border of the petrous part of the temporal bone
and the condylar part of the occipital bone. As the investigations reveal fractures in the
occipitotemporal region, along with the history of difficulty in swallowing and slurred speech, are
suggestive of injury to the glossopharyngeal and vagus nerve passing through the jugular foramen.
Unilateral lesions of IX and X cranial nerves are commonly caused by skull-base
fractures, strokes, or tumors.
Glossopharyngeal and Vagus nerve examination include:
• Observing the position and symmetry of the palate and uvula(marked as 2 in the given image) at
rest and with phonation (“aah”).
• The pharyngeal (“gag”) reflex is evaluated by stimulating the posterior pharyngeal wall on each
side with a sterile, blunt object (e.g., tongue blade)
Patients with a proximal unilateral lesion of the vagus nerve complain of dysphagia. There will be
a weakness of the soft palate muscles on the affected side. Attempts to voluntarily elevate the soft
palate, like by asking the patient to say “aah” will reveal a weakness in elevation of the palate on
the affected side. Along with this, there will be deviation of the uvula to the normal side, due to
the unopposed action of palatal muscles on that side.
The below image shows the base of the skull and its foramina:

[Anatomy-INI-CET-2021-13-exp-image]

subject: Anatomy
question:
Fibers from the marked structure end in which of the following areas of the thalamus?

[Anatomy-INI-CET-2021-14-image]
explanation:
The structure marked in the above image is mamillary body and the fibers from the mamillary
body end in the anterior nucleus of the thalamus.
The mammillary bodies are two small hemispherical, pea-sized eminences, lying side by side,
forming the floor of the third ventricle. The fibres from medial mammillary nucleus project to the
208
anterior nucleus of the thalamus.
The anterior thalamic nuclei have 3 subdivisions—anteroventral nucleus, anteromedial nucleus,
and anterodorsal nucleus.
• Major input: Mamillothalamic tract from the mammillary nuclei of the hypothalamus.
• Major cortical output: Anterior limbic area, cingulate, and the parahippocampal gyrus
• Major function:
• Regulation of alertness and attention
• Acquisition of memory

subject: Anatomy
question:
The cell marked in the below slide is inhibitory to

[Anatomy-INI-CET-2021-15-image]
explanation:
The structure marked in the image is a flask-shaped neuron in the cerebellar cortex, which is
a Purkinje cell. It is inhibitory to deep cerebellar nuclei.
Purkinje cells are the flask-shaped cells present in the Purkinje cell layer of the cerebellum. They
are evenly spaced and arranged in a single layer between molecular and granular cell layers.
The dendrite from each cell passes upward to enter the molecular layer and the axon of each cell
passes downwards, crosses the granular cell layer, and terminates on the deep cerebellar
nuclei. They are inhibitory and use GABA as their neurotransmitter.
Axons of Purkinje cells are the only efferents of the cerebellar cortex. They receive excitatory input
from the climbing fibers from the inferior olivary nucleus and mossy fibers.
Pathological significance of Purkinje cells:
• Negri bodies in rabies are usually found as eosinophilic, cytoplasmic inclusions in the neurons of
the hippocampus and Purkinje cells of the cerebellum.
• In paraneoplastic cerebellar degeneration, there is the destruction of Purkinje cells due to
antibodies directed against them.
• Purkinje cells and neurons in pyramidal cell layers of the hippocampus and cerebral cortex are
the most sensitive neurons to hypoxia and are likely to be damaged in cases of global cerebral
ischemia.

subject: Anatomy
question:
The marked structure develops from which of the following structures?
184

[Anatomy-INI-CET-2021-16-image]
explanation:
The structure marked in the given image is the azygos vein, which develops from the
supracardinal vein.
The azygos system of veins forms a connecting channel between the superior and inferior venae
cavae. It occupies the upper part of the posterior abdominal wall and the posterior mediastinum.
It also connects the portal, caval system, and vertebral venous systems.It drains the thoracic wall
and the upper lumbar region.
Development: The azygos venous system arises from the supra cardinal veins embryologically.
The right supra cardinal vein becomes the azygos vein, while the left supra cardinal vein becomes
209
the hemiazygos vein.
• The 4th to 11th right intercostal veins empty into the right supracardinal vein, which together
with a portion of the posterior cardinal vein forms the azygos vein.
• On the left, the 4th to 7th intercostal veins enter into the left supracardinal vein, and the left
supracardinal vein, then known as the hemiazygos vein, which also empties into the azygos vein.
Other options: Cardinal veins form the main venous drainage system of the embryo. It consists of,
• Anterior cardinal veins, which drain the cephalic part of the embryo, and
• Posterior cardinal veins, which drain the rest of the embryo.
During the fifth to the seventh weeks, a number of additional veins are formed. It includes,
• Subcardinal veins, which mainly drain the kidneys;
• Sacrocardinal veins, which drain the lower extremities;
• Supracardinal veins, which drain the body wall by way of the intercostal veins.
Below image shows cardinal veins development.

[Anatomy-INI-CET-2021-16-exp-image]

subject: Anatomy
question: Inferior thyroid artery supplies which of the following structures?
explanation:
All the given 4 structures are supplied by the Inferior thyroid artery.
Inferior thyroid artery is a branch of the thyrocervical trunk, which in turn, is a branch of the first
part of the subclavian artery. The inferior thyroid artery loops upwards anterior to the medial
border of the scalenus anterior, turns medially just below the sixth cervical transverse process,
and then descends on longus colli to the lower border of the thyroid gland.
Branches:
• Pharyngeal branches: These supply the lower part of the pharynx.
• Tracheal branches supply the trachea and anastomose with the bronchial arteries;
210
• Oesophageal branches supply the oesophagus and anastomose with the oesophageal branches of
the thoracic aorta; and
• Inferior and ascending glandular branches supply the posterior and inferior regions of the
thyroid gland, and anastomose with the contralateral inferior and ipsilateral superior thyroid
arteries.
• The ascending branch also supplies the parathyroid glands.
• Ascending cervical artery
• Inferior laryngeal
• Muscular branches
Note: Thymic branches originating from the internal thoracic, inferior thyroid artery, and
sometimes superior thyroid arteries supply the thymus and the surrounding mediastinal
connective tissue.

subject: Anatomy
question:
Which part of this bone is palpable in the infraclavicular fossa?
185

[Anatomy-INI-CET-2021-18-image]
explanation:
The part of the scapula marked as 1 is the coracoid process and it is palpable in the infraclavicular
fossa. The tip of the coracoid process lies approximately 2.5 cm below the clavicle, immediately
lateral to the infraclavicular fossa (deltopectoral triangle). Hence, the coracoid process can be
palpated below the clavicle.
The coracoid process arises from the upper border of the neck of the scapula and it projects
forwards and slightly laterally. Attachments at the coracoid process include
• The conoid portion of the coracoclavicular ligament
• Pectoralis minor
• Medial end of coraco-acromial ligament
• Coracohumeral ligament
• Glenocorocoid ligament
• Coracobrachialis muscle
Other options
Option B - Acromian process continues from the spine of the scapula. The acromion is
subcutaneous over its dorsal surface, The attachments at the acromion are the coracoacromial
ligament, the articular capsule of the acromioclavicular joint, and horizontal fibers of the
trapezius.
Option C - The glenoid fossa is present at the lateral angle of the scapula. It is articular with the
humerus to form the shoulder joint.
Option D - The inferior angle lies over the seventh rib. Dorsally, it is related to latissimus dorsi.
The angle is visible when the arm is raised over the head.
Parts of the scapula are given in the image below.
211

[Anatomy-INI-CET-2021-18-exp-image]

subject: Anatomy
question:
Identify the pelvic diaphragm in the picture given below.

[Anatomy-INI-CET-2021-19-image]
explanation:
The structure marked "1" is the levator ani muscle which is part of the pelvic diaphragm.
Other structures marked in the above image are
2: External anal sphincter
3: Internal anal sphincter
4: Pudendal canal
Pelvic diaphragm
The muscular part of the pelvic floor is the pelvic diaphragm. It is attached to the pelvic wall on
either side between the greater and lesser sciatic foramen. Levator ani and coccygeus muscles
form the pelvic diaphragm and delineate the lower limit of the true pelvis.
Levator ani is formed by pubococcygeus, iliococcygeus, and puborectalis. The muscles blend
together posterior to the vagina in women and around the anus in both sexes. Posterior to the
anal aperture, the form the anococcygeal body attaches to the coccyx. Urogenital hiatus is present
anteriorly separating the muscles. This allows the urethra and vagina to pass through. Levator ani
reinforces the external anal sphincter. In women, it functions as the vaginal sphincter.
The coccygeus muscles, one on each side are triangular in shape and form the posterior part of the
pelvic diaphragm. The apices are attached to the tips of ischial spines. The bases are attached to
the coccyx and sacrum.

subject: Anatomy
question:
Identify the incorrect match.

[Anatomy-INI-CET-2021-20-image]
explanation:
Among the given options, Option B represents an incorrect match, as the structure labeled as 4 is
showing the basement membrane and not lamina propria.
212
The given image is of pseudostratified ciliated columnar epithelium, as it shows irregular cells,
which are attached to the basement membrane(marked as 4 in the image). It appears to be
multi-layered. However, there is actually only one layer of cells. The multi-layered appearance is
due to the fact that the nuclei of epithelial cell(marked as 3 in the image) lie at different levels.
The epithelium shows hair-like projections called cilia (marked as 1 in the image) and goblet cells
(marked as 2 in the image). Goblet cells secrete mucous which helps to trap dust entering the
passages. The cilia are capable of movement and help in the clearance of mucous.
Example include epithelium lining the trachea and in large bronchi.

[Anatomy-INI-CET-2021-20-exp-image]

subject: Anatomy
question:
Third part of vertebral artery is related to which of the following ?
187

[Anatomy-INI-CET-2021-21-image]
explanation:
The third part of the vertebral artery (V3) is related to the area marked 2, which is the posterior
arch of the first cervical vertebra or the atlas.
The given image shows the first cervical vertebra - atlas.
• The area marked 1 is the posterior tubercle.
• The area marked 2 is the posterior arch. The superior surface bears a groove for the of vertebral
artery and venous plexus immediately behind.
• The area marked 3 is the superior articular facet.
• The area marked 4 is the posterior surface of the anterior arch. It gives attachment to the apex of
dens from the second cervical vertebra.
The vertebral artery arises from the first part of the subclavian artery. It passes through the
foramina of transverse processes of C6 to C1 cervical vertebrae and enters the cranium through
the foramen magnum. The vertebral arteries of both sides join to form the basilar artery. In its
course, it is divided into four parts:
The first part, V1, begins at its origin from the subclavian artery and ends at its entry into the
transverse process of the C6 cervical vertebra. This part is crossed by the inferior thyroid artery on
213
both sides, and thoracic duct on the left side, and the right lymphatic duct on the right side.
The second part, V2 is that part of the artery ascending through the foramina in the transverse
process of the cervical vertebrae.
The third part, V3 is curved backward behind the atlas. In this position, it lies in a groove on the
posterior arch of the atlas. This part of the artery lies in the suboccipital triangle.
The fourth part, V4 pierces the dura and arachnoid mater and unites with the contralateral
vertebral artery to form the midline basilar artery at the lower border of pons.

[Anatomy-INI-CET-2021-21-exp-image]

subject: Anatomy
question:
What is the most common complication of the central line insertion below the marked bone?

[Anatomy-INI-CET-2021-22-image]
explanation:
The bone marked in the above image is the left clavicle. The most common complication of a
central line insertion below this bone is an arterial puncture.
Complications of central vein catheterization are
• Arterial puncture- this is the most common complication.
• Cardiac tamponade is the most important life-threatening complication. It occurs as a result of
puncture of intrapericardial superior vena cava, right atrium, or right ventricle.
• Pneumothorax- injury to the underlying pleura may lead to pneumothorax. This is more
common in subclavian vein catheterization.
• Chylothorax can occur particularly in left subclavian vein catheterisation. But it is not the most
common.
• Injury to the associated nerves like brachial plexus, stellate ganglion, phrenic nerve, or vocal
cords.
• Thromboembolism leading to venous thrombosis, pulmonary embolism is more common with
femoral veins.
• Infections
214
Central venous catheterization is the insertion of the catheter into a large central vein. The veins
preferred largely depend on the underlying disease in the patient, the clinical setting, and the
expertise of the doctor.
The internal jugular vein is preferred when the patient has:
• Bleeding diathesis- Bleeding from this vein can be easily detected and can be controlled with
pressure.
• Pneumothorax- The lesser risk of injury to the lung
• Needs cardiac pacing- right internal jugular vein is used.
Femoral or subclavian veins can be used in patients when the neck is immobilized using a cervical
collar.
The below image shows central vein catheter

[Anatomy-INI-CET-2021-22-exp-image]
The below image shows subclavian vein catheterization.

[Anatomy-INI-CET-2021-22-exp-image]
215
The below image shows catheterisation into jugular vein

[Anatomy-INI-CET-2021-22-exp-image]
Below image shows radiograph of subclavian vein catherisation

[Anatomy-INI-CET-2021-22-exp-image]

subject: Anatomy
question:
Identify the cranial nerve passing through the given structure

[Anatomy-INI-CET-2021-23-image]
explanation:
In the given image, the dorsal aspect of the brainstem is marked and the cranial nerve that is
passing there is the trochlear nerve (CN IV).
The trochlear nerve is the fourth cranial nerve that originates from the trochlear nuclei within the
midbrain at the level of the inferior colliculus. It is the only cranial nerve that emerges from the
dorsal surface of the brainstem and has the longest intracranial course. It passes on to the lateral
surface of the crus of cerebellar peduncle and lateral wall of the cavernous sinus. The nerve then
crosses the oculomotor nerve to enter the orbit through the superior orbital fissure to exclusively
216
supply the contralateral superior oblique muscle.
The main actions of superior oblique are
• Depression in adducted position- The muscle elevates the posterior of the eye causing the front
of the eye to be depressed. Patients with trochlear nerve palsy have difficulty in looking
downwards when eyes are in the adducted position.
• Abduction
• Intortion of the eyeball.
Other options- Oculomotor (CN III), trigeminal (CN V) and abducens (CN VI) nerves exit ventrally
from the brainstem.

[Anatomy-INI-CET-2021-23-exp-image]
217

subject: Anatomy
question:
A patient presented with vision loss. On radiological investigation, an aneurysm causing
damage to the optic chiasma was noted. Which of the following arteries is most likely to be
the artery that is causing the damage?
explanation:
An aneurysm of the anterior communicating artery is most likely to be causing damage to the
optic chiasma, leading to vision loss in this patient.
Circle of Willis is a large arterial anastomosis that unites the internal carotid and vertebrobasilar
systems. It lies in the subarachnoid space within the basal cisterns that surround the optic
chiasma and infundibulum.
Anterior communicating artery aneurysm:
Anterior communicating artery (Acom) links the right and left anterior cerebral arteries and is
directed anterior and superior to the optic chiasma. The aneurysm will protrude in the posterior
direction and will impinge upon the optic chiasma from above, leading to loss of vision.
Compression of the optic chiasma (central lesion) by an ACom aneurysm, will lead to bitemporal
hemianopia i.e, loss of vision in the left/temporal halves of both visual fields. Nasal fibers of each
retina cross through the chiasma at this level and hence, these fibers will be compromised.
In the case of a bilateral peripheral lesion of the optic chiasma, binasal hemianopia will be seen.
Other causes of chiasmal lesions are
• Pituitary adenoma (most common)
• Craniopharyngioma
• Meningioma
• Glioma
Blood supply of optic chiasma: The left and right anterior cerebral arteries, along with the
superior hypophyseal artery (branch of the internal carotid artery) form the blood supply of optic
221
chiasma.

[Anatomy-NEET-2021-1-exp-image]
The given cadaveric image below is of the vascular structures (Circle of Willis) at the base of the
brain.

[Anatomy-NEET-2021-1-exp-image]
Investigation: Digital subtraction angiogram is the definitive imaging modality for aneurysms
arising from the circle of Willis. Other investigations are CT and MRI.
Other options:
Aneurysms of the anterior choroidal artery (option B) and middle cerebral artery (option
C) aneurysms will not compress the optic chiasma as they are anatomically not in close proximity
to be able to exert pressure on the chiasma and cause loss of vision.
Despite the anatomical proximity of the anterior cerebral artery (option D) to the optic chiasma,
anterior communicating artery (Acom) aneurysm is the commonest intracranial aneurysm.
222

subject: Anatomy
question:
A 34-year-old man presented with fever and massive splenomegaly. On palpation, the spleen
was 15 cm and directed obliquely when measured from the right iliac fossa. Which of the
following structures prevent the vertical and downward descent of the spleen?
explanation:
The vertical and downward descent of the spleen is prevented by the phrenicocolic ligament.
The phrenicocolic ligament is not attached to the spleen. It attaches the splenic flexure of the
colon to the diaphragm.
When the spleen gets enlarged, the phrenicocolic ligament along with the splenic flexure of the
colon restricts its downward displacement. The enlargement of the spleen occurs towards the
right iliac fossa and hence the spleen is directed obliquely.
Splenic ligaments and their attachments:
Splenic ligament

ure of the colon
-
Phrenicosplenic ligament

hragm to the spleen
-
Splenorenal (lienorenal) liga
ment
Hilum of the spleen to the an
terior surface of the left kidn
ey

y and vein
Gastrosplenic ligament
The greater curvature of the s
tomach to the upper pole of t
he spleen
Short gastric vesselsLeft gast
roepiploic artery

[Anatomy-NEET-2021-2-exp-image]
Cross-sectional view of the spleen and its ligaments
223

[Anatomy-NEET-2021-2-exp-image]
The spleen becomes palpable when it is enlarged 2-3 times more than its normal size. A
characteristic splenic notch can be felt in the medial border in an enlarged spleen. Also, fingers
cannot be insinuated between the left costal margin and the enlarged spleen.
In the classical method of palpation, the right hand (with fingers pointed towards the left costal
margin) is placed on the right iliac fossa. Palpation begins from the right iliac fossa towards the
left hypochondrium in an upwards and lateral direction. Between each breath of the patient, the
edge of the spleen can be felt.
In the hooking method, the hooked fingers of the right hand are placed under the left costal
margin, and with the left hand, pressure is exerted over the posterolateral aspect of the lower
thorax. Spleen is felt at the deep inspiratory phase.

subject: Anatomy
question:
Identify the papillae from the given histological section.
218

[Anatomy-NEET-2021-3-image]
explanation:
The given histological image is a section of the tongue showing circumvallate papillae, the largest
papillae of the tongue.
Circumvallate papillae are large cylindrical structures that are around 8-12 in number, covered by
non-keratinized stratified squamous epithelium.
They are arranged in a V-shaped row immediately in front of the sulcus terminalis (not on the
sides of the tongue) on the dorsal surface of the tongue. It is narrower at its base than its apex.
Numerous taste buds (around 250 taste buds for each of the 8-12 papillae) are present within the
papillary walls and they contain chemosensory cells that synapse with the gustatory nerves.
Serous glands of von Ebner empty via the ducts into the base of the sulcus.
Identification: They can be identified by their characteristic dome-shaped structure lined by
stratified squamous epithelium.
Other options:
224
Option A - Fungiform papillae are large, rounded, and red in color and are mainly present on the
lingual margins and also on the dorsal surface of the tongue. They are covered by non-keratinized
stratified squamous epithelium. It is supplied by the lingual nerve.
Option C- Filiform papillae are minute cylindrical or conical projections that are mainly present
on the dorsal part of the anterior 2/3rd of the tongue. All except filiform papillae contain taste
buds. They are covered by keratinized stratified squamous epithelium. It is supplied by the lingual
nerve.
Option D - Foliate papillae are red, leaf-like mucosal ridges that are present bilaterally in two
zones at the sides of the tongue near the sulcus terminalis. They are covered by non-keratinized
stratified squamous epithelium.

[Anatomy-NEET-2021-3-exp-image]

subject: Anatomy
question:
A 7-year-old boy was brought to the hospital with multiple fractures of the humerus
secondary to a fall from height. On examination, there is difficulty in flexion of the elbow and
supination of the forearm, and associated loss of sensation over the lateral aspect of the
forearm. Which is the nerve most likely to be injured?
explanation:
The given clinical vignette in a patient with difficulty in flexion of the elbow and supination of the
forearm, with associated loss of sensation over the lateral aspect of the forearm, is suggestive of
injury to the musculocutaneous nerve.
Fall from a height on an outstretched hand is one of the main causes of humerus fractures.
Course:
The musculocutaneous nerve (C5, C6, C7) is a branch of the lateral cord of the brachial plexus. It
emerges at the lower border of the pectoralis minor and pierces coracobrachialis. It then descends
between biceps brachii and brachialis where it supplies a branch to the shaft of the humerus. Then
it enters the elbow and continues as the lateral cutaneous nerve of the forearm.
It innervates all the muscles of the anterior compartment of the arm i.e.,
• Biceps brachii - involved in flexion of elbow and supination of the forearm
• Brachialis -  involved in the flexion of the elbow
• Coracobrachialis - flexes the arm at the glenohumeral joint
225
Cutaneous innervation of the lateral aspect of the forearm is by a branch of the musculocutaneous
nerve, namely, the lateral cutaneous nerve of the forearm (C5, C6).
Hence, injury to the musculocutaneous nerve results in weakness of supination of forearm (biceps
brachii), weakness in flexion of the elbow (biceps brachii, brachialis), and loss of sensation over
the lateral aspect of the forearm (lateral cutaneous nerve of the forearm).
Muscles of the anterior compartment of the arm:
The below image shows the anterior compartment of the arm
Muscles

Insertion
Innervation
Coracobrachia
lis
Coracoid Process of scapula
The medial side of the midsh
aft of the humerus

Brachialis

of the humerus

l nerve
Biceps Brachii
Short head: Coracoid process
Long head: Supraglenoid tub
ercle

Musculocutaneous nerve

[Anatomy-NEET-2021-4-exp-image]
Other options:
Option A:
Median nerve injury is characterized by sensory loss over the radial 3 1/2 fingers and loss of
thumb abduction. In high lesions, the long flexors of the thumb, index, and middle fingers, the
radial wrist flexors, and the forearm pronator muscles are also paralyzed. Wasting of the thenar
eminence is seen in long-standing cases.
Option B:
Radial nerve injury can present with wrist drop, thumb drop, and finger drop. Extension of the
elbow will be spared in lesions below the elbow. The extension of the IP joints is intact due to the
action of the intrinsic muscles of the hand. Loss of sensation over the first dorsal web space is
observed.
226
Option D:
Ulnar nerve injury is characterized by ulnar claw hand, wasting of the hypothenar muscles and
intrinsic muscles of the hand, and sensory loss over palmar & dorsal aspect of the medial
third of the hand and medial 1 1/2 fingers.

[Anatomy-NEET-2021-4-exp-image]
