subject: Anatomy
question:
A patient presented with jaw swelling and weight loss. Suspecting a malignancy, the
submandibular gland was resected. Which is the most likely nerve to be damaged during
submandibular gland resection?
explanation:
The most likely nerve to be damaged during submandibular gland resection is the lingual nerve.
It loops around the submandibular duct and hence is susceptible to injury during submandibular
gland resection.
Course:
227
The lingual nerve arises from the posterior trunk of the mandibular nerve (V3 branch of the
trigeminal nerve). The lingual nerve descends between the tensor veli palatini muscle and the
lateral pterygoid muscle, where it is joined by the chorda tympani nerve. It then descends across
the medial pterygoid muscle to enter the oral cavity. As the lingual nerve enters the floor of the
oral cavity, it lies on the deep portion of the submandibular gland. It then passes into the tongue
on the hyoglossus muscle where it is attached to the submandibular ganglion.
The submandibular duct lies between the lingual and hypoglossal nerves, superficial to the
hyoglossus muscle. As the duct reaches the anterior border of the hyoglossus, it is crossed by the
lingual nerve and lies in close proximity to the nerve. Hence lingual nerve injury is the most
common.
Injury:
The lingual nerve is at risk to be injured during submandibular gland resection because dissection
of the submandibular duct from the nerve is required. Also, it is connected to the submandibular
ganglion which will lead to the nerve being exposed within the operating field.
The manifestations of lingual nerve injury depend on whether the lesion is proximal or distal to
where it is joined by the chorda tympani nerve.
• Proximal injury: There is loss of sensation from the anterior two-thirds of the tongue, gingivae,
oral mucosa, lower lip, and chin.
• Distal injury: In addition to features of proximal injury, secretion from the salivary glands below
the oral fissure and taste from the anterior two-thirds of the tongue is also lost.
Note:
The cranial nerves that are at risk during submandibular gland resection include:
• The marginal mandibular branch of the facial nerve
• Lingual nerve
• Hypoglossal nerve

[Anatomy-NEET-2021-5-exp-image]
228

[Anatomy-NEET-2021-5-exp-image]

[Anatomy-NEET-2021-5-exp-image]
Other options:
Option A: Nerve to mylohyoid is a branch of the inferior alveolar nerve and lies in the mylohyoid
groove inferior to the mandibular foramen, hence the risk of injury is less.
Option B: Inferior alveolar nerve enters the mandibular canal via the mandibular foramen, hence
is unlikely to be injured during submandibular gland excision.
Option D: Hypoglossal nerve injury is less common than lingual nerve injury as it only lies below
and parallels to the submandibular duct.

subject: Anatomy
question:
A patient underwent a coronary bypass graft with the great saphenous vein. The patient now
complains of paresthesia and sensory loss over the medial aspect of the leg and foot. Which of
the following nerves is most likely to be involved?
explanation:
The given clinical vignette in a patient with paresthesia and sensory loss over the medial aspect of
the leg and foot is suggestive of injury to the saphenous nerve.
229
Injury to the saphenous nerve is common following venous cut down for access to the great
saphenous vein, in this case for coronary bypass graft.
Saphenous nerve:
It is the longest and largest cutaneous branch of the femoral nerve and is the longest nerve in the
body. It arises from the posterior division of the femoral nerve. It then enters the adductor canal
and exits it by piercing the fascia lata between the gracilis and sartorius and supplies the skin over
the patella. The saphenous nerve then descends along the medial border of the tibia along with
the great saphenous vein (anterior to the vein) and supplies the skin on the medial side of
the knee, leg, and foot as far as up to the first metatarsophalangeal joint.
Saphenous nerve injury:
When the great saphenous vein is being harvested for coronary bypass graft, injury to the
saphenous nerve is common as it closely travels with the great saphenous vein. Sensory loss over
the medial side of the knee, leg, and foot is observed in saphenous nerve injury.

[Anatomy-NEET-2021-6-exp-image]

[Anatomy-NEET-2021-6-exp-image]
230
Other options:
Option A: Superficial peroneal nerve injury causes sensory loss over the lateral aspect of the leg
and the dorsum of the foot.
Option C: Femoral nerve gives rise to anterior cutaneous branches, the saphenous nerve, and
motor supply to the quadriceps and sartorius. Anterior cutaneous branches supply the skin on the
anterior thigh and knee. If the saphenous nerve was not an option in the question, the femoral
nerve would have been the next best answer.
Option D: Sural nerve injury causes sensory loss over the posterior and lateral aspects of the distal
third of the leg and lateral side of the foot and fifth toe. It is often used as an autologous
peripheral nerve graft as it is readily identified and easy to harvest.

subject: Anatomy
question:
During the clinical examination of the nervous system, which of the following nerves can be
tested by touching the highlighted structure?

[Anatomy-NEET-2021-7-image]
explanation:
The structure marked in the given sagittal section of the head and neck is the uvula. The sensory
supply to the uvula is by the glossopharyngeal nerve and it is tested in this case.
Nerve supply by glossopharyngeal nerve:
• Sensory fibers to the uvula, tympanic cavity, pharyngotympanic tube, tonsils,
fauces, nasopharynx, and posterior third of the tongue.
• Motor fibers to the stylopharyngeus muscle
• Parasympathetic secretomotor fibers to the parotid gland
• Gustatory fibers for the postsulcal part of the tongue.
Pharyngeal plexus:
The nerve supply of the pharynx is mainly from the pharyngeal plexus of nerves. It is formed by
the pharyngeal branches of the glossopharyngeal nerve and vagus nerve. It also has contributions
from the superior cervical ganglion. The vagus nerve provides the motor supply and the
glossopharyngeal nerve provides the sensory supply.
Nerve supply by the pharyngeal branch of the vagus nerve:
• All the muscles of the soft palate(except tensor veli palatini - supplied by mandibular division of
the trigeminal nerve)
• All the muscles of the pharynx(except stylopharyngeus - supplied by the glossopharyngeal nerve)
Note:
Previously cranial root of the spinal accessory nerve was thought to contribute to the motor
functions of the pharyngeal plexus, but recent evidence suggests that it is not a major part of the
motor drive to the pharyngeal plexus.
Innervation of the soft palate:
General sensation - It is mostly carried by the lesser palatine nerve which is a branch of maxillary
nerve. The sensation from posterior part is carried by the pharyngeal branches of the
glossopharyngeal nerve and from the plexus around the tonsil which is formed by the tonsillar
branches of the glossopharyngeal and lesser palatine nerves.
231
Taste sensation - There are taste buds in the oral surface of soft palate and sensation from them is
carried by the lesser palatine nerve.
Secretomotor supply - It is by the lesser palatine nerve via postganglionic fibres from the
pterygopalatine ganglion.
Motor supply - It is by the pharyngeal branch of the vagus nerve except for tensor veli palatini
which is supplied by mandibular division of the trigeminal nerve.
232

subject: Anatomy
question: Arrange in sequence the structures involved in the direct pathway:
explanation:
The sequence of structures involved in the direct pathway is cortex > striatum > globus
pallidus > substantia nigra > thalamus.
There are five parallel circuits that connect the frontal cortex to the striatum, pallidum, substantia
nigra, and thalamus. These circuits have both direct and indirect pathways. The direct pathway
stimulates the cortex to produce movement, whereas the indirect pathway inhibit it.
The cerebral cortex projects into the striatum. The striatum is seen projected into the pallidum
and substantia nigra pars reticularis. Fibres originate from different striatal cells according to
their endings in the external and internal pallidum. In the direct pathway, striatal
neurones project directly to the internal pallidum.  Whereas some other fibres that arise from the
neurons project to the external pallidum. This is called the indirect pathway.
The sequence of structures involved in the indirect pathway is cortex > striatum > globus
pallidus externa > subthalamic nucleus > globus pallidus interna and substantia nigra
>Thalamus
The striatal activity in the direct pathway is mediated by D1 dopamine receptors and in the
indirect pathway by D2 dopamine receptors.
Putamen lies lateral and the internal capsule lies medial to the pallidum. It receives fibres from
the striatum and subthalamic nuclei. It acts as the main output of the basal ganglia to the
thalamus and superior colliculus. The substantia nigra is seen in each cerebral peduncle of the
midbrain located deep to the crus cerebri. The levels of dopamine in the substantia nigra and
striata are seen reduced in parkinsonism disease.
249

[Anatomy-INI-CET-2022-1-exp-image]

[Anatomy-INI-CET-2022-1-exp-image]
In parkinsonism disease, due to the death of neurons in substantia nigra activity of the direct
pathway is reduced and the indirect pathway is increased. This results in bradykinesia in
parkinsonism disease. Whereas in Huntington's disease, the activity of the indirect pathway is
affected resulting in choreic involuntary movements.
250

[Anatomy-INI-CET-2022-1-exp-image]

subject: Anatomy
question: What is the volume of the prostate in normal adult males?
explanation:
The volume of the prostate in normal adult males is 20cc.
Prostate is an accessory glandular tissue embedded in the fibromuscular stroma. It usually weighs
8 grams in the young adults and its weight ranges from 40-150 grams in men aged >50 years.
The prostate gland is situated in the lesser pelvis and surrounds the prostatic urethra which
extends from bladder base to the membranous urethra. Pubic symphysis and pubic arch lie
anteriorly to the prostate whereas rectourethralis and rectal ampulla lie posteriorly to it.
The prostate has three distinct zones as follows:
Zone
Structures enclosed
Clinical significance
Transitional zon
e
Urethra
Most common site for benign
 prostatic hyperplasia (BPH)
Central zone
Ejaculatory ducts
Atrophies secondary to BPH
Peripheral zone
Central and transitional zone
s Pre-prostatic urethra
Most common site for prosta
tic carcinoma
251

[Anatomy-INI-CET-2022-2-exp-image]
The prostate is composed of 5 lobes. They are anterior, posterior, two lateral and one medial or
middle lobe.
The median lobe of the prostate forms the uvula vesicae. It is an elevation found in the lower part
of the bladder trigone. It is the most common site of adenoma.

[Anatomy-INI-CET-2022-2-exp-image]
Microstructure of the prostate gland:
The glandular tissue of the prostate gland is composed of many follicles with frequent internal
papillae. The follicular epithelium is either a single-layered or pseudostratified columnar
epithelum. These follicles open into canals, which join to form the main ducts. Small eosinophilic
colloid amyloid bodies called corpora amylacea are frequently seen within a follicle.
The prostatic ducts open into the prostatic sinus in the prostatic urethra. The ducts of the prostate
follicles are lined by bilayered epithelium (basal cuboidal and apical columnar).
The secretions of the prostate are acidic and contain acid phosphatase, amylase, prostate-specific
antigen (PSA), fibrin, and zinc.
252
The blood supply to the prostate is by the inferior vesical, internal pudendal and middle rectal
arteries. It recieves nerve supply from the inferior hypogastric plexus.

subject: Anatomy
question: Which ligament connects medial cuneiform to the base of the 2nd metatarsal?
explanation:
The Lisfranc ligament connects the medial cuneiform to the base of the 2nd metatarsal.
Lisfranc ligament is a cuneometatarsal interosseous ligament. It is the strongest ligament among
the three cuneometatarsal interosseous ligaments. It passes from the lateral surface of the medial
cuneiform to the adjacent angle of the second metatarsal bone.
Deformity of this ligament can lead to instability and deformity of the tarsometatarsal joint.

[Anatomy-INI-CET-2022-3-exp-image]
Other options:
Option A: Chopart's ligament is related to both calcaneocuboid and calcaneonavicular joints. It is
a strong Y-shaped band, having a proximal stem and 2 distal limbs.
• The proximal limb is attached by a stem to the anterior part of the upper calcaneal surface.
• The distal limb divides into calcaneocuboid and calcaneonavicular parts.
253

[Anatomy-INI-CET-2022-3-exp-image]
Option B: Spring ligament also known as the plantar calcaneonavicular ligament connects
the sustentaculum tali to the navicular. It ties the calcaneus to the navicular and maintains the
medial longitudinal arch of the foot. It is made up of the superomedial calcaneonavicular portion
and the inferior calcaneonavicular portion.

[Anatomy-INI-CET-2022-3-exp-image]
Option D: Deltoid ligament also called the medial collateral ligament is attached to the apex,
anterior and posterior borders of the medial malleolus. It has 2 layers, superficial and deep:
• The superficial layer consists of tibionavicular fibres anteriorly, tibiocalcaneal fibres in the
middle, and superficial posterior tibiotalar fibres posteriorly.
• Deep layer consists of anterior tibiotalar fibres, tibiocalcaneal in the middle and deep posterior
tibiotalar posteriorly.
254

[Anatomy-INI-CET-2022-3-exp-image]

subject: Anatomy
question:
Fibres of the given structure originate from ?
233

[Anatomy-INI-CET-2022-4-image]
explanation:
The given structure is the fornix and its fibres arise from the hippocampus.
The fornix is a part of the connecting pathways of the limbic system. It arises from the
hippocampus which wraps around the thalamus and ends in the mammillary body. It connects
the hippocampus with distal brain sites.
The fornix has two types of fibres:
• Association fibres: They connect the various cortical regions within the same hemisphere
• Commissural fibres: They connect the corresponding regions of two hemispheres. It is seen in
the hippocampal commissure of the fornix.
The blood supply of fornix is by the anterior communicating artery which arises from the anterior
cerebral artery.
The limbic system is a group of structures seen between the cerebral cortex and the
hypothalamus. It is involved in emotional and motivation activity, learning, and memory.
255

[Anatomy-INI-CET-2022-4-exp-image]

[Anatomy-INI-CET-2022-4-exp-image]
Other options:
Option A: Amygdala is a part of the limbic system that consists of lateral, central and basal nuclei.
It can Influence the body's response to enviromnental changes. In a fearful situation it can change
the heart rate, blood pressure, respiratory rate etc.
Option C: Caudate nucleus is a C-shaped mass of grey matter. It is divided into a head, body, and
tail. Degeneration of the caudate nucleus is seen in Huntington's disease.

subject: Anatomy
question:
Which structure is supplied by the nerve causing this elevation?

[Anatomy-INI-CET-2022-5-image]
explanation:
Risorius is supplied by the facial nerve which causes the elevation marked. The image shows the
floor of the fourth ventricle and the facial colliculus is marked.
256
The fourth ventricle is located between the brainstem and the cerebellum. The rhomboid fossa is a
diamond-shaped depression seen on the floor of the fourth ventricle. The superior and the inferior
part of the floor is triangular in shape. It is divided by a longitudinal median sulcus into two
halves. Each half is again divided into the medial and lateral regions.
The facial colliculus is present in the superior part, which presents as a small elevation produced
by the root of the facial nerve winding around the nucleus of the abducens nerve.  Columns are
formed by groups of facial neurones, which innervate individual muscles and give branches of the
facial nerve. Dorsal neurones innervate muscles in the scalp and upper face and ventral neurons
supply the lower facial musculature.
Risorius falls under the buccolabial muscle group and act as the elevator, retractor and evertor of
the upper lip. Some of its peripheral attachments are the zygomatic arch, parotid fascia and fascia
over the mastoid process. It is involved in actions like grinning and laughing and is innervated by
the buccal branches of the facial nerve.

[Anatomy-INI-CET-2022-5-exp-image]

[Anatomy-INI-CET-2022-5-exp-image]
Other options:
257
Option A: Lateral rectus is an extraocular muscle that arises from the lateral part of the common
tendinous ring. It is innervated by the abducens nerve.
Option B: Superior oblique is an extraocular muscle that arises from the body of the sphenoid. It
is innervated by the trochlear nerve.
Option D: Levator palpebrae superiors is an extraocular muscle that arises from the lesser wing of
sphenoid. It is innervated by the oculomotor nerve.

[Anatomy-INI-CET-2022-5-exp-image]

[Anatomy-INI-CET-2022-5-exp-image]
258

[Anatomy-INI-CET-2022-5-exp-image]

[Anatomy-INI-CET-2022-5-exp-image]

subject: Anatomy
question: Arrange lung hilar structures from anterior to posterior:
explanation:
Lung hilar structures are arranged from the anterior to posterior in the order:
• Pulmonary vein
• Pulmonary artery
• Primary bronchus
• Bronchial artery
The medial surface of the lung is connected to the heart and trachea by a group of structures that
enter or leave the hilum. It is called the pulmonary root. It is anteriorly related to the phrenic
nerve, pericardiacophrenic vessels , and anterior pulmonary plexus. The vagus nerve and
posterior pulmonary plexus form the posterior relation and the pulmonary ligament the inferior
259
relation.
The superior vena cava and right atrium are seen in front of the right root and terminal part of the
azygos vein superiorly. The hilar structures are arranged from the superior to inferior as:
• Superior lobar bronchus
• Pulmonary artery
• Principal bronchus
• Inferior pulmonary vein

[Anatomy-INI-CET-2022-6-exp-image]
The aortic arch is seen superiorly and descending thoracic aorta posteriorly of the left root. The
hilar structures are vertically arranged as:
• Pulmonary artery
• Principal bronchus
• Inferior pulmonary vein

[Anatomy-INI-CET-2022-6-exp-image]
260

subject: Anatomy
question: Which is not a branch of facial artery in the face?
explanation:
Mental is not a branch of the facial artery but the branch of the maxillary artery in the face.
The first part of the maxillary artery gives rise to the mental artery as a terminal branch of the
inferior alveolar artery.
The facial artery is a branch of the external carotid artery which arises in the carotid triangle,
above the origin of the lingual artery. It runs upwards in the neck and curves around the
anteroinferior border of the masseter to enter the face.
The premasseteric artery passes along the anterior border of the masseter and the surrounding
tissues are supplied by it. The inferior labial artery mainly supplies the inferior labial glands. It
then anastomoses with its controlateral fellow and with the mental branch of the inferior alveolar
artery. The superior labial artery supplies the upper lip whereas the lateral nasal artery supplies
the dorsum and alae of the nose. Both these arteries anastomose with its controlateral fellow.
The image shows the branches of facial artery:
Branches of facial artery in th
e neck
Branches of facial artery in th
e face
Ascending palatine artery
Premasseteric artery
Tonsillar branch
Superior and inferior labial a
rteries
Submental artery
Lateral nasal artery
Branches to submandibular g
land
Angular artery

[Anatomy-INI-CET-2022-7-exp-image]
261

subject: Anatomy
question: Which of the following structures passing via jugular foramen has a separate canal?
explanation:
The glossopharyngeal nerve passing through the jugular foramen has a separate canal.
The jugular foramen is formed by the occipital bone and the petrous part of the temporal bone.
The spinal accessory nerve and vagus nerve also pass through this foramen.
The glossopharyngeal nerve leaves the skull through the anteromedial part of the jugular foramen
in a separate dural sheath.
The branches of the nerve are tympanic, carotid, pharyngeal, muscular, tonsillar and lingual
branches.
Infection, malignancy and postoperative inflammation of the tonsil and tonsillar fossa will cause
referred pain in the ear. This is conducted through the tympanic branch of the glossopharyngeal
nerve.
Pharyngeal branches of the glossopharyngeal nerve and vagus nerve form the pharyngeal plexus
of nerves. These plexes mainly supply the pharynx. It also has contributions from the superior
cervical ganglion.
Nerve supply by the pharyngeal branch of the vagus nerve are:
• All the muscles of the soft palate(except tensor veli palatini - supplied by mandibular division of
the trigeminal nerve)
• All the muscles of the pharynx(except stylopharyngeus - supplied by the glossopharyngeal nerve)
Motor fibres to the stylopharyngeus and parasympathetic secretomotor fibres to the parotid
gland are also supplied by the glossopharyngeal nerve.
Sensory supply includes uvula, posterior one-third of the tongue, tympanic cavity etc.
The glossopharyngeal nerve is rarely damaged alone. Lesions will result in loss of sensation of the
posterior third of the tongue, reduced gag reflex, loss of sensation over the ipsilateral soft palate
etc.
The main functions of IX and X can be tested together by assessing swallowing,
phonation/articulation and sensation from the pharynx/larynx.
Glossopharyngeal and vagus nerve examinations include:
• Observing the position and symmetry of the palate and uvula at rest and with phonation (“aah”).
• By assessing the strength of the cough
The image below shows the base of the skull and foramina:
262

[Anatomy-INI-CET-2022-8-exp-image]

[Anatomy-INI-CET-2022-8-exp-image]

subject: Anatomy
question:
What is the nerve supply of the structure marked in the image?
235

[Anatomy-INI-CET-2022-9-image]
explanation:
The median nerve supplies the 1st lumbrical which is marked.
There are four lumbrical muscles. They originate from the tendons of the flexor digitorum
profundus. It passes to the radial side of the corresponding fingers and is inserted into the dorsal
digital expansion of the extensor digitorum.
The action of lumbricals is flexion at the metacarpophalangeal joint and extension of
interphalangeal joints.
Lumbricals can be tested only along with interosseous muscle. The examiner will hold the
metacarpophalangeal joint of the index finger in hyperextension. Then the patient is asked to
extend the proximal interphalangeal joint against resistance. The test is repeated on other fingers
as well.
263
The median nerve innervates the first and second lumbricals. The deep terminal branch of the
ulnar nerve innervates the third and the fourth.

[Anatomy-INI-CET-2022-9-exp-image]

[Anatomy-INI-CET-2022-9-exp-image]
The median nerve is formed by the union of the lateral root (C6, 7) from the lateral cord, and the
medial root (C8, T1) from the medial cord.
On the hand, it is seen proximal to the flexor retinaculum and lateral to the tendons of flexor
digitorum superficialis. It also lies between the tendons of flexor carpi radialis and palmaris
longus.
Its cutaneous supply in hand is as follows:
• Lateral 2/3rd of the palm by superficial cutaneous branch
• Lateral 3½ fingers (palmar aspect and dorsal aspect of nailbeds) by palmar digital nerves
264

[Anatomy-INI-CET-2022-9-exp-image]
The muscles supplied by the median nerve in the hand are flexor pollicis brevis, abductor pollicis
brevis, and opponens pollicis
The median nerve sometimes gets compressed under the fibro-osseous tunnel beneath the flexor
retinaculum leading to carpal tunnel syndrome.
Pen test is used to test the median nerve in the hand. It tests the abductor pollicis brevis
muscle.  The hand must remain flat with the palm facing upwards. A pen is held above the palm
and the patient is asked to touch the pen with his thumb.

[Anatomy-INI-CET-2022-9-exp-image]

subject: Anatomy
question:
A child lacks thymus and inferior parathyroid glands. Defective development of which of the
following structures is likely to be the cause?

[Anatomy-INI-CET-2022-10-image]
explanation:
The given clinical scenario is suggestive of DiGeorge syndrome. Defective development of
the third pharyngeal pouch marked as 3 causes this condition.
265
A child with DiGeorge syndrome lacks thymus and inferior parathyroid glands due to defective
development of the 3rd pharyngeal pouch. The child can also lack superior parathyroid glands,
due to defective development of the 4th pharyngeal pouch. It is caused by chromosome 22q
deletion and is characterized by the following (Mnemonic: CATCH-22)
• Cleft palate
• Abnormal facies
• Thymic aplasia leading to T-cell deficiency and recurrent infections
• Cardiac defects
• Hypocalcemia, secondary to parathyroid aplasia.
Derivatives of the Pharyngeal Pouches
1st Pouch
• Tympanic cavity (middle ear cavity)
• Auditory tube (eustachian tube)
2nd Pouch
• Palatine tonsils
• Tonsillar fossa
3rd Pouch
• Inferior parathyroid glands
• Thymus
4th Pouch
• Superior parathyroid glands
• Ultimobranchial body - gives rise to parafollicular (C) cells of the thyroid gland

[Anatomy-INI-CET-2022-10-exp-image]
266
The medulla of the thymus has Hassall’s corpuscles. They are small rounded structures formed
from whorls of medullary epithelial cells with a central partially keratinized core appearing as an
eosinophilic-staining mass.
Histological image of Hassall’s corpuscles is given below:

[Anatomy-INI-CET-2022-10-exp-image]

subject: Anatomy
question:
Identify which of the following structure is a merocrine gland:

[Anatomy-INI-CET-2022-11-image]
explanation:
Sweat gland marked 2 is a merocrine gland.
Eccrine and apocrine are the two types of sweat glands. Secretions of the sweat glands are by
exocytosis hence they are also known as merocrine sweat glands.
A histopathological image of the sweat gland is shown below:

[Anatomy-INI-CET-2022-11-exp-image]
267

[Anatomy-INI-CET-2022-11-exp-image]
The other structures marked are:
1- Hair follicle
3- Sebaceous gland
4- Hair shaft

[Anatomy-INI-CET-2022-11-exp-image]
Glands are divided into holocrine, apocrine, and merocrine on the basis of the manner in which
their secretions are poured out of the cells.
In holocrine secretions, the secretory cells themselves are disintegrated as part of their terminal
differentiation. Afterwhich, the terminally differentiated cells filled with lipid products are
released. The sebaceous gland and hair follicles are examples.
A histopathological image of the sebaceous gland is shown below:
268

[Anatomy-INI-CET-2022-11-exp-image]

[Anatomy-INI-CET-2022-11-exp-image]
Merocrine uses exocytosis for secretions. Most of the exocrine glands are merocrine.
In apocrine, product-filled areas of cells are released, the mammary gland is an example.
Histopathological image of mammary gland is shown below:
269

[Anatomy-INI-CET-2022-11-exp-image]

[Anatomy-INI-CET-2022-11-exp-image]

subject: Anatomy
question:
What is the blood supply of the structure marked in the image below?
237

[Anatomy-INI-CET-2022-12-image]

options.a: Anterior cerebral artery and middle cerebral artery
options.b: Anterior cerebral artery and posterior cerebral artery
options.c: Anterior cerebral artery and anterior communicating artery
options.d: Middle cerebral artery and posterior cerebral artery

explanation:
Blood is supplied by the anterior cerebral artery and anterior communicating artery as the
marked structure is the anterior part of the corpus callosum.
Corpus callosum is the largest fibre pathway of the brain. The parts of the corpus callosum are the
rostrum, genu, trunk or body, and splenium.
The image below shows the parts of the corpus callosum:
270

[Anatomy-INI-CET-2022-12-exp-image]
Anterior cerebral artery is a branch of the internal carotid artery. The frontal branch of the
anterior cerebral artery supplies the corpus callosum and the central branch which supplies the
rostrum.
Central or perforating arteries arises from the circle of willis. They are again divided into the
anteromedial, anterolateral, posteromedial and posterolateral groups. The anterior cerebral and
anterior communicating artery give rise to anteromedial group which supplies the rostrum of the
corpus callosum.
The image below shows the mid sagittal section of the brain:

[Anatomy-INI-CET-2022-12-exp-image]

subject: Anatomy
question: Superior gluteal nerve supplies all of the following structures except:
explanation:
271
All of the structures except pyriformis are supplied by the superior gluteal nerve. Pyriformis is
supplied by the nerve to the piriformis.
The dorsal branches of the L4, L5, and S1 rami give rise to the superior gluteal nerve. It is
accompanied by superior gluteal vessels. This nerve leaves the pelvis via the greater sciatic
foramen above piriformis and divides into superior and inferior branches. It supplies gluteus
medius, gluteus minimus, and tensor fasciae latae.
The dorsal branches of the S1 and S2 rami give rise to the nerve to the piriformis.
Gluteus medius and gluteus minimus act as the abductors of the hip joint. Stabilizing the pelvis on
the femur during walking is done by these muscles. It is demonstrated by asking the subject to
stand on one limb. If the ipsilateral abductors are normal, the pelvis remains in a horizontal
position. If the ipsilateral abductors are weak, then the pelvis tilts towards the opposite
(unsupported) side. This is called positive Trendelenburg’s sign.

[Anatomy-INI-CET-2022-13-exp-image]

[Anatomy-INI-CET-2022-13-exp-image]
272

subject: Anatomy
question: Arrange the structures of the cubital fossa in sequence from medial to lateral.
explanation:
The structures are arranged in the cubital fossa from medial to lateral as follows:
• Median nerve
• Brachial artery
• Tendon of biceps brachii
• Radial nerve
The cubital fossa is a triangular depression in the anterior aspect of the upper forearm. It is
related to the epicondylar line proximally, lateral border of pronator teres medially, and by the
medial surface of brachioradialis laterally.
Its roof is formed by the deep fascia of the forearm. The floor of the fossa is formed by the
brachialis and supinator. Veins like basilic, median cubital, and cephalic veins are arranged in the
superficial fascia from medial to lateral side.

[Anatomy-INI-CET-2022-14-exp-image]

subject: Anatomy
question:
Arrange the cells according to their positions from the basal layer towards the lumen in the
seminiferous tubules.
explanation:
From the basal layer towards the lumen in the seminiferous tubules, the cells are arranged in the
order of:
• Myoepithelial cells
• Spermatogonia
• Primary spermatocyte
• Spermatid
• Spermatozoa
Spermatogenesis is the process in which spermatogonia are transformed into spermatozoa. It
begins at puberty. During puberty, the sex cords acquire lumen to become seminiferous tubules.
273
The primordial germ cells give rise to spermatogonial stem cells. The transformation of
spermatogonial stem cells to spermatozoa is given in the flowchart below.

[Anatomy-INI-CET-2022-15-exp-image]
The process by which spermatids are transformed into spermatozoa is called spermiogenesis.

[Anatomy-INI-CET-2022-15-exp-image]

subject: Anatomy
question: Which skull foramen is formed by 3 bones?
explanation:
Foramen lacerum is formed by three bones.
The three bones involved in the formation of foramen lacerum are:
• Petrous part of the temporal bone
• Greater wing of the sphenoid bone
• Basilar part of the occipital bone
274
The internal carotid artery enters the skull through this foramen. It ascends through the carotid
canal and from the upper end of the foramen lacerum.
It is accompanied by venous and sympathetic nerve plexuses. It is also transversed by the
meningeal branches of the ascending pharyngeal artery and emissary veins from the cavernous
sinus.
On the anterior margin of the foramen lacerum pterygoid canal can be seen which leads to the
pterygopalatine fossa. The nerve of the pterygoid canal and accompanying vessels are transmitted
by them.
The image below shows the base of the skull and foraminas:

[Anatomy-INI-CET-2022-16-exp-image]
Other options:
Option B: Foramen magnum is formed by the occipital bone.
Option C: Foramen rotundum is a hole seen in the greater wing of the sphenoid bone.

[Anatomy-INI-CET-2022-16-exp-image]
275
Option D: Foramen jugulare is formed by the petrous part of the temporal bone and occipital
bone.
The image below shows superior and inferior views of the skull base:

[Anatomy-INI-CET-2022-16-exp-image]

subject: Anatomy
question:
Which marked structure causes lateral rotation, flexion, and abduction at the hip joint, along
with flexion at the knee joint?

[Anatomy-INI-CET-2022-17-image]
explanation:
Sartorius is involved in lateral rotation, flexion, and abduction at the hip joint, and flexion at the
knee. The structure marked '2' is sartorius.
The sartorius muscle is known as the tailor muscle and is also the longest muscle in the body. The
word "sartorius" is derived from the Latin word sartor, which translates to tailor, due to the way
the individual will position one's leg while working.
Origin: Anterior superior iliac spine and the upper half of the notch below it.
Insertion: Into the superior aspect of the medial surface of the tibial shaft near the tibial
tuberosity (joins aponeurosis of gracilis and semitendinosus to form pes anserinus).
Action: Flexion, abduction, and lateral rotation of the thigh; and flexion of the leg.
Innervation: By femoral nerve (L2 and L3)
276

[Anatomy-INI-CET-2022-17-exp-image]
Other options:
Option A: Rectus femoris is involved in flexion at the hip and extension at the knee.
Option C: Gracilis is involved in adduction at the hip and flexion and medial rotation at the knee.
Option D: Adductor longus is involved in adduction, flexion, and medial rotation at the hip.

subject: Anatomy
question: Which among the following statements is true regarding the course of the mandibular nerve?
explanation:
The true statements are 2 and 3.
The lingual nerve is a branch from the posterior division of the mandibular nerve, and it arcs
around the submandibular duct.
The buccal branch is a branch from the anterior division of the mandibular nerve, and it emerges
from behind the mandibular ramus.
The other statements:
1. The auriculotemporal nerve emerges from behind the temporomandibular joint and is a branch
from the posterior division of the mandibular nerve.
4. The ophthalmic nerve arises in the middle cranial fossa and is a division of the trigeminal
nerve, along with the maxillary and mandibular nerves.

subject: Anatomy
question: Identify the incorrectly matched pair(s) of pharyngeal arch and its artery.
explanation:
All the given pairs of pharyngeal arteries and their arteries are correctly matched except for 'v'
(option C).
The 6th pharyngeal arch has its blood supply on the left side from the left pulmonary artery and
ductus arteriosus, not from the aorta.
277

subject: Anatomy
question:
The rupture of penile urethra does not cause extravasation of blood into which of the
following spaces?
explanation:
Rupture of the penile urethra causes extravasation of blood into the superficial perineal pouch,
scrotum, penis, and then up to the lower anterior abdominal wall.
The superficial perineal pouch is a compartment of the perineum enclosed by the membranous
layer of the perineal fascia that surrounds the scrotum or labia and penis or clitoris.
Anteriorly, the membranous layer of perineal fascia is continuous over the pubic symphysis and
pubic bones. It is later continuous with the membranous layer of the anterior abdominal wall. 
Thus, the fluid that accumulates in the perineal pouch extends to the lower anterior abdomen.
In the lower lateral abdominal wall, the abdominal fascia is attached to the deep fascia of the
thigh, inferior to the inguinal ligament. Thus, on trauma at the penile urethra, urine does not
extravasate into the anterior thigh.
The superficial perineal pouch is closed posteriorly by attaching the membranous layer of
superficial fascia to the posterior margin of the urogenital diaphragm. Thus, commonly there is no
extravasation into the deep perineal pouch and the ischiorectal fossa.
Also, the membranous layer of superficial fascia attaches to the ischiopubic rami and forms the
lateral borders of the urogenital triangle. Thus, the urine does not extravasate to the thigh.
But, the ischiorectal fossa would be a better answer because of the following reasons:
• Bulbourethral glands that lie in the deep perineal space open their ducts into the spongy urethra,
which is related to the superficial perineal space. Also, the muscles surrounding these glands were
recently found to be a continuation of the deep transverse perinei and tail of the external urethral
sphincter, both of which are components of the deep perineal space. Injury to the penile urethra
can damage bulbourethral glands' ducts, which can lead to damage to the above-mentioned
structures and may cause extravasation of urine into the deep perineal space.
• Previously, the perineal membrane was thought to be a triangular membrane. However, recent
studies show that it is contiguous anteriorly with the structures that support the urethra.
Posteriorly, it is related to the support of the perineal body and lateral vaginal wall. So damage to
the urethra might damage the anterior part of the perineal membrane which can make way for
extravasation of blood into the deep perineal space.
• Deep perineal pouch: It was believed initially that the urogenital diaphragm was formed by 3
layers: An inferior fascia (also called the perineal membrane), deep perineal space, and a superior
fascia. The superior fascia formed the inferior border of the ischioanal fossa. The Colles fascia also
separates the perineal membrane from the ischioanal fossa. So, extravasation of blood into the
deep perineal space is possible but extravasation into the ischioanal fossa due to penile urethral
injury is highly unlikely. Recent studies have shown that this might be a slightly more complex
structure.
278

[Anatomy-INI-CET-2022-20-exp-image]

[Anatomy-INI-CET-2022-20-exp-image]

subject: Anatomy
question:
Which nerve is blocked when local anesthesia is given below the inguinal ligament during
femoral vein catheterization?
240
explanation:
Genitofemoral nerve is blocked when the local anesthetic is given below the inguinal ligament
during femoral vein catheterization.
The genitofemoral nerve originates from the L1 and L2 ventral rami. It divides into the genital and
femoral branches after crossing behind the ureter.
The genital branch enters the inguinal canal through the deep ring and accompanies the
spermatic cord or round ligament. It exits the superficial inguinal ring and supplies the cremaster
muscle and skin of the external genitalia.
The femoral branch descends lateral to the external iliac artery to pass behind the inguinal
ligament and enter the femoral sheath lateral to the femoral artery. It pierces the anterior wall of
the femoral sheath and fascia lata and supplies them.
279
The genitofemoral nerve gives sensory supply to the anteromedial thigh via the femoral branch.
The genital branch supplies skin of the scrotum in males and mons pubis and labium majus in
females. The genitofemoral nerve also innervates the cremaster muscle via the genital branch and
is responsible for the cremasteric reflex.
The image given below shows nerves related to the inguinal region:

[Anatomy-INI-CET-2022-21-exp-image]
Other options:
Option A: Femoral nerve arises from the posterior division of ventral rami of L2-L4 spinal
segments on the posterior abdominal wall. It enters the femoral triangle by passing under the
inguinal ligament. In the femoral triangle, it lies lateral to the femoral artery and outside the
femoral sheath. Later, it is divided into anterior and posterior branches by the lateral circumflex
femoral artery.
Option C: Lateral cutaneous nerve (a.k.a lateral femoral cutaneous nerve) of the thigh is most
commonly injured at the site where it leaves the pelvis. An injury to the lateral cutaneous nerve of
the thigh leads to pain and paresthesia over the anterolateral region of the thigh, a condition
called meralgia paresthetica.
Option D: Ilioinguinal nerve is most commonly injured within the inguinal canal in open hernia
repair due to traction or thermal injury. Injury causes loss of sensation in the root of
the penis and adjacent part of the scrotum.

subject: Anatomy
question:
Identify the nerve passing around the marked structure.

[Anatomy-INI-CET-2022-22-image]
explanation:
The structure marked in the given image is the neck of fibula. The nerve passing around the neck
of fibula is the common peroneal nerve (common fibular nerve).
The common peroneal nerve is derived from the dorsal branches of the ventral rami of L4, L5, S1,
and S2. The sciatic nerve divides into the tibial nerve and the common fibular nerve.
The common fibular nerve runs obliquely along the lateral side of the popliteal fossa to the fibular
head. It lies between the biceps femoris and the lateral head of the gastrocnemius. It then curves
280
laterally around the neck of the fibula and gets divided into superficial and deep fibular nerves.
Superficial fibular nerve:
Motor innervation:
• Peroneus longus
• Peroneus brevis
Sensory innervation:
• Lateral aspect of the lower leg
• Dorsum of the toes except for the lateral side of the 5th toe and the first interdigital webspace
Deep fibular nerve:
Motor innervation:
• Extensor digitorum longus
• Extensor hallucis longus
• Tibialis anterior
• Peroneus tertius
Sensory innervation:
• The first interdigital space

[Anatomy-INI-CET-2022-22-exp-image]
281

[Anatomy-INI-CET-2022-22-exp-image]

[Anatomy-INI-CET-2022-22-exp-image]
The common peroneal nerve is more prone to injury as it lies superficial at the level of the lateral
aspect of the neck of the fibula. Injury results in foot drop (which causes high-stepping gait), loss
of eversion of the foot, and sensory loss on the dorsum of the foot.

subject: Anatomy
question: Which of the following cells are present in the molecular layer of the cerebellum?
explanation:
The molecular layer of the cerebellum contains stellate and basket cells.
The molecular layer is the outermost layer that contains stellate cells and basket cells, and
dendrites of Purkinje cells. They receive excitatory input from the parallel fibers and
form inhibitory synapses on the Purkinje cells. This layer contains many myelinated and
unmyelinated nerve fibers and appears pale on H&E staining.
282

[Anatomy-INI-CET-2022-23-exp-image]

subject: Anatomy
question:
Bleeding as shown in the image is due to which of the following vessels?

[Anatomy-INI-CET-2022-24-image]
explanation:
The image showing a bleed over the arachnoid layer but under the dura is suggestive of a subdural
hemorrhage. Most commonly, it results from the shearing of cortical bridging veins.
Subdural hemorrhage (SDH) is a collection of blood between the dura and the arachnoid layers of
the meninges. The causes of SDH include
• trauma
• anticoagulants, antiplatelets
• alcohol abuse
• battered baby syndrome.
Non-contrast CT is the investigation of choice.
Other options:
Middle meningeal artery rupture would result in an extradural hemorrhage.
Lenticulostriate artery would lead to an intraparenchymal hemorrhage.
Vertebral artery dissection leads to subarachnoid hemorrhage or sometimes intraparenchymal
hemorrhage.
283

[Anatomy-INI-CET-2022-24-exp-image]
The given image shows types of brain hemorrhage:

[Anatomy-INI-CET-2022-24-exp-image]

subject: Anatomy
question: Which of the following statements are true about the internal capsule?
explanation:
Statements 1, 2, and 4 are true about the internal capsule.
The charcot’s artery of hemorrhage is a larger lenticulostriate or lateral striate artery. Lateral
striate arteries supply the anterior limb, genu and the posterior limb of the internal capsule. These
arteries are also commonly involved in ischemic and hemorrhagic strokes.
The retrolentiform part of the internal capsule carries fibers mainly from optic radiation. A lesion
here produces homonymous hemianopia, superior quadrantanopia, or inferior quadrantanopia.
The anterior limb of the internal capsule carries fronto-pontine fibers. A lesion here
produces confusion, impaired attention, agitation, and dysarthria.
284
The posterior limb of the internal capsule carries cortico-spinal motor fibers in the anterior part
and the lesion here produces contralateral hemiparesis. The posterior part of the posterior limb of
the internal capsule carries sensory fibers and lesions here may produce contralateral
hemi-sensory deficits.
The internal capsule predominantly contains projection fibers, not commissural fibers. Projection
fibers are the fibers that project from higher to lower centers or vice versa in CNS.

[Anatomy-INI-CET-2022-25-exp-image]

subject: Anatomy
question: Which of the following is not a branch of posterior division of internal iliac artery ?
explanation:
The inferior gluteal artery is a branch of the anterior division of the internal iliac artery.

[Anatomy-INI-CET-2022-26-exp-image]
Branches of the anterior trunk of the internal iliac artery:
• Obturator artery
285
• Superior vesical artery
• Inferior vesical artery
• Inferior gluteal artery
• Middle rectal artery
• Vaginal artery (in females)
• Uterine artery (in females)
• Internal pudendal artery
Branches of the posterior trunk of the internal iliac artery:
• Iliolumbar artery
• Lateral sacral arteries
• Superior gluteal artery

[Anatomy-INI-CET-2022-26-exp-image]

subject: Anatomy
question:
Identify the incorrect statement regarding the marked structure.

[Anatomy-INI-CET-2022-27-image]

options.a: It is the meeting point of frontal, parietal, temporal and sphenoid bones.
options.b: Blow to the lateral side of the skull injures the marked structure.
options.c: Accessory middle meningeal artery lies under this structure.
options.d: It corresponds to site of anterolateral fontanelle of fetal skull.

explanation:
The structure marked in the image is the pterion. The incorrect statement is statement 3, which is
the accessory middle meningeal artery lies under this structure.
The pterion is an H-shaped sutural junction between four bones of the skull:
• Frontal bone
• Squamous part of the temporal bone
• Greater wing of the sphenoid bone
• Parietal bone
It is located approximately 4 cm above the midpoint of the zygomatic arch.
286

[Anatomy-INI-CET-2022-27-exp-image]
The pterion overlies the anterior branch of the middle meningeal artery and the lateral fissure of
the cerebral hemisphere. The bone in this area is thin, and any injury to this area may lead to
rupture of the middle meningeal artery, leading to extradural hematoma.
The pterion also corresponds to the site of the anterolateral (sphenoidal) fontanelle of the
neonatal skull, which closes in the third month after birth.

[Anatomy-INI-CET-2022-27-exp-image]
The accessory meningeal artery may be a branch of the middle meningeal artery or might arise
directly from the maxillary artery. It reaches the cranium via the foramen ovale and distributes
principally in the extracranial region. It is distributed over the medial and lateral pterygoids,
tensor veli palatini, greater wing and pterygoid processes of the sphenoid bone, mandibular nerve,
and the otic ganglion. So, it supplies the trigeminal ganglion, dura mater and bone. But in some
people, it may be replaced by small separate arteries.
287

subject: Anatomy
question:
What are the derivatives of the marked cells?
243

[Anatomy-INI-CET-2022-28-image]
explanation:
The cells shown in the image are neural crest cells (NCC) present adjacent to the neural tube (NT).
NCCs contribute to many organs and tissues, and the neural crest is sometimes referred to as the
fourth germ layer:
• In the trunk region, they form:
• Melanocytes
• Sensory ganglia
• Sympathetic and enteric neurons
• Schwann cells
• Adrenal medulla cells
• In the cranial region, they contribute to the:
• Craniofacial skeleton
• Neurons for cranial ganglia
• Satellite glial cells
• Melanocytes
• Some of the other neural crest derivatives are
• C cells of the thyroid gland
• Conotruncal septum in the heart
• Odontoblasts
• Smooth muscle cells to blood vessels to the face and forebrain.
The intermediate mesoderm is a longitudinal strip of intraembryonic mesoderm that connects the
paraxial with the lateral plate mesoderm. The intermediate mesoderm develops into the genital
ridge and nephrogenic cord.
The majority of the heart, including the right ventricle and outflow tract, is derived from the
bulbus cordis, which is further derived from the pharyngeal mesoderm.
288

[Anatomy-INI-CET-2022-28-exp-image]

subject: Anatomy
question: Which of the following muscles form the pelvic diaphragm?
explanation:
Iliococcygeus and pubococcygeus are parts of the pelvic diaphragm, which is also known as the
levator ani.
The levator ani muscle is a complex unit that consists of 3 muscle components:
• Pubococcygeus
• Pubovaginalis - elevates the urethra and hence contributes to urinary continence
• Puboperinealis - draws the perineal body towards the pubic symphysis
• Puboanalis - elevates the anus and keeps the urogenital hiatus narrowed
• Puborectalis
• Iliococcygeus
Levator ani muscles are the most important muscles in the pelvic floor and maintain a constant
state of contraction. They provide a stable floor and support the weight of the abdominopelvic
contents against intra-abdominal forces. Weakening of levator ani and pelvic fascia from
stretching or tearing during childbirth, may decrease support for the vagina, bladder, uterus, or
rectum or alter the position of the neck of the bladder and urethra.
Other options:
The obturator internus forms the lateral wall of the ischioanal fossa and is not a part of the levator
ani.
The deep transverse perineal muscle is the content of the deep perineal space and contributes to
the formation of the perineal body.
289

[Anatomy-INI-CET-2022-29-exp-image]

subject: Anatomy
question:
Which of the following arteries passes in close proximity to the area marked by the arrow in
the given image?
244

[Anatomy-INI-CET-2022-30-image]

options.a: Anterior division of middle cerebral artery
options.b: Anterior division of the middle meningeal artery
options.c: Posterior division of middle cerebral artery

explanation:
The area marked by an arrow in the given image is pterion and the artery that passes in close
proximity to this area is the middle meningeal artery.
The pterion is an H shaped sutural junction between four bones of the skull:
• Frontal bone
• Squamous part of the temporal bone
• Greater wing of the sphenoid bone
• Parietal bone.
It is an important structure as it overlies the anterior branch of the middle meningeal artery and
lateral fissure of the cerebral hemisphere. The bone in this area is thin and any injury to this area
may lead to rupture of the middle meningeal artery leading to extradural hematoma.
290

[Anatomy-INI-CET-2022-30-exp-image]

subject: Anatomy
question: Fracture of the jugular canal can present as

options.a: Loss of taste sensation in anterioir 2/3rd of tongue
options.b: Loss of taste sensation in posterior 1/3rd of tongue
options.c: Loss of general sensation in anterior 2/3rd of tongue
options.d: Loss of motor sensation in anterior 2/3rd of tongue

explanation:
Fracture of the jugular canal affects glossopharyngeal nerve and presents as loss of taste sensation
in the posterior 1/3rd of the tongue.
The lingual nerve which is a branch of the mandibular division of the trigeminal nerve gives
general sensation, chorda tympani branch of facial nerve gives taste sensation for the anterior
2/3rd of tongue. Both taste and general sensation of the posterior 1/3rd is by the glossopharyngeal
nerve. The muscles of the tongue, with the exception of palatoglossus, are supplied by the
hypoglossal nerve. Palatoglossus is supplied via the pharyngeal plexus.
The other nerves passing through the jugular foramen are glossopharyngeal(cranial nerve IX),
vagus(cranial nerve X), and accessory nerve(cranial nerve XI).
The following is an image of the base of skull showing jugular foramen:

[Anatomy-INI-CET-2022-31-exp-image]
291

subject: Anatomy
question:
Injury of the lower brachial plexus leads to Klumpke's paralysis. What is the characteristic
clinical finding seen in this lesion?
explanation:
The characteristic clinical finding in Klumpke's paralysis is a complete claw hand.
Klumpke's paralysis is caused by injury of the lower trunk of the brachial plexus-mainly T1 and
partially C8, due to traction injury of the lower trunk that may be seen in undue abduction of the
arm (outstretched arm grabbing an object during a fall from height) or birth injury (traction of the
extended arm during delivery)
This leads to paralysis of the intrinsic muscles of the hand (T1) that are responsible for flexion at
metacarpophalangeal joints (MCP) and extension at proximal and distal interphalangeal
joints(PIP and DIP). The unopposed action of extensor digitorum at MCP causes hyperextension.
The unopposed action of flexor digitorum superficialis and profundus at PIP and DIP causes
flexion. Thus giving rise to the characteristic claw hand.
Other changes include anesthesia and analgesia along the ulnar border of the forearm, vasomotor
changes, absence of sweating, and trophic changes in long-standing cases. If the C8 is also
damaged, the extent of anesthesia will be greater and will involve the medial side of the forearm,
hand, and medial two fingers.
Horner's syndrome (option D) may occur in one-third of the cases due to injury of sympathetic
fibers to the head and neck that leave the spinal cord through nerve T1.
The following image shows a complete claw hand in Klumpke's paralysis:

[Anatomy-INI-CET-2022-32-exp-image]
Other options:
Option A: Policeman tip deformity, also known as waiter's tip hand or porter's tip hand, is a
finding of Erb's palsy due to injury of the upper trunk of the brachial plexus involving C5 and C6.
The hand is adducted, medially rotated, and hangs by the side with the elbow extended and
pronated.
The following image shows policeman tip deformity in Erb's palsy:
292

[Anatomy-INI-CET-2022-32-exp-image]
Option B: Wrist drop is a feature of radial nerve injury in the axilla, radial groove, anterolateral
side of the lower arm, or injury of the deep branch in the cubital fossa.
Option D: Horner's syndrome is caused due to the disruption of sympathetic supply to the face. It
causes ptosis, miosis, anhydrosis, enophthalmos, and loss of ciliospinal reflex of the affected eye.
It may occur in some cases of Klumpke's paralysis and is not a characteristic finding.
The following image shows the ocular manifestation of Horner syndrome:

[Anatomy-INI-CET-2022-32-exp-image]

subject: Anatomy
question:
Identify the liver segment marked in the image below:

[Anatomy-INI-CET-2022-33-image]
explanation:
The liver segment highlighted in green in this CT section is segment V. The section of the CT given
is taken below the portal vein as the cross-section of the portal vein before its bifurcation can be
viewed.
293
A horizontal plane known as the portal plane divides the liver where the portal vein bifurcates and
becomes horizontal. This divides each section of the liver into superior and inferior segments:
• Left lateral section: segment II above and segment III below the portal plane
• Left medial section: segment IVa above and segment IVb below the portal plane
• Right anterior (or medial) section: segment VIII above and segment V below the portal plane
• Right posterior (or lateral) section: segment VII above and segment VI below the portal plane
Shown below is a section from below the portal plane.

[Anatomy-INI-CET-2022-33-exp-image]
Couinaud classification is the commonly used classification of the liver. It divides the liver is
divided into eight segments. It is based on the distribution of portal venous branches in the liver
parenchyma. Each segment has its own vascular supply, biliary drainage, and lymphatic drainage.
The Couinaud's liver segments are as follows:

[Anatomy-INI-CET-2022-33-exp-image]
294

subject: Anatomy
question:
Which of the following lobes of the lung is affected when there is aspiration in supine
position?

options.b: Upper part of right inferior lobe

explanation:
The upper part of the right inferior lobe is prone to aspiration in the supine position. It is
commonly seen in alcoholics.
The posterior segment of the right superior lobe and the superior segment of the inferior lobes are
the most commonly involved lung sites when aspiration occurs in a recumbent patient.
The location of aspiration depends on the position of the patient since it is gravity-dependent.
Aspiration while standing leads to infiltration of bilateral inferior lobes because of larger and
more vertical corresponding bronchi, the middle lobes and the lingula.
Aspiration of contents into foreign substances into the bronchial tree, usually oral or gastric
contents, produces aspiration pneumonia. Chemical (gastric acid) injury or bacterial infection
complicates the condition.

subject: Anatomy
question:
Identify the cranial nerve that arises from the marked part.
246

[Anatomy-INI-CET-2022-35-image]
explanation:
The structure marked in the image is the abducens nerve that arises from the pontomedullary
junction.
In the groove between the medulla and pons, abducens, facial, and vestibulocochlear nerves
emerge arranged medially to laterally.
The abducens nerve is the 6th cranial nerve that arises from the brainstem at the pontomedullary
junction, passes through Dorello's canal, and enters the cavernous sinus where it lies lateral to the
internal carotid artery. It then enters the orbit through the common tendinous ring in the superior
orbital fissure to supply the lateral rectus.

subject: Anatomy
question: Which of the following are a part of the limbic system?
explanation:
The amygdala, hippocampus, and thalamus are all a part of the limbic system.
The limbic system refers to the interconnected neuronal circuit in the basal part of the brain
that controls emotional behavior and other vegetative functions such as body temperature, the
osmolality of the body fluids, and the drives to eat and drink and to control body weight.
The limbic system includes the following:
• Subcallosal gyrus
• Cingulate gyrus
• Parahippocampal gyrus
• Hippocampal formation
• Amygdaloid nucleus
• Mammillary bodies
295
• Anterior thalamic nucleus
• Hypothalamus
The connecting pathways for this system comprise the alveus, the fimbria, the fornix, the
mammillothalamic tract, and the stria terminalis.

[Anatomy-INI-CET-2022-36-exp-image]

subject: Anatomy
question:
A 50-year-old male presented with a positive Trendelenburg test on the right side. Identify
the nerve injured.
explanation:
Positive Trendelenburg test on the right side indicates a right superior gluteal nerve injury.
When the patient stands on his right foot, there is drooping of the left pelvis, this is a Positive
Trendelenburg test on the right side.
The gluteus medius and minimus are powerful abductors of the hip supplied by the superior
gluteal nerve.
Normally, when the leg is lifted off the ground, the gluteus medius and minimus on the opposite
(unsupported) side exert traction on the hip bone. This results in a slight raising of the pelvis on
the unsupported side, which helps maintain the trunk upright position.
If the gluteus medius and minimus are paralyzed, the pelvis drops on the contralateral side. This
is known as a positive Trendelenburg sign. It is positive in the paralysis of the hip abductors, and
in developmental dysplasia of the hip. A characteristic lurching gait is seen.
The image below shows the Trendelenburg test.
296

[Anatomy-INI-CET-2022-37-exp-image]
297

subject: Anatomy
question:
The structure marked A in the image below gives rise to which of the following structures?

[Anatomy-NEET-2022-1-image]
explanation:
The structure marked A is ventral mesentery. The falciform ligament is a derivative of the
ventral mesentery.

[Anatomy-NEET-2022-1-exp-image]
Mesenteries are double layers of the peritoneum that enclose an organ and connect it to the body
wall. They provide pathways for vessels, nerves, and lymphatics to and from the abdominal
viscera.
301
• Ventral mesentery: Only the foregut has a ventral mesentery. It is attached to the terminal part
of the esophagus, the stomach, and the upper part of the duodenum. The ventral mesentery is
derived from the septum transversum. When the liver grows into the mesenchyme of the septum
transversum, it divides the ventral mesentery into 2 parts:
• Lesser omentum- extends from the lower portion of the esophagus, the stomach, and the upper
portion of the duodenum to the liver
• Ligaments of the liver- all ligaments of the liver (falciform ligament, right and left triangular
ligaments, and coronary ligaments), except ligamentum teres
• Dorsal mesentery: The foregut, midgut, and hindgut have a dorsal mesentery. This connects the
organs to the posterior abdominal wall. It runs from the lower end of the esophagus to the cloacal
region of the hindgut. It has the following special names in different regions:
• Dorsal mesogastrium (or greater omentum)- stomach
• Dorsal mesoduodenum- duodenum
• Mesentery proper- jejunum and ileum
• Mesoappendix- appendix
• Transverse mesocolon- transverse colon
• Sigmoid mesocolon- sigmoid colon
• Gastrosplenic ligament (stomach to the spleen), lienorenal ligament (spleen to the left kidney),
and gastrophrenic ligament (stomach to diaphragm) are derived from the dorsal mesentery.

[Anatomy-NEET-2022-1-exp-image]
302

[Anatomy-NEET-2022-1-exp-image]

subject: Anatomy
question:
A patient underwent surgery for the varicose vein. He now complains of sensory loss over the
medial aspect of the leg and foot. Which of the following nerves is most likely to be injured?
explanation:
The given clinical scenario in a patient with sensory loss over the medial aspect of the leg and foot
is suggestive of injury to the saphenous nerve. Injury to the saphenous nerve is common following
varicose vein surgery.
The saphenous nerve is the longest and largest cutaneous branch of the femoral nerve and is the
longest nerve in the body. It arises from the posterior division of the femoral nerve. It then enters
the adductor canal and exits it by piercing the fascia lata between the gracilis and sartorius and
supplies the skin over the patella. The saphenous nerve then descends along the medial border of
the tibia along with the great saphenous vein (anterior to the vein) and supplies the skin on the
medial side of the knee, leg, and foot up to the first metatarsophalangeal joint.
During stripping of veins in varicose surgery, injury to the saphenous nerve is common as it
closely travels with the great saphenous vein. Sensory loss over the medial side of the knee, leg,
and foot is observed in saphenous nerve injury.
303

[Anatomy-NEET-2022-2-exp-image]

[Anatomy-NEET-2022-2-exp-image]
Other options:
Option A: Sural nerve injury causes sensory loss over the posterior and lateral aspects of the distal
third of the leg and lateral side of the foot and fifth toe. It is often used as an autologous
peripheral nerve graft as it is readily identified and easy to harvest.
Option B: Superficial peroneal nerve injury causes sensory loss over the lateral aspect of the leg
and the dorsum of the foot. It also causes weakness of foot eversion.
Option C: Deep peroneal nerve injury causes weakness of ankle dorsiflexion and toe extension but
normal eversion of the foot.

explanation:

The given image shows prominent collagen fibers arranged in bundles with intervening rows of
chondrocytes. This is suggestive of white fibrocartilage.
Microscopy of fibrocartilage shows the following:
• Chondrocytes- round in shape (in tendons they are flattened and elongated)
• Ground tissue with collagen fibers- type I collagen fibers
• Absence of perichondrium

[Anatomy-NEET-2022-3-exp-image]
Fibrocartilage is found in:
• Articular discs (temporomandibular joint and sternoclavicular joint)
• Intervertebral discs
• Menisci
• Glenoid and acetabular labrum
• Pubic symphysis
• Manubriosternal joint
• The lining of the bony grooves for tendons
The image below describes the histology of different types of catilages.

subject: Anatomy
question:
A patient presents with a unilateral throbbing headache, photophobia, and excessive
lacrimation. He also complains of hemifacial pain on clenching of teeth. On examination,
pupillary reaction, light reflex, and accommodation reflex are normal. Which of the following
marked nerves is involved in the above scenario?
299

[Anatomy-NEET-2022-4-image]
explanation:
The above patient presents with features suggesting an involvement of the trigeminal nerve. The
structure marked 3 is the trigeminal nerve.
Other marked structures:
• 1- Optic nerve. The normal visual field and light reflex rules out the involvement of the optic
nerve.
• 2- Oculomotor nerve. Normal accommodation and pupillary reflex rule out the involvement of
the oculomotor nerve.
• 4- Facial nerve. Absence of motor involvement of the face rule out the involvement of the facial
nerve.

[Anatomy-NEET-2022-4-exp-image]
306
The trigeminal nerve leaves the anterior aspect of the pons as a small motor root and a large
sensory root. The large sensory root expands to form the crescent-shaped trigeminal ganglion,
which lies within a pouch of dura mater called the trigeminal or Meckel's cave. The ophthalmic,
maxillary, and mandibular nerves arise from the anterior border of the ganglion.
Branches of the trigeminal nerve:
• The ophthalmic nerve (V1)
• Leaves the skull through the superior orbital fissure to enter the orbital cavity.
• Contains only sensory fibers
• Cornea (photophobia)
• The skin of the forehead, scalp, eyelids, and nose
• The mucous membrane of paranasal sinuses and nasal cavity
• The maxillary nerve (V2)
• Leaves the skull through the foramen rotundum.
• Contains only sensory fibers from
• Teeth of the upper jaw
• The skin of the face over the maxilla
• The mucous membrane of the nose, the maxillary sinus, and the palate
• The mandibular nerve (V3)
• Leaves the skull through the foramen ovale.
• Contains both sensory and motor fibers.
• Sensory from:
• The skin of the cheek, over mandible, and side of the head
• Teeth of the lower jaw and temporomandibular joint (hemifacial pain on clenching of teeth)
• The mucous membrane of the mouth and anterior part of the tongue
• Motor to:
• Muscles of mastication (pterygoids, masseter, and temporalis)
• Tensor tympani
• Anterior belly of digastric
• Mylohyoid

subject: Anatomy
question:
Identify the given structure:

[Anatomy-NEET-2022-5-image]
explanation:
The above image shows pancreatic islet cells. Islet of Langerhans makes up the endocrine part of
the pancreas.
307
Islets are characterized by secretory cells supported by reticulin fibers, and containing numerous
fenestrated capillaries. There is a delicate capsule around each islet. They are paler than the
surrounding exocrine cells, as they have less rough endoplasmic reticulum.
The image given below shows the histology of the pancreas.

[Anatomy-NEET-2022-5-exp-image]
The islets of Langerhans contain four major and two minor cell types:
• Four main types are β, α, δ, and PP (pancreatic polypeptide) cells:
• α or A cells- located peripherally and secrete glucagon
• β or B cells- located centrally and produce insulin
• δ or D cells- scattered between beta cells and secrete somatostatin
• PP cells- are present in islets and also in the exocrine pancreas. They secrete a pancreatic
polypeptide, which exerts several gastrointestinal effects, such as stimulation of secretion of
gastric and intestinal enzymes and inhibition of intestinal motility.
• Minor cell types:
• D1 cells secrete vasoactive intestinal peptide (VIP), a hormone that induces glycogenolysis and
hyperglycemia. It also stimulates gastrointestinal fluid secretion and causes secretory diarrhea.
• Enterochromaffin cells synthesize serotonin and are the source of pancreatic tumors that cause
carcinoid syndrome.
Other options:
Option A: Lymph nodes are secondary lymphoid organs. The structure of lymph nodes consists of
an outer cortex and inner medulla. The cortex contains an aggregation of B-lymphocytes (primary
follicles) and quiescent secondary follicles with a pale central germinal center.
The inner medulla consists of medullary cords (lymphocytes and plasma cells) and medullary
sinuses. Between the cortex and medulla, there is an ill-defined zone called the paracortex, which
contains T lymphocytes.
The image given below shows the histology of the lymph node.
308

[Anatomy-NEET-2022-5-exp-image]
Option B: Glomerulus is a rounded tuft of anastomosing capillaries. The glomerular capillary wall
is the filtering membrane and consists of fenestrated endothelial cells, basement membrane, and
visceral epithelial cells called podocytes, which contain foot processes.
The glomerular tuft is supported by the mesangium containing mesangial cells and a non-cellular
mesangial matrix.
The image given below shows the histology of the glomerulus.

[Anatomy-NEET-2022-5-exp-image]
Option C: Spleen is a secondary lymphoid organ. It contains round white pulp surrounded by red
pulp. The white pulp is made up of aggregations of lymphocytes that surround a small artery or
arterioles. Lymphocytes surrounding the arteriole are called peri-arterial lymphatic sheath (PALS)
and contain T cells. Follicles in white pulp contain B cells.
The red pulp is a modified lymphoid tissue infiltrated with all the cells of circulating blood. It is
like a sponge permeated by spaces (splenic sinusoids) lined by reticular cells. The zone of red pulp
immediately surrounding white pulp is the marginal zone. This zone has a rich network of
309
sinusoids.
The image given below shows the histology of the spleen.

[Anatomy-NEET-2022-5-exp-image]
310

subject: Anatomy
question:
A defect in which of the following forms the structure marked below?

[Anatomy-NEET-2023-1-image]
explanation:
The structure marked is the deep inguinal ring, which is formed by a defect in fascia transversalis.
The deep inguinal ring is an opening in the transversalis fascia present 1 cm above the inguinal
ligament, midway between the anterior superior iliac spine and the pubic symphysis. An indirect
inguinal hernia arises through the deep inguinal ring lateral to the inferior epigastric vessel.

[Anatomy-NEET-2023-1-exp-image]
315

subject: Anatomy
question:
A student had his jaw locked while yawning. Which of the following muscles is attached to the
articular disc of the temporomandibular joint?
explanation:
The muscle that is attached to the articular disc of the temporomandibular joint is the lateral
pterygoid.
Lateral pterygoid muscle:
• Origin - Upper head arises from the infratemporal surface and infratemporal crest of the greater
wing of the sphenoid bone. The lower head arises from the lateral surface of the lateral pterygoid
plate.
• Insertion - It is inserted into the pterygoid fovea, the capsule of the temporomandibular joint,
and the articular disc.
The action of lateral pterygoid muscle is depression, protrusion, and side-to-side movement of the
jaw.

subject: Anatomy
question:
311
Identify the structure(s) in the following histological image.

[Anatomy-NEET-2023-3-image]
explanation:
The above histology image shows the glomerulus.
Glomerulus is a rounded tuft of anastomosing capillaries lined by fenestrated endothelium. Blood
enters the tuft through an afferent arteriole and leaves it through an efferent arteriole. It is
supported by the mesangium containing mesangial cells and a non-cellular mesangial matrix.
The glomerular capillary wall is the filtering membrane and consists of fenestrated endothelial
cells, basement membrane, and visceral epithelial cells called podocytes, which contain foot
processes.
The image given below shows the histology of the glomerulus.

[Anatomy-NEET-2023-3-exp-image]
Option A: Leydig cells, also known as interstitial cells of Leydig, are present in the interstitial
connective between seminiferous tubules.
316
The testis contains numerous seminiferous tubules separated by connective tissue in
between. Seminiferous tubules are lined by several spermatogonia cells, which produce
spermatozoa, and Sertoli (sustentacular) cells, which have a supportive function.
The connective tissue in between the tubules is made up of Leydig cells, collagen fibers,
fibroblasts, macrophages, mast cells, blood vessels, and lymphatics. Leydig cells are large, round,
or polyhedral cells with eccentric nuclei. They are lightly stained and often have a foamy
appearance. They secrete testosterone upon stimulation by luteinizing hormone from the anterior
pituitary.
The image below shows seminiferous tubules and Leydig cells.

[Anatomy-NEET-2023-3-exp-image]
Option B: Islets of Langerhans makes up the endocrine part of the pancreas.
Islets are characterized by secretory cells supported by reticulin fibers and contain numerous
fenestrated capillaries. There is a delicate capsule around each islet. The islet cells are paler than
the surrounding exocrine cells, as they have less rough endoplasmic reticulum.
The islet of Langerhans is mainly composed of three types of cells:
• α or A cells - located peripherally and secrete glucagon
• β or B cells - located centrally and produce insulin
• δ or D cells - scattered between beta cells and secrete somatostatin
The image given below shows the islets of Langerhans.
317

[Anatomy-NEET-2023-3-exp-image]
Option C: Hassall's corpuscles are small rounded structures present in the medulla of the thymus.
Each corpuscle has a central core of degenerated epithelial cells and macrophages surrounded by
concentrically arranged epithelial cells. The degenerated cells ultimately give pink staining hyaline
mass. The function of the corpuscles of Hassall is still not known.
The image given below shows Hassall's corpuscle.

[Anatomy-NEET-2023-3-exp-image]

subject: Anatomy
question:
Injury at which of the following marked sites causes failure of dorsiflexion?

[Anatomy-NEET-2023-4-image]
explanation:
The site marked as '3' in the x-ray given above is the neck of the fibula. Injury at this site causes
failure of dorsiflexion (foot drop) due to injury to the common fibular nerve (also know as
common peroneal nerve)
318
The common fibular nerve is derived from the dorsal branches of the 4th and 5th lumbar and 1st
and 2nd sacral ventral rami. It runs obliquely along the lateral side of the popliteal fossa to the
fibular head. It lies between the biceps femoris and the lateral head of the gastrocnemius. It then
curves laterally around the neck of the fibula and gets divided into superficial and deep fibular
nerves.
Superficial fibular nerve
Deep fibular nerve
Motor innervation
Peroneus longusPeroneus bre
vis
Extensor digitorum longusEx
tensor hallucis longusTibialis
 anteriorPeroneus tertius

on

gDorsum of the toes except f
or the lateral side of the 5th t
oe and the first interdigital w
eb-space

[Anatomy-NEET-2023-4-exp-image]

[Anatomy-NEET-2023-4-exp-image]
319

[Anatomy-NEET-2023-4-exp-image]
Injury to the common peroneal nerve (common fibular nerve) at the level of the neck of the fibula
causes foot drop. Ankle dorsiflexion, toe extension, and eversion of the foot are lost, while the
inversion and plantar flexion are usually preserved. Sensory innervation to the dorsum of the foot,
including the first web space, is lost. The ankle reflex is usually preserved.

subject: Anatomy
question:
A defect in which of the following aortic arches causes the defect shown in the image?

[Anatomy-NEET-2023-5-image]
explanation:
The image above shows a patent ductus arteriosus due to a defect in the left side of the 6th aortic
arch.
Usually, in term infants, the ductus arteriosus closes within 72 hours of birth. Failure of the
ductus arteriosus to close results in patent ductus arteriosus (PDA).
PDA usually presents with a hyperdynamic precordium, bounding peripheral pulses, wide pulse
pressure, a machine-like continuous or systolic murmur, cardiomegaly, increased pulmonary
vascular markings, hepatomegaly, increasing oxygen dependence, carbon dioxide retention, and
renal failure.
In a majority of cases, PDA closes spontaneously. If spontaneous closure fails to occur, COX
inhibitors like indomethacin or ibuprofen can be used. Surgical ligation is indicated in case of
contraindications to medical management or failure of the same.
Other options
Option A: The 4th right aortic arch gives rise to the proximal part of the right subclavian artery.
Option B: The 4th left aortic arch gives rise to the arch of the aorta. Defects involving the 4th left
aortic arch could cause a variety of aortic arch anomalies, such as interruption of the aortic arch,
but not PDA.
Option C: The 6th right aortic arch forms the proximal part of the right pulmonary artery.
320

subject: Anatomy
question:
Which of the following structures develops from the structure marked A?
313

[Anatomy-NEET-2023-6-image]
explanation:
The structure marked A is the allantois. Its distal portion forms the urachus, which in turn gives
rise to the median umbilical ligament.
The given image shows an embryo at the end of the third week.

[Anatomy-NEET-2023-6-exp-image]
Meckel's diverticulum is a congenital diverticulum arising from anti-mesenteric border of ileum. It
is a remnant of the proximal part of the vitelline duct, which is made up of pluripotent cell lining.
Meckel's diverticulum may harbor abnormal tissues, containing embryonic remnants of other
tissue types, mostly gastric mucosa.
