subject: Anatomy
question:
A patient presents with swelling under the left ear lobule and complains of pain. Which of the
following structures is the likely cause of the pain?

options.d:
Investing layer of deep cervical fascia
314

explanation:
The cause of the pain in this patient complaining of pain in the parotid region is likely to be the
greater auricular nerve.
The greater auricular nerve provides sensory innervation to the skin overlying the parotid gland
and the ear lobule. Pain associated with swelling and inflammation in the parotid region could be
transmitted through the greater auricular nerve. The parotid gland is covered by a parotid capsule
that is derived from the investing layer of deep cervical fascia. Any acute inflammation of the
gland such as acute sialadenitis results in pain in the preauricular region. The pain is mainly due
to stretching of the capsule resulting in stimulation of the greater auricular nerve.
Other options:
Option A: The facial nerve is not the primary cause of pain in the parotid region due to swelling.
The Hitzelberger sign(numbness in the posterosuperior meatal wall of the external auditory
canal), associated with facial nerve involvement, is seen in acoustic neuroma and is not the cause
of pain in this scenario.
Option D: Investing layer of deep cervical fascia is not the correct answer because the investing
layer of deep cervical fascia itself does not transmit pain sensation. The investing layer of deep
cervical fascia is a layer of connective tissue that envelops the neck and extends to cover the
parotid gland, forming the parotid capsule. While inflammation or swelling in the parotid gland
can stretch the capsule and cause pain, the actual transmission of pain sensation is mediated by
sensory nerves, such as the greater auricular nerve, which innervates the skin over the parotid
gland and the ear lobule.
The greater auricular nerve (Option B) is a more appropriate answer in this context, as it is
responsible for carrying the pain sensation from the area of swelling and inflammation in the
parotid region.
Option C: The auriculotemporal nerve can cause referred pain due to caries tooth, ulcerative
lesions of the oral cavity, temporomandibular joint disorders, and sphenopalatine neuralgia.
However, it is not the primary cause of pain in the parotid region due to swelling.
322

subject: Anatomy
question:
Which of the following vessels is involved in the condition affecting the region marked in red
in the image shown below?

[Anatomy-INI-CET-2023-1-image]
explanation:
The given image is suggestive of lateral medullary syndrome/Wallenberg syndrome. This may
result from a stroke affecting the Posterior Inferior Cerebellar Artery.
The clinical findings correspond to the neuroanatomical structures affected in the lateral portion
of the medulla:
• Descending tract and nucleus of CN V (trigeminal)- loss of pain and sensation over the ipsilateral
half of the face.
• Inferior cerebellar peduncle – ipsilateral ataxia (gait and limb)
• Lateral spinothalamic tract – loss of pain and temperature sensation over the contralateral half
of the body.
• IX and X CN - ipsilateral paralysis of palate, vocal cord, diminished gag reflex giving rise
to dysphagia, dysarthria.
• Vestibular nucleus - Nystagmus, diplopia, vertigo, nausea
336
• Descending sympathetic tract – ipsilateral Horner’s syndrome (miosis, ptosis, and anhidrosis).

The muscles marked in the above image are rhomboids minor and major. These muscles cause
retraction and elevation of the scapula and they rotate the scapula tilting the glenoid cavity
inferiorly.
Rhomboids minor and major are innervated by the dorsal scapular nerve. They are supplied by
the dorsal scapular artery or the deep branch of the transverse cervical artery.
Muscles involved in movements of the scapula are:
• Protraction (forward movement): Serratus anterior and pectoralis minor are prime movers.
Rhomboid muscles control the rate and range of movements. The upper part of the latissimus
dorsi acts like a strap across the inferior scapular angle in protraction.
• Retraction (backward movement): Middle trapezius and rhomboids.
• Elevation: Trapezius, Levator scapulae and rhomboids.
• Depression: Usually gravity alone is sufficient for the depression of the scapula. When required,
a part of the serratus anterior, pectoralis minor, and trapezius are used.

explanation:

Chorda tympani nerve which is a branch of facial nerve carries taste from anterior 2/3rd of
the tongue.
The trapezius is innervated by the spinal accessory nerve (CN XI). It causes retraction of
scapula, shrugging of the shoulder and overhead abduction (along with serratus anterior).
Paralysis of trapezius causes drooping of the shoulder and slight winging of the scapula (the
superior angle of scapula becomes more prominent).
The glossopharyngeal nerve carries taste sensation from the posterior 1/3rd of the tongue.
The mandibular nerve (V3) is responsible for chewing movements as it gives motor supply to
muscles of mastication. It contains both sensory and motor fibers.

subject: Anatomy
question: Which of the following muscles are supplied by Superior Gluteal Nerve?
explanation:
Gluteus Medius, Gluteus Minimus, and Tensor fascia lata are supplied by Superior Gluteal Nerve.
Piriformis is supplied by the nerve to the piriformis.
The dorsal branches of the L4, L5, and S1 rami give rise to the superior gluteal nerve. It is
accompanied by superior gluteal vessels. This nerve leaves the pelvis via the greater sciatic
foramen above piriformis and divides into superior and inferior branches. It supplies gluteus
medius, gluteus minimus, and tensor fasciae latae.
The dorsal divisions of the S1 and S2 rami give rise to the nerve to the piriformis.
Gluteus medius and gluteus minimus act as the abductors of the hip joint. Stabilizing the pelvis on
the femur during walking is done by these muscles. It is demonstrated by asking the subject to
stand on one limb. If the ipsilateral abductors are normal, the pelvis remains in a horizontal
339
position. If the ipsilateral abductors are weak, then the pelvis tilts towards the opposite
(unsupported) side. This is called positive Trendelenburg’s sign.

[Anatomy-INI-CET-2023-4-exp-image]

[Anatomy-INI-CET-2023-4-exp-image]

subject: Anatomy
question:
A 25-year-old male presented with symptoms of inability to flex the distal interphalangeal
joint of the 4th and 5th digits. He was also not able to hold a piece of paper between his
fingers. What is the likely site of injury?

[Anatomy-INI-CET-2023-5-image]
explanation:
The given clinical situation with a positive card test and an inability to flex the distal
interphalangeal joint of the 4th and 5th digits, suggests an ulnar nerve injury. The most likely site
of injury would thus be medial epicondyle: site C
Injury at the medial epicondyle damages the ulnar nerve, leading to paralysis of flexor carpi
ulnaris (loss of ulnar deviation) and medial half of flexor digitorum profundus (loss of distal
interphalangeal joint flexion). It leads to hypothenar paralysis (lead to atrophy) and sensory loss.
Also, flexor digitorum superficialis (supplied by median nerve) and extensor digitorum (supplied
by the radial nerve) are still functional. Hence complete paralysis of the 3rd and 4th digits
340
is not seen.  The positive card test is due to the weakness of palmar interossei leading to loss of
adduction.
Ulnar nerve branches and supply:
Superficial branch supplies palmaris brevis, skin on the palmar surface of medial 1½ fingers.
The deep branch runs within the concavity of deep palmar arch and supplies all intrinsic
muscles of the hand (except first 2 lumbricals and thenar muscles).
Injury to the Ulnar nerve:
Other tests seen in ulnar nerve lesions are as follows:
• Claw hand deformity
• Froment's sign positive
• Wartenberg sign
• Palmaris brevis sign

subject: Anatomy
question: Which of the following muscles form the pelvic diaphragm?
explanation:
342
Iliococcygeus and pubococcygeus are parts of the pelvic diaphragm, which is also known as the
levator ani.
The levator ani muscle is a complex unit that consists of 3 muscle components:
• Pubococcygeus
• Pubovaginalis - elevates the urethra and hence contributes to urinary continence
• Puboperinealis - draws the perineal body towards the pubic symphysis
• Puboanalis - elevates the anus and keeps the urogenital hiatus narrowed
• Puborectalis
• Iliococcygeus
Levator ani muscles are the most important muscles in the pelvic floor and maintain a constant
state of contraction. They provide a stable floor and support the weight of the abdominopelvic
contents against intra-abdominal forces. Weakening of levator ani and pelvic fascia from
stretching or tearing during childbirth, may decrease support for the vagina, bladder, uterus, or
rectum or alter the position of the neck of the bladder and urethra.
Other options:
The obturator internus forms the lateral wall of the ischioanal fossa and is not a part of the levator
ani.
The deep transverse perineal muscle is the content of the deep perineal space and contributes to
the formation of the perineal body.

[Anatomy-INI-CET-2023-6-exp-image]

subject: Anatomy
question: Anterior relations of the third part of the duodenum are all except:

options.d: Fundus and body of Gall bladder

explanation:
Anterior relations of the third part of the duodenum include the Superior mesenteric vessels, the
Root of the mesentery, and Loops of the jejunum.
Relations of the 3rd part of duodenum:
343
• Posterior relations of 3rd part of duodenum include right ureter, right psoas major, right
gonadal vessels, inferior vena cava and abdominal aorta (at the origin of the inferior mesenteric
artery).
• Anterior relations: Loops of jejunum, Root of mesentery and Superior mesenteric vessels.
• Superiorly related to head of the pancreas and Inferiorly to loops of jejunum.
Note: Root of mesentery (containing Superior mesenteric vessels) lies anterior to the 3rd part of
Duodenum. Hence, posterior relations of 3rd part of duodenum are the same as structures
crossed by root of mesentery.

subject: Anatomy
question:
Given below is the image of a 33-hour chick embryo. Which of the following is derived from
the marked structure?
326

[Anatomy-INI-CET-2023-10-image]
explanation:
The structure marked in the given image is the para-axial mesoderm. The ribs are derived from
the para-axial mesoderm.
During the third week of embryological development, the process of gastrulation occurs, which
refers to the formation of the three germ layers: ectoderm, mesoderm and endoderm. This is
followed by the formation of the notochord, which is the axial structure, which induces the
formation of the neural tube above it. During the formation of the neural tube, some cells at the
lateral border dissociate and form the neural crest cells, which is sometimes referred to as the
fourth germ layer.

[Anatomy-INI-CET-2023-10-exp-image]
346

[Anatomy-INI-CET-2023-10-exp-image]
After the formation of the notochord, which is the axial structure, the mesoderm on either side
can be seen to be organized into three layers as follows:
1. Para-axial mesoderm - Thickened plate of the mesoderm close to the midline, on either side of
the notochord. This forms the somitomeres and somites, which have the following derivatives:
• Dermatome: Forms the segmental dermis of the skin
• Myotome: Forms the skeletal muscle
• Sclerotome: Forms the vertebral column and ribs
2. Intermediate mesoderm - Longitudinal strip of mesoderm that connects the para-axial with the
lateral plate mesoderm. The urinary (Option A) and genital systems are derived from the
intermediate mesoderm.
3. Lateral plate mesoderm - A thinner layer of mesoderm that lies laterally. This layer develops a
large cavity called the intraembryonic coelom that gives rise to pericardial, pleural (Option C), and
peritoneal cavities.
The neural crest cells also contribute to the formation of structures as follows:
In the trunk region, they form melanocytes, sensory ganglia (Option B), sympathetic and enteric
neurons, Schwann cells, and adrenal medulla cells.
In the cranial region, they contribute to the craniofacial skeleton, neurons for cranial ganglia,
satellite glial cells, and melanocytes.

subject: Anatomy
question: Which of the following is false regarding the anatomy of the adrenal glands?

options.a: The right adrenal gland is semi lunar in shape
options.b: The left adrenal gland is closely related with tail of pancreas and spleen
options.c: The adrenals are yellow on gross appearance
options.d: The adrenals are retroperitoneal in location

explanation:
Option A is false as the right adrenal gland is pyramidal in shape while the left adrenal gland is
semi-lunar/crescent shaped.
The adrenal glands, also called the supra-renal glands, are endocrine glands which are located at
the upper pole of each kidney. They are grossly yellow in appearance and are retroperitoneal in
347
location. Both glands are surrounded by areolar tissue and perirenal fascia. The following are the
key differences between the right and the left adrenal glands and their relations:
Each gland is supplied by:
• Superior suprarenal artery - branch of the inferior phrenic artery.
• .Middle suprarenal artery - branch of the abdominal aorta.
• Inferior suprarenal artery - branch of the renal artery.
Each gland has one draining vein.
• The right suprarenal vein drains into the IVC.
• The left suprarenal vein drains into the left renal vein.

subject: Anatomy
question:
The sulcus located deep to the marked structure is?
327

[Anatomy-INI-CET-2023-12-image]
explanation:
348
The area marked by an arrow in the given image is pterion and the stem of the lateral sulcus lies
under it.
The pterion is an H-shaped sutural junction between four bones of the skull:
• Frontal bone
• Squamous part of the temporal bone
• Greater wing of the sphenoid bone
• Parietal bone.
The surface of the cerebrum consists of folds called gyri separated by fissures called sulci. The
lateral sulcus, also called the Sylvian Fissure, is present on the infero-lateral surface of each
cerebral hemisphere and separates the frontal lobe from the temporal lobe. The lateral sulcus
consists of a stem which divides into three rami, which are, the anterior horizontal, anterior
ascending, and the posterior rami.
The structures lying deep to the pterion are:
• The anterior branch of the middle meningeal artery
• The middle meningeal vein
• The stem of the lateral sulcus (Sylvian fissure)
The bone in this area is thin and an injury to this area may lead to rupture of the middle
meningeal artery leading to extradural hematoma.

[Anatomy-INI-CET-2023-12-exp-image]
349

[Anatomy-INI-CET-2023-12-exp-image]

subject: Anatomy
question:
Given below is an illustration of a transverse section of the heart. Identify the marked
structure.

[Anatomy-INI-CET-2023-13-image]
explanation:
The marked structure is the sub-aortic curtain. It is also called the trigonum fibrosum sinistrum.
The fibrous skeleton of the heart is formed by fibrous tissue that is continuous between the
atrio-ventricular and arterial orifices. They exist to keep the valves competent, allow attachment
of cardiac musculature, and act as an insulating layer for the conducting system of the heart. The
fibrous rings of importance are:
• The atrioventricular fibrous rings - existing in a figure of 8 configuration, connecting the atria,
ventricles and the membranous part of the interventricular septum.
• The trigonum fibrosum dextrum - between the atrioventricular rings and the aortic ring.
• The trigonum fibrosum sinistrum - between the mitral valve and the aortic valve.

[Anatomy-INI-CET-2023-13-exp-image]
350

subject: Anatomy
question:
Identify the structure marked as A.

[Anatomy-INI-CET-2023-14-image]
explanation:
The arrow-marked structure in the given image is the terminal bronchiole. It can be identified by
the absence of cartilage, which would be present in the bronchus, and the presence of cilia and
stratified columnar epithelium, which would not be seen in respiratory bronchioles and alveolar
sac.
The following is a summary of the intrapulmonary passages:
Trachea → Main Bronchi → Lobar Bronchi → Segmental Bronchi → Terminal Bronchiole →
Respiratory Bronchiole → Alveolar Duct → Alveolar Sac → Alveoli
Bronchi and bronchioles can be distinguished by the presence of cartilage and glands in the
bronchi which are not present in the bronchioles.
Terminal bronchioles represent the most distal parts of the conducting passage. Each terminal
bronchiole ends by dividing into respiratory bronchioles. Terminal bronchioles contain cilia and
stratified columnar epithelium, whereas respiratory bronchioles contain few or no cilia, and
simple cuboidal epithelium. Another differentiating point between the two is that terminal
bronchioles would not communicate with the alveoli but respiratory bronchioles would have a
communication with the alveoli.
Each respiratory bronchiole ends by dividing into a few alveolar ducts. Each alveolar duct ends in
a passage, the atrium, which leads into a number of rounded alveolar sacs. Each alveolar sac is
studded with a number of air sacs or alveoli.

[Anatomy-INI-CET-2023-14-exp-image]
A: Terminal Bronchiole, B: Respiratory Bronchiole, C: Alveolar Sac, D: Alveolar Duct
The coloured strips in the following illustration represent the location of different cells/structures
in the trachea and bronchial tree.
351

subject: Anatomy
question:
The fibres of the internal oblique muscle and transverse abdominus muscle contribute to
which boundaries of the inguinal canal walls:
explanation:
The fibres of the internal oblique muscle and transverse abdominus muscle contribute to the roof
and posterior wall of the inguinal canal walls.
The boundaries of the inguinal canal walls are as follows:
• Anterior wall: Formed by the external oblique aponeurosis.
• Roof: Formed by the arched fibres of the internal oblique and transversus abdominis muscles.
• Floor: Formed by the grooved upper surface of the Abdomen and Pelvis inguinal ligament.
• Posterior wall: Consists of the transversalis fascia and conjoint tendon (the lowest fibres of the
transverses abdominis muscle fuse with the lowest fibres of the internal oblique to form the
conjoint tendon).

subject: Anatomy
question:
Which of the following is a function of the marked layer?

[Anatomy-INI-CET-2023-16-image]
explanation:
The image shows a histological section of the adrenal gland and the layer marked is the outermost
layer, ie, the zona glomerulosa, which secretes the mineralocorticoid - aldosterone, responsible for
sodium and water retention.
Based on structure and function, the adrenal cortex has three zones, which from outer to inner,
are:
• Zona glomerulosa: Secretes mineralocorticoids (aldosterone) - which acts in response to the
activation of the renin-angiotensin-aldosterone-system (RAAS) and acts at the distal convoluted
tubule and the collecting ducts favouring sodium and water retention and potassium excretion.
352
• Zona fasiculata: Secretes glucocorticoids (cortisol and corticosterone) - which has multiple
functions that include metabolic effects which induce hyperglycemia inducing gluconeogenesis
(Option A) and mediates anti-inflammatory and immunosuppresive effects by interacting with the
cells of the immune system (Option D).
• Zona reticularis: Secretes sex steroids (dehydroepiandrosterone, androstenedione, estrogens,
and glucocorticoids) (Option C)

[Anatomy-INI-CET-2023-16-exp-image]

subject: Anatomy
question:
A patient's right hip drops when he takes the right foot off the ground. Which of the following
would not be involved in the patient?
330

[Anatomy-INI-CET-2023-17-image]
explanation:
A patient with dropping of the right hip on lifting the right foot off the ground, has a positive
Trendelenburg’s sign. The sciatic nerve would not be involved.
A positive Trendelenburg's sign is seen in the paralysis of the gluteus medius and gluteus minimus
muscles, both of which are supplied by the superior gluteal nerve. In the scenario mentioned,
since the patient has dropping of the hip on the right side, the structures on the left side would be
affected.
353

[Anatomy-INI-CET-2023-17-exp-image]
Mechanism of actions of gluteus medius and minimus:
• When the leg on one side is lifted off the ground,  making it the unsupported side,  gluteus
medius and minimus muscles on the opposite side, which is the supported side, exert traction on
the hip bone by acting from the femur. This action results in slight raising of the pelvis on the
unsupported side, which helps maintain the trunk in the upright position.
• When the gluteus medius and minimus are paralyzed, the pelvis on the unsupported side sinks
when the patient lifts the foot off the ground. This causes the patients to have a characteristic
lurching gait.

[Anatomy-INI-CET-2023-17-exp-image]

subject: Anatomy
question:
Which of the following statements is incorrect about the anomaly shown below?

[Anatomy-INI-CET-2023-18-image]

options.a: It occurs due to the persistance of prechordal plate
options.b:
It occurs due to the defective migration of primordial cells to the genital ridge
331
options.c: It contains skin, cartilage and bone
options.d: It is a benign tumor of coccyx

explanation:
354
The image shows a sacrococcygeal teratoma, which occurs due to the persistence of pluripotent
cells of the primitive streak in the sacrococcygeal region.
During the third week of embryological development, the process of gastrulation occurs, which
refers to the formation of the three germ layers. These germ layers are formed from the
primordial germ cells of the epiblast, which are pluripotent. The process begins with the
formation of the primitive streak on the epiblast, which develops into the primitive node in the
cranial region, and a primitive pit. The cells from the epiblast, migrate towards the primitive
streak and invaginate into the primitive pit and organizes itself into cells arranged in three layers,
which eventually develop into ectoderm, mesoderm, and endoderm. The prechordal plate simply
refers to the cells that have invaginated in the cranial region.

[Anatomy-INI-CET-2023-18-exp-image]
Sacrococcygeal teratomas occur due to remnants of pluripotent cells from the primitive streak due
to defective migration of the cells (Option B). As these cells are from the epiblast, teratomas
contain tissues from all germ layers which may include skin, hair, cartilage and bone (Option C).
These tumors are benign in nature (Option D), but may rarely become malignant. If they occur in
the cranial end of the primitive streak, they form oro-pharyngeal teratomas (as shown below),
whereas if they occur in the caudal end of the primitive streak in the coccygeal region, they are
called sacro-coccygeal teratomas.
355

[Anatomy-INI-CET-2023-18-exp-image]

subject: Anatomy
question:
What is the role of the marked structure in the given image?
332

[Anatomy-INI-CET-2023-20-image]
explanation:
The marked structure is the subthalamic nucleus, which is a part of the basal ganglia and is
concerned with motor function.
The basal ganglia refers to the mass of grey matter located within the cerebral white matter and is
responsible for a number of coordinated functions including the control of extra-pyramidal
pathways for motor coordination. There are two major pathways involved which are, the direct
and indirect pathways.
The direct pathway stimulates the cortex to produce movement, whereas the indirect pathway
inhibits it. The striatal activity in the direct pathway is mediated by D1 dopamine receptors and in
the indirect pathway by D2 dopamine receptors. The subthalamic nucleus is a part of the indirect
pathway. A lesion at subthalamic nuclei leads to loss of inhibitory effect of the indirect pathway
resulting in dopaminergic overactivity responsible for features of hemiballismus.

[Anatomy-INI-CET-2023-20-exp-image]
357

[Anatomy-INI-CET-2023-20-exp-image]
Other Options:
Option B: Reward-punishment is associated with the prefrontal area and the dopaminergic
connections linked to it from the ventral tegmental area.
Option C: Judgement is controlled by the frontal lobe.
Option D: Memory is controlled by the hippocampus.

subject: Anatomy
question: Which of the following muscles are supplied by the lateral plantar nerve?
explanation:
From the given list, the muscles supplied by the lateral plantar nerve are the flexor digitorum
accesorius and the first interossei.
358
The sole of the foot is supplied by the medial and lateral plantar nerves which are both branches
of the tibial nerve.
All the muscles of the sole of the foot are supplied by the lateral plantar nerve, except for the
following four muscles which are supplied by the medial plantar nerve:
• Abductor hallucis
• Flexor digitorum brevis
• Flexor hallucis brevis
• First lumbrical
The sensory supply of the sole of the foot is as shown in the illustration below.

[Anatomy-INI-CET-2023-21-exp-image]
The muscles of the sole of the foot are arranged in 4 layers as shown below:
1st layer: It consists of the abductor hallucis, flexor digitorum brevis and abductor digiti minimi.

[Anatomy-INI-CET-2023-21-exp-image]
359
2nd layer: It consists of flexor accessorius and the four lumbrical muscles,the tendons of flexor
hallucis longus and flexor digitorum longus run in the same plane as the muscles of the second
layer.

[Anatomy-INI-CET-2023-21-exp-image]
3rd layer: It consists of the shorter intrinsic muscles of the toes, which are flexor hallucis brevis,
adductor hallucis and flexor digiti minimi brevis.

[Anatomy-INI-CET-2023-21-exp-image]
4th layer: It consists of plantar and dorsal interossei and the tendons of tibialis posterior and
fibularis longus.

subject: Anatomy
question:
On neurologic examination, a patient is not able to identify an object but is able to recognize
the object from its texture, colour and pattern. Which of the following is likely to have been
affected?
explanation:
The clinical scenario suggests that the patient has visual agnosia and the likely site of involvement
would be the inferior temporal lobe.
• Agnosia refers to the inability to visually identify objects and is also called optic aphasia.
According to De Jong, visual agnosia is further classified into:
• Apperceptive visual agnosia - the visual image appears distorted to the patient and hence they
are unable to recognise it. This occurs due lesions in the parieto-occipital region
• Associative visual agnosia - inability to visually identify objects in the absence of visual
impairment, with ability to identify the object using other sensory modalities (as suggested in the
scenario). This occurs due to lesions of occipito-temporal junction, which is present in the inferior
temporal lobe. Prosopagnosia is a type of associatetive visual agnosia which refers to the
inability to visually identify faces.
The occipital lobe acts as the primary visual area and a lesion in this region would result in
cortical blindness and achromatopsia, which is the inability to visualise colour. In the given
scenario, as the patient is also able to identify colour, it indicates that the occipital lobe (Option A)
is unaffected and (hence inferior temporal lobe is the better answer).
Stereognosis is the ability to identify an object by a perception of the form and nature of an object
without looking at it. In the given scenario, the patient is able to recognize the object from its
texture and pattern which indicates that stereognosis is intact. Loss of this ability is known as
astereognosis which occurs in lesions of the superior parietal lobule (Options B and C) and dorsal
column pathways.

subject: Anatomy
question:
Apocrine glands are present in which of the following?
334

[Anatomy-INI-CET-2023-24-image]
explanation:
Apocrine glands are present in the axilla.
362
Exocrine glands can be classified into three subtypes, based on the manner in which their
secretions are poured out of the cells:
• Apocrine
• Merocrine
• Holocrine
In apocrine glands, the apical parts of the cells are shed off to discharge the secretion. Examples
of apocrine glands are :
• Secretion of milk fat by mammary glands
• Atypical sweat glands in the axilla, perianal region, periumbilical skin, mons pubis.
• Ceruminous glands of the external auditory meatus are modified apocrine glands
Apocrine glands are large glands found in the dermis or subcutaneous fat. They are a type of
sweat gland. It consists of a basal secretory coil followed by a straight duct that opens into
the pilary canal above the duct of the sebaceous gland. In hairless skin, it directly opens onto the
skin surface.
Apocrine activity is minimal before puberty, after which it is androgen-dependent and responsive
to emotional stimuli. It is controlled by adrenergic nerves and is sensitive to adrenaline and
noradrenaline. The secretion is initially sterile and odorless, but it undergoes bacterial
decomposition to generate odorous and musky compounds. Obstruction of apocrine sweat ducts
and associated upper hair follicles in the axillae, breast areolae, and pubic region, mainly in
women, is thought to cause Fox–Fordyce disease. It is also implicated in the causation of a
painful, occasionally debilitating condition called hidradenitis suppurativa.
The given image shows an illustration of apocrine secretion.

[Anatomy-INI-CET-2023-24-exp-image]
363

[Anatomy-INI-CET-2023-24-exp-image]

[Anatomy-INI-CET-2023-24-exp-image]

subject: Anatomy
question: Which of the following is a tributary of the inferior vena cava?
explanation:
The left inferior phrenic vein is a tributary of the inferior vena cava (IVC).
From inferior to superior, the following vessels are direct tributaries of the IVC:
• Common iliac veins (left and right common iliac veins merge to form the IVC)
• Median sacral vein
• Four pairs of lumbar veins
• Right gonadal vein
• Renal veins (both left and right)
• Right supra-renal vein
364
• Hepatic veins
• Inferior phrenic veins (both left and right)
Other Options:
Option B: The left supra-renal vein drains into the left renal vein.
Option C: The left gonadal vein drains into the left renal vein.
Option D: The right ascending lumbar vein joins the subcostal vein on the right side to form the
azygos vein. The left ascending lumbar vein joins the subcostal vein on the left side to form the
hemi-azygos vein.

subject: Anatomy
question:
The spinal sensory nucleus of the trigeminal nerve has 2nd order neurons to carry which
sensation?
explanation:
The trigeminal nerve (cranial nerve V) is responsible for sensory input from the face, as well as
motor functions such as chewing. Its sensory pathway involves three branches: the ophthalmic
(V1), maxillary (V2), and mandibular (V3) divisions. The first-order neurons for each division
originate from different sensory ganglia:
1.Ophthalmic Division (V1):
First-order neurons: Cell bodies are located in the trigeminal ganglion (also called the semilunar
or Gasserian ganglion).
2. Maxillary Division (V2):
First-order neurons: Cell bodies are located in the trigeminal ganglion.
3. Mandibular Division (V3):
First-order neurons: Cell bodies are located in the trigeminal ganglion.
378
These first-order neurons carry sensory information from the face to the brainstem. Upon
entering the brainstem, the fibers synapse with second-order neurons in specific nuclei:
1. Principal Sensory Nucleus (Main Sensory Nucleus):
a.Receives input from all three divisions of the trigeminal nerve.
b.Located in the pons.
c.Second-order neurons here carry discriminative touch and proprioceptive information.
2. Spinal Trigeminal Nucleus (Spinal Nucleus of Trigeminal):
a.Receives input mainly from the maxillary and mandibular divisions.
b.Located in the medulla.
c.Second-order neurons here carry pain and temperature sensations.
After synapsing in these nuclei, the sensory information is further relayed to higher brain centers
for processing and perception.

subject: Anatomy
question:
366
Which of the following is not seen in the floor of the inferior horn of the lateral ventricle?
explanation:
The tail of the caudate nucleus is not seen on the floor of the inferior horn of the lateral ventricle.
It is present on the roof of the inferior horn of the lateral ventricle.
The inferior horn is the largest compartment of the lateral ventricle and extends up to 2.5 cm
from the temporal pole. The relations are:
Roof - Tapetum of corpus callosum laterally and tail of caudate nucleus medially.
Floor (lateral to medial) - Collateral eminence produced by collateral fissure, hippocampus.
The floor of the temporal horn consists of the hippocampus medially and the collateral eminence,
formed by the infolding of the collateral sulcus, laterally. The inferior part of the choroidal fissure,
located between the fimbria of the fornix and the inferolateral aspect of the thalamus, lies in the
medial wall.
The lateral ventricle is divided into the body and 3 horns. The locations of the parts are:
• Body - parietal lobe
• Anterior horn - frontal lobe
• Posterior horn - occipital lobe
• Inferior horn - temporal lobe.
379

[Anatomy-INI-CET-2024-3-exp-image]
The anterior horn extends from the genu of the corpus callosum up to the interventricular
foramen. The relations of the anterior horn are:
• Anteriorly - Genu and rostrum of the corpus callosum
• Roof - Anterior part of the body of the corpus callosum
• Floor and lateral wall - Caudate nucleus
• Medial wall - Septum pellucidum and anterior column of fornix
The body or central part extends from the interventricular foramen to the splenium of the corpus
callosum. The relations of the body are:
• Roof - Body of the corpus callosum
• Medial wall - Septum pellucidum
• Floor (from lateral to medial) - Caudate nucleus, stria terminalis, thalamostriate vein, thalamus,
choroid plexus, and body of the fornix
The posterior horn extends from the splenium to the occipital pole. The relations are:
• Roof and lateral wall - Tapetum of corpus callosum
• Medial wall - Forceps major and calcar avis (calcarine sulcus
Note: There is no lateral wall for the lateral ventricle.
380

[Anatomy-INI-CET-2024-3-exp-image]

subject: Anatomy
question:
Which of the following marked areas in the histopathological specimen shows calcification?

[Anatomy-INI-CET-2024-4-image]
explanation:
The given specimen is that of an epiphyseal cartilage, where zone 3 corresponds to the zone of
calcification.
Epiphyseal cartilage is divided into five zones :
• The resting zone consists of hyaline cartilage without morphological changes in the cells (option
1)
• In the proliferative zone, chondrocytes divide rapidly and form columns of stacked cells parallel
to the long axis of the bone (option 2)
• The hypertrophic cartilage zone contains large chondrocytes whose cytoplasm has accumulated
glycogen. The resorbed matrix is reduced to thin septa between the chondrocytes.
• Simultaneous with the death of chondrocytes in the calcified cartilage zone, the thin septa of
cartilage matrix become calcified by the deposit of hydroxyapatite.
• In the ossification zone, endochondral bone tissue appears (option 4). Blood capillaries and
osteoprogenitor cells formed by mitosis of cells originating from the periosteum invade the
cavities left by the chondrocytes. The osteoprogenitor cells form osteoblasts, which are distributed
in a discontinuous layer over the septa of the calcified cartilage matrix. Ultimately, the osteoblasts
deposit bone matrix over the three-dimensional calcified cartilage matrix.
The epiphyseal cartilage is responsible for the growth in the length of bone and it disappears in
adults, which is why bone growth ceases in adulthood.
381

The structures passing in between the superior constrictor muscle ( structure 1 ) and middle
constrictor muscle ( structure 2) are glossopharyngeal nerve and stylopharyngeus muscle.
The structures passing in between pharyngeal muscles are as follows:
1. Sinus of Morgagni - It is a semilunar space that exists between the upper concave border of the
superior constrictor and the skull base. It is closed off by the upper sturdy portion of the
pharyngobasilar fascia. Structures passing through this space include:
a. The auditory tube
b. The levator veli palatini muscle (Option C)
c. The ascending palatine artery
382

glossopharyngeal nerve.

laryngeal vessels penetrate the thyrohyoid membrane (Option B).
4.  Between the lower border of the inferior constrictor and the esophagus - The recurrent
laryngeal nerve and the inferior laryngeal vessels.

subject: Anatomy
question:
At what vertebral level does the part marked in the image lie in a normal anatomical
position?

[Anatomy-INI-CET-2024-6-image]

options.d: Disc space between L4 and L5

explanation:
In the right pelvic bone given above the marked structure is the ischial spine which lies
approximately at the level of the fifth sacral vertebrae in a normal anatomical position. As the tip
of the coccyx is the closest option relating to the spine , it can be the most appropriate answer.
The ischial spine is a bony prominence found on the ischium, one of the three bones that make up
the hip bone. The ischial spine forms a part of the plane of least pelvic dimensions or narrow
pelvic plane which comprises of :
• lower border of the symphysis pubis
• the tip of ischial spines
• fifth sacral vertebra
The interspinous distance is the distance between the tip of two ischial spines. ( Bispinous
diameter = 10.5 cm or 4.5" )
The important events occurring at the narrowest plane in the pelvis :
• It corresponds roughly to the origin of levator ani muscles.
• The internal rotation of the fetal head occurs during labour at this plane.
• It marks the beginning of the forward curve of the pelvic axis
• This level of ischial spines indicates station ‘O’
• When the fetal head station is at ‘O’, the head is considered to be engaged.
• Deep transverse arrest occurs at this plane.
• The ischial spine can serve as a landmark in pudendal anaesthesia, as the pudendal nerve lies
close to the ischial spine
The image given below highlights the interspinous distance seen through the pelvic inlet.
383

subject: Anatomy
question:
The nerve related to the area marked in the picture has its root value from?

[Anatomy-INI-CET-2024-7-image]

options.d: Dorsal root ganglia of T1 and T2

explanation:
The above is an image of the left lung with the arrow pointing at the nerve passing anterior to the
left hilum. The phrenic nerve passes in front of the left hilum and has its root value from the
ventral horn of C4.
In the given image, the left lung is identified by the vertical sequence of structures at the left
hilum - pulmonary artery, principal (hyparterial) bronchus, and inferior pulmonary vein. The
image also shows an oblique fissure dividing the lung into superior and inferior lobes.
The phrenic nerve, originating from the cervical nerves C3–C5, is a mixed nerve that carries motor
fibres to the diaphragm and sensory fibres from the mediastinal pleura, pericardium, and a
portion of the peritoneum.
The course of the left phrenic nerve:
384
Cervical region - both phrenic nerves pass, Anterior to scalenus anterior muscle
Posterior to sternocleidomastoid and inferior belly of omohyoid
Posterior to internal jugular vein, transverse cervical artery, and suprascapular artery
Posterior to the thoracic duct on the left
Root of the neck - It lies medial to the trunks of the brachial plexus in the neck. At the root of the
neck, it runs anterior to the second part of the subclavian artery and posterior to the subclavian
vein.
Thorax - The phrenic nerve enters the thorax by crossing medially in front of the internal thoracic
artery. Within the thorax, the phrenic nerve descends anterior to the pulmonary hilum between
the fibrous pericardium and mediastinal pleura, accompanied by the pericardiacophrenic vessels.

question:
What level of the vertebral column does the given cross-sectional image correspond to?

[Anatomy-INI-CET-2024-9-image]
explanation:
The above cross-sectional picture is denoted to be T10 because ,
At the level of the T10 vertebra, we observe:
• The esophagus enters the abdomen through the diaphragm (at the level of T10), connecting
shortly afterward to the stomach at the cardiac orifice. In this image, the esophagus is positioned
slightly left to the midline and is surrounded by the crura of the diaphragm.
386

subject: Anatomy
question:
The structure marked as 'X' in image A has a direct connection with which of the following
structures marked in image B?

[Anatomy-INI-CET-2024-10-image]
explanation:
Image A shows a section of the cerebellum and image B shows a section of the midbrain. The
structure labeled as 'X' represents the dentate nucleus of the cerebellum, which has a direct
connection with the structure marked as '1', which is the red nucleus.
The dentate nucleus is the largest of the cerebellar nuclei. It has the shape of a crumpled bag with
the opening facing medially. The interior of the bag is filled with white matter made up of efferent
fibers that leave the nucleus through the opening to form a large part of the superior cerebellar
peduncle.
The efferent pathways from the dentate nucleus connect to the thalamus and the red nucleus, via
the dentato- rubro -thalamic pathway.
Other structures in the image B :
2 - Represents superior colliculus, which receives afferents from the retina and the occipital
cortex.
3 - Represents substantia nigra, which receives afferents mainly from the caudate nucleus
4 - Represents the crus cerebri, which contains fibers from the cerebral cortex.

subject: Anatomy
question: Which of the following statements is true concerning the anatomy of the breast?

options.a: Inner quadrants contain most breast tissue
options.b: Ligaments of cooper are found only in lower quadrants
options.c: Tail of Spence extends across anterior axillary fold

explanation:
The tail of Spence extends across the anterior axillary fold and is a small extension of the upper
lateral quadrant of the breast.
The breast lies in the superficial fascia of the pectoral region. It is divided into four quadrants, i.e.
upper medial, upper lateral, lower medial and lower lateral.
Vertically, it extends from the second to the sixth ribs. Horizontally, it extends from the lateral
border of the sternum to the midaxillary line. A small extension of the upper lateral quadrant,
called the axillary tail of Spence, passes through an opening in the deep fascia and lies in the
axilla. The opening is called the foramen of Langer.
387
The breast lies on the deep pectoral fascia covering the pectoralis major and serratus anterior
superiorly, the external abdominal oblique and its aponeurosis inferiorly, and the clavipectoral
fascia laterally. Fibrous strands or sheets consisting of condensations of connective tissue extend
between the posterior lamina of the superficial fascia and the dermis. These suspensory ligaments
(retinacula or Cooper’s ligaments) are often well-developed in the superior parts of the breast and
support the breast tissue, helping to minimize drooping.
The breasts normally comprise up to 20 discrete lobes that contain ductal-glandular tissue
consisting of branching ducts and terminal secretory lobules in a connective tissue stroma. Each
lobe is derived by branching from one central lactiferous duct in the nipple. The terminal duct
lobular unit (TDLU or lobule) is the functional milk secretory component of the breast.
The upper outer quadrant of the breast contains a greater volume of tissue than the other
quadrants. This is the reason for the majority of the carcinomas arising in the upper outer
quadrant.

[Anatomy-INI-CET-2024-11-exp-image]

[Anatomy-INI-CET-2024-11-exp-image]
388

subject: Anatomy
question:
371
A professor underwent a left parotidectomy. Post surgery she complains of left lower lip
paralysis. Which structure is involved?
explanation:
Lower lip paralysis is caused by damage to the cervical branch of the facial nerve.
The cervical branch of the facial nerve is responsible for innervating the platysma muscle, which is
one of the muscles involved in the movements of the lower lip. Damage to this nerve can
potentially lead to paralysis of the lower lip.
The facial nerve is the motor nerve of the face. Its five terminal branches emerge from the parotid
gland and diverge to supply the various facial muscles as follows :
• Temporal — frontalis, auricular muscles, orbicularis oculi
• Zygomatic — orbicularis oculi (lower eyelid part)
• Buccal — muscles of the cheek and upper lip
• Marginal mandibular — muscles of the lower lip
• Cervical — platysma

[Anatomy-INI-CET-2024-12-exp-image]

subject: Anatomy
question:
Identify the normal anatomical variation given in the following CT image.

[Anatomy-INI-CET-2024-13-image]
explanation:
The CT finding in the image is suggestive of an aberrant right subclavian artery.
An aberrant right subclavian artery occurs when the right fourth aortic arch and right dorsal aorta
involute cranial to the seventh intersegmental artery.
This artery arises distal to the left subclavian artery and crosses the midline posterior to the
esophagus. Because of the absence of a normal right subclavian-carotid junction to pull down the
right recurrent laryngeal nerve (RLN) during embryologic development, the right RLN follows a
straight path from the vagus to the larynx and is called a non-recurrent right laryngeal nerve.
Most patients with this anomaly are asymptomatic, and the aberrant subclavian artery is of no
clinical consequence. Dysphagia caused by compression of the esophagus by an abnormal
subclavian artery is called dysphagia lusoria.
389

[Anatomy-INI-CET-2024-13-exp-image]

subject: Anatomy
question:
Identify the marked structure
372

[Anatomy-INI-CET-2024-14-image]
explanation:
The marked structure is superficial temporal artery.
The superficial temporal artery is one of the terminal branches of the external carotid artery,
along with the maxillary artery. It ascends in front of the ear, passing over the zygomatic arch and
travels superficially, supplying the scalp and areas around the temple.

[Anatomy-INI-CET-2024-14-exp-image]
Other Options:
Option B: The posterior auricular artery is smaller and located posterior to the ear.
Option C: The middle meningeal artery is deep within the cranial cavity and supplies the
meninges.
Option D: The occipital artery lies posteriorly and supplies the scalp at the back of the head.
390

subject: Anatomy
question:
The marked structure serves as a surface landmark for which anatomical structure?

[Anatomy-INI-CET-2024-15-image]
explanation:
The marked structure indicates the pterion and it serves as a landmark for the stem of the lateral
sulcus.
The frontal, parietal, temporal, and sphenoid bones meet at the pterion. It is a landmark for the
stem of the lateral sulcus (Sylvian fissure), the most prominent sulcus of the cerebral hemisphere.
It separates the frontal lobe from the temporal lobe. Deep to the lateral sulcus lies the insular
cortex.

[Anatomy-INI-CET-2024-15-exp-image]
Pterion marks as a surface landmark for the following structures:
• Middle meningeal artery and vein: fractures in this area can rupture the middle meningeal
artery, leading to an epidural hematoma.
• Broca’s area: Located in the dominant hemisphere (usually left) in the inferior frontal gyrus, it
lies in proximity to the pterion
• The insular cortex and parts of the basal ganglia, such as the putamen, are located deep to the
pterion, beneath the lateral sulcus.
• Stem of the lateral sulcus, also called the Sylvian fissure. It houses the middle cerebral artery and
its branches, playing a crucial role in brain vascularization and cortical organization.
During surgeries, the pterion is a common site for trephining (making a hole in the skull).
Other Options:
Option A: The pineal gland is located in the posterior aspect of the cranial fossa in the brain. It is
located deep in the brain, near the midline.
Option B: Wenicke's area is in the superior temporal gyrus in the dominant cerebral hemisphere.
It is found in the posterior part of the superior temporal gyrus.
391
Option D: The internal carotid artery siphon is a part of the internal carotid artery's course.
Characterized by its S-shaped curvature as it traverses the cavernous sinus and enters the cranial
cavity. This configuration helps dissipate pulsatile energy, reducing stress on cerebral vessels, and
allows adaptability to cranial movements while maintaining cerebral perfusion.

subject: Anatomy
question:
A 60-year-old male recovering from facial nerve palsy complains of food getting stuck
between the cheek and teeth while eating. What is the muscle affected in this case?
explanation:
This patient, recovering from facial nerve palsy, getting food stuck between the cheek and teeth
while eating, is because of the buccinator being affected.
The buccinator is a muscle of facial expression. It is innervated by the facial nerve. It is known as
the whistling muscle.
The buccinator muscle is primarily responsible for keeping food between the teeth during
mastication by pressing the cheek against the teeth (involved in flattening the cheeks against the
gums and teeth).
In facial nerve palsy, paralysis of the buccinator muscle can lead to food accumulating in the
cheek pouch, as the muscle is unable to contract properly.
Other options:
Option B: Levator Labii Superioris: This muscle elevates the upper lip and contributes to facial
expressions like showing the upper teeth and dilates the nostrils. It is not involved in keeping food
in place during chewing.
Option C : Zygomaticus Major: Responsible for pulling the corner of the mouth upward and
outward, contributing to smiling. It does not influence food placement during mastication.
Option D : Orbicularis Oris: Encircles the mouth and helps in closing the lips. While it is involved
in mastication and speech, its paralysis would not cause food to accumulate in the cheek.

subject: Anatomy
question: Which of the following is not a derivative of neural crest cells?
explanation:
Retinal pigment epithelium is not a derivative of neural crest cells.
Retinal pigment epithelium (RPE) is derived from the neuroectoderm. The RPE develops from the
outer layer of the optic cup, a structure that arises from the neural tube (neuroectodermal origin),
not from neural crest cells.
Neural crest cells are a group of cells that migrate during embryonic development and give rise to
various structures, including parts of the nervous system, face, and heart.
Neural crest cells (NCC) are present adjacent to the neural tube and are sometimes referred to as
the fourth germ layer. It contributes to the formation of many organs and tissues:
In the trunk region, they form:
• Melanocytes (Option D)
• Sensory ganglia
• Sympathetic and enteric neurons
392
• Schwann cells
• Adrenal medulla cells
In the cranial region, they contribute to the:
• Craniofacial skeleton
• Neurons for cranial ganglia-Ciliary ganglion  (Option C)
• Ciliary muscles  (Option A)
• Satellite glial cells
• Melanocytes
Some of the other neural crest derivatives are:
• C cells of the thyroid gland
• Conotruncal septum in the heart
• Odontoblasts
• Smooth muscle cells to blood vessels to the face and forebrain.

subject: Anatomy
question: The cranial component of the neural crest cells leads to the formation of?
explanation:
The cranial component of neural crest cells (NCC) leads to the formation of Conotruncal septum.
The conotruncal septum is also known as the aortopulmonary septum. The formation of the
aortopulmonary septum in the truncus arteriosus divides the outflow channel of the heart into the
ventral aorta and the pulmonary trunk. Persistent truncus arteriosus results when the conotruncal
ridges fail to form.
The development of the heart begins in the third week of embryogenesis with the formation of the
cardiogenic mesoderm. The heart tube forms through fusion of paired endocardial tubes, which
then undergo looping and septation to establish the four chambers. By the end of the eighth week,
the heart achieves its definitive structure, with separate atria, ventricles, and major vessels.
The heart develops from mesodermal origins, with different parts deriving from specific
embryonic structures:
• First Heart Field (Primary Heart Field): Forms the left ventricle and part of the atria.
• Second Heart Field: Contributes to the right ventricle, outflow tract (conus arteriosus and
truncus arteriosus), and part of the atria.
• Neural Crest Cells: Contribute to the septation of the outflow tract and the formation of the
aorticopulmonary septum.
• Proepicardium (Epicardial Cells): Forms the epicardium and contributes to coronary vessels.
• Sinus Venosus: Forms part of the right atrium (sinus venarum) and the coronary sinus.
• Endocardial Cushions: Form the atrioventricular valves and contribute to septation.
Other options:
393
Option A: SA node is derived from the primary heart field - sinus venosus.
Option B: AV cushions are derived from adjacent endocardial cells. The endocardial cells along
with the epicardium and mesenchyme, form the AV cushions.
Option D: Chordae tendineae are formed from mesenchymal tissue within the heart.

[Anatomy-INI-CET-2024-18-exp-image]

subject: Anatomy
question:
A 15-year-old boy underwent splenectomy for immune thrombocytopenic purpura. During the
procedure, the surgeon noticed bleeding while separating the spleen from the stomach.
Which vessels are most likely implicated in this bleeding?
explanation:
This patient, undergoing splenectomy, while separating the spleen from the stomach, is likely
bleeding from the short gastric vessels.
In an open splenectomy, the gastrosplenic ligament is opened and short gastric vessels are ligated
and divided.
The spleen is attached to the stomach through the gastrosplenic ligament. The gastrosplenic
ligament attaches the greater curvature of stomach to the upper pole of the spleen.
394

[Anatomy-INI-CET-2024-19-exp-image]
Contents of the gastrosplenic ligament include:
• Short gastric vessels
• Left gastroepiploic vessels
Hence, while separating the spleen from the stomach, there are maximum chances of bleeding if
there is injury to the short gastric vessels.
Other Options:
Option A: Left gastric vessels: Left gastric vessels run along the lesser curvature of the stomach. It
is unlikely to be the source of bleeding in this case.
Option C: Right gastro-epiploic vessels: The right gastroepiploic vessels run along the pylorus and
inferior region of the greater curvature of the stomach. It is not a part of the gastrosplenic
ligament.
Option D: Splenic vessels: The splenic artery and vein run along the superior border of the
pancreas. They are not directly involved in the separation of the spleen from the stomach.

subject: Anatomy
question:
Identify the marked structure

[Anatomy-INI-CET-2024-20-image]
explanation:
The marked structure is the primitive streak.
The primitive streak appears in the early stages of gastrulation, a process that reorganizes the
epiblast into three germ layers: ectoderm, mesoderm, and endoderm. It appears as a linear
thickening on the dorsal surface of the epiblast in the bilaminar embryonic disc. It is found in the
caudal (tail-end) region of the developing embryo.
395

[Anatomy-INI-CET-2024-20-exp-image]
Functions of the primitive streak:
• Serves as the site where cells migrate inward to form the mesoderm and endoderm.
• Marks the beginning of gastrulation.
• Defines the body axis (head-to-tail, left-to-right).
Other Options:
Option B: Notochord: A rod-like structure derived from mesoderm that forms after the primitive
streak and is crucial for the development of the neural tube. Located cranially relative to the
primitive streak.

[Anatomy-INI-CET-2024-20-exp-image]
Option C: Neural Groove: A depression in the ectoderm that forms during neurulation, leading to
the development of the neural tube. It appears after the primitive streak.
Option D: Secondary Neural Plate: Part of the neural plate involved in the later stages of neural
tube development. It is not directly related to the primitive streak.
396

subject: Anatomy
question:
Which of the following is the first to be formed by the migration of the epiblast along the
primitive streak?
explanation:
Endoderm is the first to be formed by the migration of the epiblast along the primitive streak.
Gastrulation is when the single-layered blastula reorganizes into a multi-layered structure called
the gastrula. This process establishes the three primary germ layers that give rise to all tissues and
organs of the body. During gastrulation, epiblast cells migrate through the primitive streak to
form the three germ layers: endoderm, mesoderm, and ectoderm.
Cells from the epiblast migrate inward (invagination) through the primitive streak.
The first group of migrating epiblast cells replaces the hypoblast to form the definitive endoderm.
The endoderm gives rise to structures like the gastrointestinal tract, liver, and lungs.
Subsequent migrating cells spread between the epiblast and the newly formed endoderm to create
the mesoderm, which forms structures like muscles, bones, and the cardiovascular system.
Remaining epiblast cells that do not migrate through the primitive streak form the ectoderm,
which gives rise to the epidermis and the nervous system.

[Anatomy-INI-CET-2024-21-exp-image]
Other Options:
Option B: Cardiac Mesoderm: The mesoderm forms after the endoderm and differentiates later
into subtypes, including the cardiac mesoderm.
Option C: Epidermis: The epidermis arises from the ectoderm, which forms after both the
endoderm and mesoderm.
Option D: Neural Groove: The neural groove is part of the ectoderm and forms during
neurulation, which occurs much later than the formation of the endoderm.

subject: Anatomy
question:
During walking, the hip bone on the side of the suspended leg is raised by the action of which
muscle present in the supporting leg?
explanation:
397
Gluteus Medius of the supporting leg is responsible for raising the hip bone on the side of the
suspended leg.
Actions of Gluteus Medius:
•  Hip abduction
•  Stabilizes the pelvis during gait.
•  Assists the internal rotation of the thigh.
Gluteus medius and gluteus minimus act as the abductors of the hip joint. Stabilizing the pelvis on
the femur during walking is done by these muscles. It is demonstrated by asking the subject to
stand on one limb.
Trendelenburg Sign:
Trendelenburg sign is a clinical sign used to assess the function of the gluteus medius.
It is positive when the pelvis drops on the opposite side of the stance leg, indicating weakness or
dysfunction of the gluteus medius on the supporting leg.
In a healthy individual, the gluteus medius contracts to prevent the pelvis from tilting when one
leg is lifted. If the gluteus medius is weak or paralyzed, the pelvis tilts toward the side of the
suspended leg.

[Anatomy-INI-CET-2024-22-exp-image]
Option B: Gluteus Maximus: Extends and externally rotates the hip; key for climbing stairs and
rising from sitting. Weakness causes difficulty in hip extension, affecting activities like standing
up or climbing.
Option C: Obturator Internus: Externally rotates and stabilizes the hip joint; abducts the thigh
when the hip is flexed. Dysfunction can impair hip rotation, commonly affected in pelvic injuries.
Option D: Quadratus Femoris: Externally rotates and adducts the hip. Injury or entrapment of the
nerve to quadratus femoris can cause deep hip pain and weakness in rotation.
398

subject: Anatomy
question:
Identify the marked structure?

[Anatomy-INI-CET-2024-23-image]
explanation:
The structure shown in the above image is the oculomotor nerve. The oculomotor nerve (CN III)
passes through the superior orbital fissure, which lies within the region of the cavernous sinus.

[Anatomy-INI-CET-2024-23-exp-image]
Option B: The abducens nerve (CN VI) also passes through the cavernous sinus and superior
orbital fissure. However, it is located more medially in the sinus and the fissure compared to the
oculomotor nerve.
Option C: The trochlear nerve (CN IV) passes through the superior orbital fissure as well, but it is
the smallest cranial nerve and follows a higher and more lateral path than the oculomotor nerve.
Option D: The ophthalmic division of the trigeminal nerve enters the lateral wall of the cavernous
sinus, below the oculomotor nerve and trochlear nerve.

[Anatomy-INI-CET-2024-23-exp-image]
399

subject: Anatomy
question: Injury to the posterior interosseous nerve leads to?

options.b:
Loss of extension of the wrist
376

explanation:
Injury to the posterior interosseous nerve leads to loss of extension of digits.
The posterior interosseous nerve (PIN) is a continuation of the deep branch of the radial nerve. It
originates from the radial nerve in the cubital fossa, near the lateral epicondyle of the humerus.
After passing through the supinator muscle, it transitions into the PIN.
The PIN is purely a motor nerve and supplies the posterior compartment of the forearm.
Clinical correlation based on location of lesion:
Monteggia fracture involves a fracture of the proximal ulna with a dislocation of the radial head.
The radial nerve, especially its posterior interosseous branch, is at risk due to its proximity to the
fracture site and leads to loss of extension of digits.
