subject: Anatomy
question: An absent gag reflex can result from injury to which of the following nerves?
explanation:
An absent gag reflex can result from injury to the glossopharyngeal nerve (IX) and the vagus nerve
(X).
The afferent nerve for the gag reflex is the glossopharyngeal nerve, while the efferent nerve
consists of both the glossopharyngeal (to the stylopharyngeus muscle) and vagus. Both these
nerves pass through the jugular foramen and damage to them may be the cause for loss of gag
reflex.
Pharyngeal branches of the glossopharyngeal nerve and vagus nerve form the pharyngeal plexus
of nerves. These plexes mainly supply the pharynx. It also has contributions from the superior
cervical ganglion.
Nerve supply by the pharyngeal branch of the vagus nerve are:
• All the muscles of the soft palate(except tensor veli palatini - supplied by mandibular division of
the trigeminal nerve)
405
• All the muscles of the pharynx(except the stylopharyngeus - supplied by the glossopharyngeal
nerve).
The glossopharyngeal nerve is rarely damaged alone. Lesions will result in loss of sensation of the
posterior third of the tongue, reduced gag reflex, loss of sensation over the ipsilateral soft palate,
etc. Motor fibres to the stylopharyngeus and parasympathetic secretomotor fibres to the parotid
gland are also supplied by the glossopharyngeal nerve. Sensory supply includes uvula, posterior
one-third of the tongue, tympanic cavity etc.
The main functions of IX and X can be tested together by assessing swallowing,
phonation/articulation and sensation from the pharynx/larynx.
Glossopharyngeal and vagus nerve examinations include:
• Observing the position and symmetry of the palate and uvula at rest and with phonation (“aah”).
• By assessing the strength of the cough

subject: Anatomy
question:
If a tumor compresses the structures passing through the opening marked in the given image,
which of the following sensations would be affected?

[Anatomy-NEET-2024-2-image]

options.c: Sensation in the anterior scalp and dorsum of the nose

explanation:
The marked foramen is the superior orbital fissure. If a tumour compresses the structures passing
through this foramen, it would impair sensation in the anterior scalp and dorsum of the nose, as
this would affect the frontal nerve and nasociliary nerve.
The superior orbital fissure is an important anatomical opening in the skull that allows several
critical structures to pass through. Among these structures, the frontal nerve and nasociliary
nerve are branches of the ophthalmic nerve, the first division of the trigeminal nerve (CN V1),
which is responsible for sensory innervation to parts of the face.
The frontal nerve provides sensation to the forehead, the anterior scalp, and the dorsum of the
nose, while the nasociliary nerve supplies sensation to parts of the nasal cavity. Therefore, if a
tumour or lesion compresses the structures passing through the superior orbital fissure, it would
likely affect the sensation in the anterior scalp and dorsum of the nose because these areas are
innervated by the branches of the trigeminal nerve that pass through this fissure.
Other options:
Option A: Sensation of the nasopharynx is innervated by other branches of the trigeminal and
glossopharyngeal nerve, not by structures passing through the superior orbital fissure.
Option B: While some sensation from the nasal cavity comes from the trigeminal nerve, the
specific branches affected by a lesion in the superior orbital fissure are more focused on the
anterior areas of the face.
Option D: Sensation of the chin is primarily innervated by the mandibular branch (CN V3) of the
trigeminal nerve, which passes through the foramen ovale, not the superior orbital fissure.

subject: Anatomy
question:
401
A chronic tobacco consumer went to the dental clinic with bleeding lesions on his tongue and
was diagnosed with carcinoma of the tongue. Which of the following papillae, which do not
have taste buds, is least likely to be involved in such a cancer?
explanation:
Among the different types of papillae on the tongue, the one that does not have taste buds is the
filiform papillae.
406
Filiform papillae are minute cylindrical or conical projections that are mainly present on the
dorsal part of the anterior 2/3rd of the tongue. The filiform papillae do not contain taste buds.
They are covered by keratinized stratified squamous epithelium.
Other papillae of the tongue are:
Circumvallate papillae are 8-12 in number which are large cylindrical structures. They form a
V-shaped row just in front of the sulcus terminalis. Usually, the entire papillae are covered by
non-keratinized stratified squamous epithelium. Taste buds are present in the lateral wall of these
papillae which surrounds the papillae circumferentially.
Fungiform papillae are large, rounded, and red and are mainly present on the lingual margins and
also on the dorsal surface of the tongue. They are covered by non-keratinized stratified squamous
epithelium. They are supplied by the facial nerve.
Foliate papillae are red, leaf-like mucosal ridges that are present bilaterally in two zones at the
sides of the tongue near the sulcus terminalis. They are covered by non-keratinized stratified
squamous epithelium.
A diagrammatic representation of the various papillae of the tongue is given below:

[Anatomy-NEET-2024-3-exp-image]
Below is the image showing the various parts of the tongue.
407

[Anatomy-NEET-2024-3-exp-image]
The given image depicts the histology image of filiform papillae:

[Anatomy-NEET-2024-3-exp-image]
The given image depicts the histology image of circumvallate papillae:
408

[Anatomy-NEET-2024-3-exp-image]
The given image depicts the histology image of foliate papillae:

[Anatomy-NEET-2024-3-exp-image]
The given image depicts the histology image of fungiform papillae:
409

[Anatomy-NEET-2024-3-exp-image]

subject: Anatomy
question:
Which artery is used for arteriovenous shunting with the great saphenous vein for
hemodialysis, and is located behind the medial malleolus and 2.5 cm in front of the
tendocalcaneus?
explanation:
The posterior tibial artery, located behind the medial malleolus and 2.5 cm in front of the
tendocalcaneus, that is used for arteriovenous (AV) shunting with the great saphenous vein for
hemodialysis.
The posterior tibial artery is situated behind the medial malleolus and approximately 2.5 cm in
front of the tendocalcaneus, making it a suitable site for creating an AV fistula with the great
saphenous vein. This location allows for optimal access and ensures that the shunt can handle the
necessary blood flow for effective dialysis with the great saphenous vein. However, they are not
commonly used for AV shunting in common practices.
Arteries used for AV shunting include the radial artery and the brachial artery in the upper arm,
which are frequently employed for hemodialysis access. In the lower leg, the anterior tibial artery
and the peroneal (fibular) artery are less commonly used for this procedure.
Other options:
Option A: In comparison, the anterior tibial artery is located on the anterior aspect of the leg and
runs down the front of the tibia, not behind the medial malleolus.
Option C: The peroneal (fibular) artery is situated on the lateral side of the leg, travelling
alongside the fibula, and is also not located behind the medial malleolus.
Option D: The fibular circumflex artery is a small artery and is not commonly used for
hemodialysis access. It is not a standard choice and does not fit the anatomical location described,
as it is a branch of the anterior tibial artery.

subject: Anatomy
question:
What is the outer covering of the condition shown in the image?

[Anatomy-NEET-2024-5-image]
explanation:
The diagnosis based on the given image is omphalocele and is covered by the amnion.
410
Omphalocele is a midline abdominal wall defect where the bowel and other solid viscera protrude
through the umbilicus. In this condition, the herniated organs are covered by both peritoneum
and an amnion. The umbilical cord inserts directly into this sac, which helps in identifying the
condition. The defect occurs due to the failure of the physiological hernia to return to the body
cavity by the 11th week of gestation.
The size of an omphalocele can vary significantly. It may be relatively small, containing only the
gut, or it can be classified as a giant omphalocele if it measures 4 cm or more in diameter,
encompassing the gut, liver, and other abdominal organs. The condition is often associated with
other congenital anomalies such as trisomies 13, 15, 18, or 21, Beckwith-Wiedemann syndrome,
exstrophy of the bladder or cloaca, and pentalogy of Cantrell.
Treatment for omphalocele involves covering the exposed organs to prevent fluid loss and protect
them from infection. Prophylactic antibiotics may be administered in case of rupture. Surgical
correction is usually performed once the baby is stable. In omphalocele, since the umbilicus is
covered by a reflection of the amnion, this membrane also covers the omphalocele itself,
differentiating it from other types of abdominal wall defects.

[Anatomy-NEET-2024-5-exp-image]
Note : Gastroschisis is a condition where the intestinal loops herniate through the defect in the
abdominal wall. The defect usually lies on one side of the umbilicus (right side commonly). The
loops of the bowel are not covered by amnion. It is associated with elevated alpha-fetoprotein
levels.

subject: Anatomy
question:
Identify the marked structure.

[Anatomy-NEET-2024-6-image]
explanation:
The marked structure in the image is the medial pectoral nerve.
The given image shows the medial pectoral nerve arising from the medial cord of the brachial
plexus. It also shows the axillary artery traversing inferior to the median pectoral nerve and the
thoracoacromial artery. The medial pectoral nerve and the thoracoacromial artery both contribute
to the neurovascular supply of the pectoral muscles.
411
After originating from the medial cord, the medial pectoral nerve passes anterior to the axillary
artery. This nerve is specifically known for its anatomical path of piercing the pectoralis minor
muscle before branching out to supply both the pectoralis minor and the pectoralis major
muscles.

[Anatomy-NEET-2024-6-exp-image]

subject: Anatomy
question:
A patient presents with sciatica and tenderness in the hamstring muscles. Which nerve
supplies the hybrid muscle that is likely to be spared in this condition?
explanation:
The adductor magnus is the hybrid muscle that is likely to be spared in this condition.
In sciatica, the sciatic nerve is typically affected, which branches into the tibial nerve and the
common peroneal nerve. The adductor magnus muscle is an example of a hybrid muscle with dual
innervation:
• The adductor part of the adductor magnus is innervated by the obturator nerve.
• The hamstring part of the adductor magnus is innervated by the tibial nerve.
During sciatica, the tibial nerve is often affected, which would impact the hamstring part of the
adductor magnus. However, because the adductor part of the adductor magnus is innervated by
the obturator nerve, it remains functional and is likely to be spared.
The obturator nerve arises from the anterior division of the ventral rami of L2-L4. Near the
obturator foramen, it divides into anterior and posterior branches.
• Anterior division supplies pectineus, adductor longus, adductor brevis, and gracilis
• Posterior division supplies adductor magnus, obturator externus. It also supplies adductor brevis
if the muscle is not supplied by the anterior division
The image given below shows the course of the obturator nerve.
412

[Anatomy-NEET-2024-7-exp-image]
Other options:
Option A: The common peroneal nerve is a branch of the sciatic nerve and can also be affected in
sciatica. It is responsible for innervating muscles in the anterior and lateral compartments of the
leg.
Option B: The tibial nerve is a branch of the sciatic nerve and is often affected in sciatica. It
supplies the hamstring part of the adductor magnus, which would be impacted during sciatica.
Option C: The femoral nerve is not involved in sciatica. It supplies the quadriceps and other
muscles in the anterior compartment of the thigh, and does not innervate the adductor magnus.

subject: Anatomy
question:
Where will you find the epithelium shown in the image?

[Anatomy-NEET-2024-8-image]
explanation:
The epithelium shown in the above image can be found in the ureter.
The ureter is lined by transitional epithelium (also called urothelium), similar to the urinary
bladder. This specialized epithelium allows stretching, which is crucial as the urine passes
through. The presence of transitional epithelium is characteristic of structures in the urinary
system, such as the ureters, bladder, and part of the urethra.
Given below is a cross-section of the ureter showing the transitional epithelium and the layer of
smooth muscle.
413

[Anatomy-NEET-2024-8-exp-image]
Other options:
Option A: Gall bladder: The gall bladder is lined by simple columnar epithelium, which facilitates
the absorption of water and electrolytes from bile.
Option B: Trachea: The trachea is lined by pseudostratified columnar ciliated epithelium with
goblet cells, which helps trap and remove particles from the respiratory tract.
Option D: Duodenum: The duodenum is lined by simple columnar epithelium with goblet cells,
aiding in digestion and nutrient absorption.

subject: Anatomy
question:
During the examination of a patient, a professor asks the patient to invert his foot and points
to a prominent tendon located behind the medial malleolus. Which tendon is being
highlighted?
explanation:
The tendon being highlighted here is of the tibialis posterior.
It passes behind the medial malleolus and is a key muscle responsible for foot inversion. The
tendon of this muscle becomes prominent in this area during foot inversion. Dysfunction of this
tendon can lead to flatfoot deformity.
The given image shows the tarsal tunnel and position of the tendon of tibialis posterior:
414

[Anatomy-NEET-2024-9-exp-image]
Other options:
Option A: Tibialis anterior lies anterior to the medial malleolus and aids in dorsiflexion and
inversion of the foot but is not located behind the medial malleolus.
Option B: Extensor digitorum is involved in extending the toes and runs anteriorly to the foot, not
behind the medial malleolus.
Option D: Extensor hallucis longus extends the big toe and runs along the anterior portion of the
foot, also not behind the medial malleolus.

subject: Anatomy
question:
An elderly woman reports difficulty in buttoning her shirt and increased clumsiness during
daily activities. On examination, she is unable to make an 'O' shape by opposing her thumb
and index finger. Which of the following muscles is most likely involved?
404
explanation:
The difficulty in buttoning the shirt and the inability to make the "O" shape with fingers suggest
the involvement of the abductor pollicis brevis muscle.
This is a case of anterior interosseous nerve (AIN) syndrome. The AIN is a purely motor branch of
the median nerve that affects specific muscles in the forearm, the flexor pollicis longus and flexor
digitorum profundus leading to difficulty in performing tasks requiring fine motor control, such as
the "OK" sign. Among the options provided, the abductor pollicis brevis is uniquely and
consistently innervated by the recurrent motor branch of the median nerve, making it susceptible
to dysfunction in conditions affecting the median nerve.
Other options:
Option A: The opponens pollicis is innervated primarily by the lateral terminal branch of the
median nerve, with some contributions from a branch of the deep terminal branch of the ulnar
nerve. While it plays a role in thumb opposition, it is not consistently affected in anterior
interosseous nerve (AIN) syndrome, which primarily impacts deeper flexor muscles like the flexor
pollicis longus. Therefore, it is less likely to be the cause of the patient’s inability to perform the
"OK" sign.
Option B: The palmar interossei muscles are innervated by the deep branch of the ulnar nerve.
Because they do not receive any innervation from the median nerve, they are not implicated in
415
AIN syndrome. Their primary function involves the adduction of the fingers.
Option C: The flexor pollicis brevis has dual innervation: the superficial head is supplied by the
recurrent branch of the median nerve, while the deep head is innervated by the deep branch of the
ulnar nerve. This dual supply makes it less likely to be solely affected in AIN syndrome.
416

subject: Physiology
question:
Given below is the demonstration of tetanization in the gastrocnemius muscle of a frog.
Identify the tetanising frequency.

[Physiology-AIIMS-2017-1-image]
explanation:
Tetanizing frequency demonstrated by the muscle is 33Hz. In the above graph, we know,
• The speed of the paper is 1000mm/sec.
• The y-axis represents millivolts, the x-axis represents time.
• The scale on the x-axis is 10mm.
• The area under the graph represents the muscle contraction.
Contraction period (latent period to maximum force of contraction) includes 3 scales = 30 mm.
As paper speed is 1000mm/sec,
Contraction period = 30mm = (30/1000) = 30 msec.
Tetanizing frequency = 1/contraction period = 1000/30 = 33Hz.
Tetanization
The features of muscle contraction can be demonstrated by eliciting single muscle twitches. The
adding together of individual twitch contractions to increase the intensity of overall muscle
contraction is Summation. Summation occurs in two ways:
• by increasing the number of motor units contracting simultaneously, which is multiple Iber
summations.
• by increasing the frequency of contraction, which is called frequency summation and can lead to
tetanization.
When the frequency of a contraction reaches a critical level, the successive contractions eventually
fuse together, and the whole muscle contraction appears to be completely smooth and continuous,
this is called tetanization.
Tetanization occurs in two ways:
• Complete tetanus occurs when no relaxation occurs between stimuli.
• Incomplete tetanus when periods of incomplete relaxation take place between the summated
stimuli.

[Physiology-AIIMS-2017-1-exp-image]
425

subject: Physiology
question: The oxygen carrying capacity of a 18 year old boy with haemoglobin of 14g/dl is
explanation:
The oxygen carrying capacity of a boy with haemoglobin 14g/dl is 18.
When blood is saturated with 100% O2, the haemoglobin is also 100% saturated and each gram of
normal haemoglobin contains 1.39 mL of O2.
However, blood normally contains some inactive haemoglobin derivatives, and the measured
value in vivo is thus slightly lower hence, each gram of haemoglobin in vivo contains 1.31 mL of
O2 (Hufner’s constant).
According to the above question, when Hb=14g/dl, O2= 1.31*14= 18.34
The oxygen-hemoglobin dissociation curve
• Demonstrates a progressive increase in the percentage of haemoglobin bound with oxygen as
blood Po2 increases, which is called the percent saturation of haemoglobin.
• Blood leaving the lungs and entering the systemic arteries usually has a Po2 of about 95 mm Hg,
the usual oxygen saturation of systemic arterial blood averages 97 per cent.
• Conversely, in normal venous blood returning from the peripheral tissues, the Po2 is about 40
mm Hg, and the saturation of haemoglobin averages 75 per cent.

[Physiology-AIIMS-2017-2-exp-image]

subject: Physiology
question:
417
A 30-year-old patient with respiratory volume of 500 mL. The intraplueral pressure measured
-4 before inspiration and -8 at the end of inspiration. The pulmonary compliance of the
patient is
explanation:
Pulmonary compliance of this patient is 125 mL/cm of H2O.
Compliance is developed due to the tendency for tissue to resume its original position after an
applied force has been removed.
426
After an expiration during quiet breathing, the lungs have a tendency to collapse and the chest
wall has a tendency to expand. The interaction between the recoil of the lungs and recoil of the
chest can be measured through a spirometer and a curve of airway pressure plotted against
volume is the pressure-volume curve of the total respiratory system (Ptr).
Compliance of the lung and chest wall is measured as the slope of the Ptr curve, or, as a change in
lung volume per unit change in airway pressure (∆V/∆P).
Compliance = Change in lung volume / Change in pressure
Compliance = 500/4
Compliance = 125 ml/cm H20

subject: Physiology
question: The function of the Golgi tendon organ is to _________

options.c: Sense changes in position of the muscle
options.d: Sense changes in length of the muscle

explanation:
Golgi tendon organ sense changes in muscle tension.
Golgi tendon reflex (Inverse myotatic/ Inverse stretch reflex) is disynaptic, is the opposite of the
stretch reflex.
When the tension in a contracted muscle is significantly elevated, the inhibitory effect from the
Golgi tendon organ leads to instantaneous relaxation of the entire muscle. This effect is called
the lengthening reaction.
It is a protective mechanism to prevent tearing of the muscle or avulsion of the tendon from its
attachments to the bone.
Note: Muscle spindles senses changes in muscle length (stretch).

[Physiology-AIIMS-2017-4-exp-image]

subject: Physiology
question:
Difference in trajectory between inspiratory loop and the expiratory loop in the curve is due
to:

[Physiology-AIIMS-2017-5-image]

options.a: Difference in alveolar pressure during inspiration and expiration
options.b:
Difference in conc. of surfactant during inspiration and expiration
418
options.c: Difference in airway resistance during inspiration and expiration
options.d: Inspiration is active and expiration is passive

explanation:
The difference between the inflation and the deflation paths is called hysteresis.
It exists because a greater transpulmonary pressure is required to open a previously closed
airway, due to a deficit of surfactant at the air-water interface, than to keep an open airway from
closing, as then it has abundant surfactant.

subject: Physiology
question: Reflex responsible for tachycardia during right atrial distension is:
explanation:
Reflex responsible for tachycardia during right atrial distension is Bainbridge Reflex.
The stretch receptors of the atria that elicit the Bainbridge reflex transmit their afferent
signals through the vagus nerves to the medulla of the brain. Then efferent signals are transmitted
back through vagal and sympathetic nerves to increase heart rate and strength of heart
contraction. Thus, this reflex helps prevent damming of blood in the veins, atria, and
pulmonary circulation.
Other options:
Option A: Bezold–Jarisch Reflex: Activation of chemosensitive vagal C fibers in the
cardiopulmonary region causes profound bradycardia, hypotension and a brief period of apnoea
followed by rapid shallow breathing.
Option C: Cushing Reflex: When intracranial pressure is increased, the blood supply to
Rostral Ventro-Lateral Medulla (RVLM) neurons is compromised. The local hypoxia and
hypercapnia cause an increase in discharge from the neurons, producing hypertension,
bradycardia, and irregular respiration.
Option D: J Reflex: The stimulation of the J-receptors (C receptors/juxta-pulmonary capillary
receptors) causes apnea followed by rapid breathing, bradycardia, and hypotension.
428

subject: Physiology
question:
Feed forward control system is employed during the regulation of
419
explanation:
Feed forward control system is employed during regulation of temperature.
Feed forward control
429
There is a control system in our body when no stimulus is required but still the system anticipates
and makes corrective changes.
Such a system is called Feed forward or Anticipatory or Adaptive Control.
Examples include
• Temperature control – Thermoregulatory responses are initiated by hypothalamus BEFORE the
changes in environmental temperature have succeeded in changing the body’s core body
temperature
• Cephalic phase of gastric secretion- Just thinking about food increases gastric acid production
• Thinking about exercise itself increases heart rate & respiratory rate
• Role of cerebellum in motor coordination

subject: Physiology
question: Cellular junctions present in a cardiac muscle are all except:
explanation:
Zonula occludens is the other name for the tight junction. It is absent in a cardiac muscle.
Cardiac muscle cells are connected by Fascia adherens (a structural analog of zonula
adherens), Gap junctions and Macula adherens.
Cardiac muscle fibers are made up of many individual cells connected in series and parallel to one
another. The areas where the end of one muscle fiber abuts on another are called intercalated
disks. At each intercalated disc, the plasma membrane fuses with one another forming gap
junction which provides low resistance bridges for the spread of excitation from one fiber to
another permitting the cardiac muscle to function as a syncytium.

subject: Physiology
question: Efferent arteriolar constriction causes all except:

options.c: Decreases oncotic pressure in glomerular capillaries
options.d: Increases hydrostatic pressure in glomerular capillaries

explanation:
Efferent arteriolar constriction increases oncotic pressure in glomerular capillaries.
Efferent arteriolar constriction has a biphasic response on GFR depending on the severity of
resistance.
Moderate efferent arteriolar constriction causes increased glomerular hydrostatic pressure and
slightly increased GFR.
However, in cases of severe efferent arteriolar constriction (more than threefold increase in
efferent arteriolar resistance), the renal blood flow is significantly reduced. This results in
increased plasma protein concentration, thereby increasing the glomerular colloid oncotic
pressure. As the increase in colloid oncotic pressure exceeds the increase in hydrostatic pressure,
the net force for filtration decreases, resulting in decreased GFR.
430

[Physiology-AIIMS-2017-10-exp-image]

subject: Physiology
question: Absolute refractory period is due to:

options.c: Closure of active gates of sodium channel
options.d: Closure of inactive gates of sodium channel

explanation:
Absolute refractory period is due to closure of inactive gates of sodium channel.
Voltage-gated sodium channels have 3 states that you can find them in. They can either be closed,
open, or inactive.
When the stimulus causes activation of the open gates, sodium rushes into the cell causing the
membrane potential to become more positive. After 0.5 to 1 millisecond, the inactive gate
automatically closes and no stimulus, no matter how strong, can excite the nerve. This is called
the absolute refractory period.

[Physiology-AIIMS-2017-11-exp-image]
431

subject: Physiology
question:
X, Y, Z are three permeable ions. X = -50 and Y = -30. At RMP, if there is no net electrogenic
transfer, what is the value of Z?
420
explanation:
No net electro genic transfer at RMP means X + Y + Z = 0
Since X = -50 and Y = -30, then Z must be +80 since (-80 +80 = 0)

subject: Physiology
question: Slow waves are generated by:
explanation:
The interstitial cells of Cajal undergo cyclic changes in membrane potential due to unique ion
channels that periodically open and produce inward (pacemaker) currents that generate
slow-wave activity.
Interstitial cells of Cajal are the pacemaker cells of the GIT that initiate the basic electrical
rhythm (BER). These are stellate mesenchymal pacemaker cells with smooth muscle-like features.
In the stomach and the small intestine, these cells are located in the circular smooth muscle layer
near the myenteric plexus. In the colon, they are at the submucosal border of the circular muscle
layer.
The slow waves usually do not by themselves cause muscle contraction in most parts of the
gastrointestinal tract, except perhaps in the stomach. Instead, they mainly excite the appearance
of intermittent spike potentials, and the spike potentials in turn actually excite the muscle
contraction.
Note: Gastrointestinal stromal tumors arise from the interstitial cells of Cajal.

subject: Physiology
question:
A patient has a Iblood glucose level of 200 mg/dL and GFR of 90 ml/min. What is the
amount of glucose excreted if the transport maximum of the patient is as shown below?

[Physiology-AIIMS-2017-14-image]
explanation:
The amount of glucose excreted in the above scenario is 30 mg/min.
Calculation:
Step 1: Calculate total filtered glucose:
In this question,
• GFR = 90 ml/min
• Plasma glucose concentration = 200 mg/dL= 2 mg/ml (1 dL=100 ml).
• Total filtered glucose = GFR X Plasma glucose concentration.
= 90 ml/min X 2 mg/ml
= 180 mg/min.
Step 2: Check transport maximum:
In the above question transport maximum is 150 mg/min.
Step 3: Calculate the excretion:
Excretion = Total filtered glucose - Total reabsorbed glucose (Transport Maximum)
= 180-150 mg/min
432
= 30 mg/min

subject: Physiology
question: Which of the following can be responsible for Bezold-Jarisch reflex?
explanation:
Serotonin can be responsible for Bezold–Jarisch reflex.
Activation of chemosensitive vagal C fibers located in the cardiopulmonary region (ex: ventricles,
atria, great veins, pulmonary artery, juxtacapillary region of alveoli) causes profound bradycardia,
hypotension, and apnea followed by rapid shallow breathing. This response is called
Bezold-Jarisch reflex.
Other substances that can cause Bezold–Jarisch reflex are capsaicin, phenyl biguanide, and
veratridine.

subject: Physiology
question:
Which of the following is the constituent of the marked area in the electron microscopic
picture of muscle?

[Physiology-AIIMS-2017-16-image]
explanation:
Alpha actinin is the constituent of the marked area.
The image depicts sarcomere and the arrow points towards Z-line. Alpha actinin binds actin to
Z-lines.

[Physiology-AIIMS-2017-16-exp-image]
The orderly arrangement of actin, myosin, and related proteins that produces this pattern. The
area between two Z-lines is called sarcomere.
The thick filaments, which are about twice the diameter of the thin filaments, are made up of
myosin, which are lined to form A bands; the thin filaments are made up of actin, tropomyosin,
and troponin. The array of thin filaments extends out of the A band and into the less dense
staining I bands.
The lighter H bands in the center of the A bands are the regions where, when the muscle is
relaxed, the thin filaments do not overlap the thick filaments.  The Z lines allow for anchoring of
433
the thin filaments.
Actinin, titin and desmin are additional structural proteins.Titin connects Z-lines to the M line
and provides the scaffolding to the sarcomere.  Desmin adds structure to Z-lines by connecting
Z-line to plasma membrane.
Note: Some sources say that even titin is a component of Z-disc so if alpha actinin is not given in
the options titin is the second best answer.

[Physiology-AIIMS-2017-16-exp-image]

options.a: Closure of aortic and pulmonary valves
options.b:
Vibrations in the ventricular wall during systole

options.d: Closure of mitral and tricuspid valves

The fourth heart sound is caused by ventricular filling.
A fourth sound is heard immediately before the first sound when atrial pressure is high or the
ventricle is stiff in conditions such as ventricular hypertrophy. It is due to ventricular filling and is
rarely heard in normal adults.

explanation:
"a" wave in JVP represents atrial contraction.
Waves of JVP :
• a- atrial systole pushing the blood to the ventricles.
• c- isovolumetric contraction of ventricles causing the tricuspid valve to bulge.
• x'- fall in atrial pressure during ventricular systole due to pull of valve leaflets.

subject: Physiology
question:
In the 2 vessels shown below, assume the pressure along both the vessels is the same and
both follow linear flow pattern. What will be the amount of blood flow in A compared to B

[Physiology-AIIMS-2017-19-image]
explanation:
The relationship between the flow in a long narrow tube, the viscosity of the fluid, and the radius
of the tube is expressed mathematically in the formula called as Poiseuille's law :
435

[Physiology-AIIMS-2017-19-exp-image]
Blood flow (F) inside the vessel depends on:
• Pressure difference: directly proportional
• Viscosity: inversely proportional
• Radius: directly proportional to the fourth power
• Length of the vessel: inversely proportional
Here, the radius is doubled so the flow increases by 2 to the power of 4 = 16.
the length of the vessel is also doubled, so the flow decreases by half (÷2) = 8

question:
423
A 13-year-old boy presents with involuntary dancing movements of his right hand. He was
diagnosed with rheumatic fever. What constitutes the ganglia involved in the presenting
complication?
explanation:
The given clinical scenario is suggestive of Sydenham's chorea, a complication of rheumatic fever.
The choreic movements are caused by the destruction of D2 neurons of the striatum. The
neostriatum consists of the caudate nucleus and putamen.

subject: Physiology
question: Which of the following is the diluting segment of the kidney?
explanation:
The ascending thick limb of the loop of Henle is called the diluting segment.
In the ascending limb, there is the movement of Na+ and Cl– out of the tubular lumen, and the
tubular fluid becomes more dilute.
When the fluid reaches the top of the ascending limb, it becomes hypotonic to plasma.

subject: Physiology
question:
Identify the ions A and B based on conductance graph of a neuron shown below:

[Physiology-AIIMS-2018-2-image]
explanation:
Channel A: Na+, Channel B: K+
Na+ channels are fast activating and are responsible for depolarization phase of action potential.
447
K+ channels are slow activating and open for prolonged duration than the sodium channels. They
are responsible for repolarization phase of action potential. Also, the postassium channels return
slowly to their closed state, which is responsible for after-hyperpolarization.

[Physiology-AIIMS-2018-2-exp-image]
The sequence of events during the action potential occur in 7 steps as described below.
1. Resting state - Both channels are closed
2. Na+ channels open in response to depolarizing stimulus. Sodium enters into the cell. The
membrane reaches threshold potential.
3. The sodium influx causes opening of more sodium channels through positive feedback
mechanism. Rapid depolarization occurs.
4. Sodium conductance decreases.
5. Na+ channels close and K+ channels open. Repolarization occurs.
6. Sodium channels are reset, and are ready to respond to another depolarizing stimulus (relative
refractory period). During this phase, the potassium channels remain open and slowly return to
closed state causing after-hyperpolarization.
7. Both channels are closed. The membrane returns to RMP.
448

[Physiology-AIIMS-2018-2-exp-image]

subject: Physiology
question:
How is an electrical synapse different from a chemical synapse?
439

options.a: No cytoplasmic continuity between presynaptic and postsynaptic membrane
options.c: Connexons are involved in electrical synapse
options.d: Electrical synapse has more latent period than chemical synapse

explanation:
In electrical synapses, the cytoplasm of adjacent cells is directly connected by gap junctions
(connexons) that allow free movement of ions from the interior of one cell to the interior of the
next cell.
The transmission in electrical synapses is bidirectional and there is no synaptic delay. Unlike
chemical synapses, electrical synapse has cytoplasmic continuity.
Note:
Most of the synapses used for signal transmission in the central nervous system are chemical
synapses.

subject: Physiology
question:
Hormone degradation as an important mechanism for regulating local concentrations is seen
in _________.

options.c: Degradation of retinoic acid by Cyp26b1 in primordial germ cells of males

explanation:
Hormone degradation as an important mechanism for regulating local concentrations is seen in
all of the above.
11β-hydroxysteroid dehydrogenase inactivates glucocorticoids in renal tubular cells, preventing
actions through the mineralocorticoid receptor.
During development, degradation of retinoic acid by Cyp26b1 prevents the primordial germ cells
in the male from entering meiosis, which occurs in the female ovary.
The thyroid hormone deiodinases convert T4 to T3 and can inactivate T3. All the three are hence
examples of local hormone degradation being utilized as a method for regulation of hormone
concentration locally.
449

subject: Physiology
question:
The factors that can cause a person to drink water due to stimulation of thirst mechanism
is/are ___________.
explanation:
Dehydration, hemorrhage, and prandial drinking: All three can cause increased thirst resulting in
water drinking.
Increased osmolality acts via osmoreceptors in the anterior hypothalamus.
The decreased plasma volume, as in hemorrhage, acts via
1. Renin-angiotensin system
2. Baroreceptors in heart and blood vessels.
The intake of liquids during a meal is known as prandial drinking. The increase is known as
learned or habit response but is not understood completely.
The following factors are thought to play a role
1. Increase in plasma osmolality due to food absorption
2. Action on hypothalamus by one or more gastrointestinal hormones

subject: Physiology
question:
On performing a histamine test on an individual with brachial plexus trunk injury, you can
expect ________.

options.a: Central capillary dilatation → wheal → Surrounding flare
options.b: Central capillary dilatation → wheal without surrounding flare
options.c: Central capillary dilatation → wheal with exaggerated surrounding flare

explanation:
Brachial plexus trunk injury leads to anesthetized skin over the region. On performing a histamine
test on such an individual, one can expect central capillary dilatation → wheal without
surrounding flare. The incomplete triple response over the anesthetic skin is characteristic of
postganglionic lesions.
Intradermal injection of histamine usually causes a triple response in the surrounding skin i.e, red
reaction→ flare → wheal
• Red reaction is independent of innervation and is due to histamine-mediated relaxation of the
precapillary sphincters
• Flare reaction (Flush, Axon reflex) is due to Substance P mediated arteriolar
dilatation (antidromic conduction of afferent impulses via collaterals of the sensory nerve fiber
that lead to the release of vasoactive mediators)
• Wheal (skin edema) is due to histamine-mediated increased capillary permeability
• The triple response persists after total sympathectomy
• Flare is absent in locally anesthetized skin

subject: Physiology
question:
440
GFR is increased by all except
explanation:
Ureteric stone causes a reduction in glomerular filtration rate (GFR).
Following ureteral obstruction, there is a progressive fall in glomerular filtration rate (GFR) due to
a reduction in a single-nephron glomerular filtration rate (SNGFR) and a reduced number of
filtering nephrons.
All the other factors/options increase GFR.
450

subject: Physiology
question: Baroreceptor provides ________
explanation:
Baroreceptors provide negative feedback in response to an increase in blood pressure.
Increased baroreceptor discharge inhibits the tonic discharge of sympathetic nerves and excites
the vagal innervation of the heart.
Baroreceptor reflex
• Increased BP → stimulation of baroreceptors → stimulation of NTS → inhibition of RVLM →
inhibition of sympathetic system
• Stimulation of NTS also stimulates CVC → parasympathetic stimulation
• Net effect = sympathetic inhibition and parasympathetic stimulation → decreased HR and BP
NTS - Nucleus tractus solitarius
CVC - Cardiovagal center
RVLM - Rostral ventrolateral medulla
HR- Heart rate
BP - Blood pressure

subject: Physiology
question:
The diagram below shows the pressure-volume curves for 3 different lungs. Which of the
following is the best representation of compliance between the three lungs?

[Physiology-AIIMS-2018-9-image]
explanation:
• The pressure-volume relationship is the fundamental relation between the work of breathing and
lung mechanics.
• The diagram depicts the change in volume with respect to the change in intrapleural pressure,
which is compliance. Here, the change is more (steep curve) with B and least (flat curve) with C.
• The “stiffer” lung displays a more horizontal curve (C), meaning that it takes a greater pressure to
achieve the same inflation volume compared with a normal lung (A). These lungs are called less
compliant.
• Curve (B) is more compliant as it takes lesser pressure to achieve the same inflation volume
compared to normal (A).
Applied aspect:
Compliance is increased in emphysema. The mechanism is shown in the flow chart below.
451

[Physiology-AIIMS-2018-9-exp-image]
Compliance is decreased in case of pulmonary edema or fibrosis.

subject: Physiology
question:
Identify the site which is responsible for relieving pain when pressure is applied to the painful
area.

[Physiology-AIIMS-2018-10-image]
explanation:
It is a cross section of brain stem at pons.
• A is a periaqueductal gray matter which also consists of raphe nucleus.
• Descending pathways from the mesencephalic periaqueductal gray matter inhibit transmission
in nociceptive pathways. This descending pathway includes a synapse in the ventromedial medulla
(Raphe nucleus) and the release of endogenous opiates. So periaqueductal gray matter releases

Other marked structures are
B: Corticospinal tract
452

subject: Physiology
question: Inhibitory control by the hypothalamus is exerted on:
explanation:
Inhibitory control by the hypothalamus is exerted mainly on prolactin and growth hormone.
The major hypothalamic releasing hormones are:
• Thyrotropin releasing hormone (TRH)
• Corticotropin releasing hormone (CRH)
• Growth hormone releasing hormone (GHRH)
• Gonadotropin releasing hormone (GnRH)
The major hypothalamic inhibitory hormones are:
• Prolactin inhibitory hormone or dopamine
• Growth hormone inhibitory hormone (GHIH), also called Somatostatin.

subject: Physiology
question: Which of the following hormones play a permissive role in the attainment of puberty?
explanation:
The hormone that plays a permissive role in the attainment of puberty is leptin.
It now appears that leptin may be the link between body weight and puberty. Leptin is a
satiety-producing hormone secreted by adipocytes. High leptin levels reduce appetite and increase
energy expenditure.
Evidence for which is given by:
• Obese ob/ob mice that cannot make leptin are infertile, and their fertility is restored by
injections of leptin.
• Leptin treatment also induces precocious puberty in immature female mice.
• However, the way that leptin fits into the overall control of puberty remains to be determined.
Leptin also accelerates GnRH pulsatility in neurons, fastening puberty.

subject: Physiology
question: Weber-Fechner law relates magnitude of sensation to ____.
explanation:
Weber-Fechner Law states that the magnitude of sensation felt is proportionate to the log of the
intensity of the stimulus.
Other Laws in Physiology:
• Doctrine of specific nerve energies - When the nerve pathways from a particular sense organ are
stimulated, the sensation evoked is that for which the receptor is specialized no matter how or
453
where along the pathway the activity is initiated.
• Law of projection - No matter where a particular sensory pathway is stimulated along its course
to the cortex, the conscious sensation produced is referred to the location of the receptor. This
principle is called the law of projection.

subject: Physiology
question:
The following Darrow Yannet diagram is suggestive of which condition? Note : The solid line
is the original fluid compartment and the colour shaded compartment within the dotted line
denotes the change caused by the condition.

[Physiology-AIIMS-2018-14-image]
explanation:
In adrenal insufficiency there is hypo-osmotic volume contraction. Aldosterone deficiency leads to
increased sodium loss (loss of hyperosmotic fluid).
• The osmolarity of ECF decreases.
• ECF volume decreases because of the loss of volume.
• Water shifts into ICF; as a result of the shift, ICF osmolarity decreases until it is equal to ECF
osmolarity, and ICF volume increases.
• So, loss of hypertonic fluid causes hypotonic volume contraction.
Please note that in the diagram, the horizontal axis represents volume and vertical axis represents
osmolarity.

[Physiology-AIIMS-2018-14-exp-image]
454

[Physiology-AIIMS-2018-14-exp-image]
The Darrow Yannet diagrams of various conditions are shown above.

subject: Physiology
question: The mechanism by which glucose is absorbed in the intestine is:
explanation:
Glucose is absorbed in the intestine by secondary active transport.
On the basolateral side, there is active transport of sodium ions out of the cell into the interstitial
fluid. This depletes sodium inside the cell. Because of this Na+ concentration difference, more
Na+ binds to the SGLT on the luminal side. This increases the affinity of SGLT for glucose and
glucose binds to it.
Then, both the Na+ and glucose are transported together to the interior of the cell. Thus, glucose
is absorbed in the intestine by secondary active transport. The energy for glucose transport is
provided indirectly, by the active transport of Na+ out of the cell. The glucose moves out of the
cell into the blood across the basolateral membrane by facilitated diffusion mediated via GLUT.

[Physiology-AIIMS-2018-15-exp-image]
455

subject: Physiology
question: The earliest stage at which erythropoietin is effective in red blood cell formation is?
explanation:
BFU is the earliest stage of hematopoiesis where erythropoietin (EPO) acts
Under the influence of EPO, both BFU and CFU grow in semisolid culture media and give rise to
colonies of well-hemoglobinized erythroblasts.
Erythroid burst-forming unit (BFU-E) is the most primitive single lineage committed erythroid
progenitor. In addition to EPO, they form the sub-population of Erythroid Colony Forming Units
(CFU-E).
CFU is derived from burst-forming units.
Note: While CFUs have the highest number of receptors for EPO, the earliest stage at which it acts
is on the BFU.

subject: Physiology
question: Integrin connects with:
explanation:
Integrin connects with fibronectin.
Integrins are transmembrane glycoproteins (integral proteins) that allow cells to attach to
extracellular matrix constituents such as laminin, fibronectin and collagen. It helps in linking the
intracellular cytoskeleton functionally and structurally with the outside world.

subject: Physiology
question:
Which of the following organelles is absent in the area of the cardiac muscle marked:
444

[Physiology-AIIMS-2018-18-image]
explanation:
The area of the cardiac muscle marked is devoid of nuclei. Lysosomes are commonly observed in
perinuclear areas. Other organelles are present in abundance throughout the cell.

subject: Physiology
question: Which of the following fibres reach the Purkinje cell directly?
explanation:
The fibres of inferior olivary nucleus (climbing fibres) reach the purkinje cell directly.
Note:
The input from the Purkinje cells to the deep nuclei is inhibitory.
However, the deep nuclei output is always excitatory.

subject: Physiology
question:
Calculate the net filtration pressure across the GBM for a patient with Glomerular
ultrafiltration coefficient = 6 Glomerular hydrostatic pressure =45 Glomerular capillary
oncotic pressure =20 Bowman’s capsule hydrostatic pressure =10 Bowman’s capsule oncotic
pressure =0
explanation:
456
Net filtration pressure = Hydrostatic pressure in the glomerular capillaries - Pressure in Bowman's
capsule - Oncotic pressure in the glomerular capillaries.
Net filtration pressure= [PG -PB- πG+ πB]
Therefore,
Net filtration pressure      = (45-10-20+0)
= 25-10
= 15 mmHg
Note:
Here, just the net filtration pressure is asked and not the GFR.
GFR= Kf [PG -PB- πG+ πB]

subject: Physiology
question:
In a 100-m dash, most of the energy consumed by skeletal muscles to replenish ATP is
derived from _______
explanation:
In a 100-m dash that takes 10 s, 85% of the energy consumed is derived anaerobically
by phosphocreatine, which is hydrolyzed to creatine and phosphate groups with the release of
considerable energy.
The ATP concentration in a muscle is low enough (4 millimolar) to sustain contractions for 1-2
seconds after which ATP is dephosphorylated into ADP. The ADP is re-phosphorylated by
phosphocreatine for the next 5 to 8 seconds.
ADP is re-phosphorylated by 3 sources.
• Phosphocreatine after 2 seconds upto 10 seconds.
• Glycolysis (anaerobic) after 10 seconds upto a minute
• Oxidative metabolism - after a minute upto 2-4 hours.

subject: Physiology
question: In the homunculus, which of the following has maximum representation?
explanation:
In a homunculus, the more the representation of an area, the more precise the movements are.
• The relative sizes of the cortical receiving areas of the sensory homunculus are proportionate to
the use of the part.
• The sizes of these areas are directly proportional to the number of specialized sensory
receptors in each respective peripheral area of the body.
• Some body parts are represented by large areas in the somatic cortex—the lips the greatest of all,
followed by the face and thumb, whereas the trunk and lower part of the body are represented by
relatively small areas.
457

[Physiology-AIIMS-2018-22-exp-image]

subject: Physiology
question: What is the usual position of the Golgi apparatus in serous acinar cells?
explanation:
Golgi complex is located in supranuclear cytoplasm in serous acinar cells. Serous acinar cells are
generally found in pancreas and the submandibular salivary glands.
458

subject: Physiology
question: The function of the Golgi tendon organ is to _________

options.c: Sense changes in position of the muscle
options.d: Sense changes in length of the muscle

explanation:
Golgi tendon organ sense changes in muscle tension.
Golgi tendon reflex (Inverse myotatic/ Inverse stretch reflex) is disynaptic, is the opposite of the
stretch reflex.
When the tension in a contracted muscle is significantly elevated, the inhibitory effect from the
Golgi tendon organ leads to instantaneous relaxation of the entire muscle. This effect is called
the lengthening reaction.
It is a protective mechanism to prevent tearing of the muscle or avulsion of the tendon from its
attachments to the bone.
Note: Muscle spindles senses changes in muscle length (stretch).
463

[Physiology-AIIMS-2019 (May & Nov)-1-exp-image]

[Physiology-AIIMS-2019 (May & Nov)-1-exp-image]

subject: Physiology
question: Which is the first-order neuron in the optic pathway?
explanation: Photoreceptors - rods and cones are the first-order neurons in the visual pathway.

subject: Physiology
question: Pacemaker generating the rhythm for breathing is ________

options.b: Dorsal group of neurons in the medulla

explanation:
Pre-Botzinger complex generates the rhythm for automated breathing.
• These are also known as pacemaker cells located in the medulla.
• These are the first set of neurons that initiate inspiration.
464
• These neurons discharge rhythmically, and they produce rhythmic discharges in the phrenic
motor neurons.
Breathing is under the control of both the following:
• Voluntary control: Located in the cerebral cortex (via corticospinal tract)
• Automatic control: Driven by pacemaker cells in the medulla (Pre-Botzinger complex). These
activate motor neurons via cervical (Phrenic nerve innervating diaphragm) and thoracic spinal
cord (external intercostal muscle).
There is a reciprocal innervation to expiratory and inspiratory muscles in that motor neurons
supplying expiratory muscles are inactive when motor neurons supplying inspiratory muscles are
active, and vice versa.
Inspirati
on
Initiation
Pre-Botzinger complex (Pace
maker cells) in Medulla
Maintena
nce
Apneustic center in lower Po
ns stimulates Dorsal group of

Terminat
ion
Apneustic center inhibited by
 Vagus and Pneumotaxic cent
er
Regulation of expiration: During quiet breathing, expiration is a passive process; there is no firing
of neurons. Ventral respiratory group of neurons are involved in forceful expiration like during
exercise.
Mnemonic DIVE: Dorsal respiratory group for Inspiration and Ventral respiratory group for
Expiration.

subject: Physiology
question: Which of the following is not an example of feed forward control mechanism?

options.d: Exercise resulting in heart rate increase

explanation:
Exercise resulting in heart rate increase is not an example of feedforward control mechanism.
However, thinking about exercise and it resulting in a heart rate increase would be an example of
feedforward control mechanism.
Feedforward control is that which makes an anticipatory or adaptive response without a specific
stimulus.
Temperature control - Thermoregulatory responses are initiated by the hypothalamus before the
changes in environmental temperature changes the core body temperature. Thus, shivering in
response to cold environment would be an example of feedforward control.
In the cephalic phase of gastric secretion, the thought of food itself and not food per se produces a
gastric secretion.
The cerebellum has a feed-forward inhibition that limits the duration of the excitation produced
by any given afferent volley.
465

subject: Physiology
question: Single breath nitrogen method is used for measuring ____
explanation:
Anatomical dead space can be measured by the single-breath N2 curve (Fowler's method).
• Anatomical dead space is the respiratory system volume exclusive of alveoli.
• Alveolar dead space is the volume of gas not equilibrating with blood i.e. wasted ventilation.
• Physiological/total dead space is the sum of anatomical and alveolar dead space.
• The equation used for the calculation of total/physiological dead space is the Bohr’s equation.

[Physiology-AIIMS-2019 (May & Nov)-5-exp-image]
The patient takes a deep breath of O2 From mid-inspiration, then exhales steadily. The changes in
the N2 concentration of expired gas during expiration are plotted.
Region I - Dead Space (DS)
From I–II is a mixture of DS and alveolar gas.
The transition from III-IV is the closing volume (CV).
End of IV - Residual Volume (RV).
Factors affecting dead space:
Option B: Functional Residual Capacity (FRC) is measured using body plethysmography.
Option C: Tidal volume (TV) is measured using spirometry.
Option D: Diffusion Capacity is measured using carbon monoxide diffusion capacity (DLCO).

subject: Physiology
question: I-125 labelled albumin is used for measurement of:
explanation:
I-125 labelled albumin is the most commonly used substance for the measurement of plasma
volume.

subject: Physiology
question:
Which of the following hormones is exclusively secreted by adrenal gland but not by the
ovaries of females?
explanation:
DHEAS is exclusively secreted from the adrenals but not by ovaries because ovaries lack the
enzyme dehydroepiandrosterone-sulphotransferase needed to form DHEAS.
90% of DHEA is synthesised by the adrenals and 10% comes from the ovaries.

subject: Physiology
question: Which hormone crosses the plasma membrane for its action?
explanation:
Thyroxine crosses the plasma membrane for its action.
Thyroid hormones enter the nucleus and bind to thyroid hormone receptors. The
hormone-receptor complex in the nucleus binds to DNA via zinc fingers. This increases the
expression of a variety of different genes that code for proteins regulating cell function.  Although
thyroxine is an AA derivative, there are transporters to move it into the cell, hence they are
intracellular receptors acting on the nucleus.
Epinephrine, glucagon, and insulin bind to cell surface receptors.

subject: Physiology
question: Afferent fibers from baroreceptors for blood pressure control goes to?
explanation:
Afferent fiber for blood pressure control goes to the Nucleus Tractus Solitarius (NTS).
Increased BP stimulates the baroreceptors which provide negative feedback in response to the
increase in blood pressure.
Baroreceptor reflex:
467
• Increased BP → stimulation of baroreceptors → stimulation of NTS → excitation of CVLM →
inhibition of RVLM → inhibition of sympathetic system
• Stimulation of NTS also stimulates CVC → parasympathetic stimulation
• Net effect = sympathetic inhibition and parasympathetic stimulation → decreased HR and BP
NTS - Nucleus tractus solitarius
CVC - Cardiovagal centre
CVLM - Caudal ventrolateral medulla
RVLM - Rostral ventrolateral medulla
HR - Heart rate
BP - Blood pressure

subject: Physiology
question:
If the lung is allowed to undergo elastic recoil without the chest wall, the resultant volume of
the lung would be?
explanation:
If lung is allowed to undergo elastic recoil without the chest wall, the resultant volume of the lung
would be minimum volume as the opposing force by the chest wall would be non existant.

[Physiology-AIIMS-2019 (May & Nov)-10-exp-image]
The yellow line in the above graph denotes the compliance of lungs alone. According to the graph,
when the pressure (x axis)  inside the lung is zero, there is some volume of air lesser than FRC still
present (y axis) inside the lungs which is less than the functional residual capacity and residual
volume which can be called as minimum volume.

subject: Physiology
question: Myosin head detaches from actin by which of the following mechanism?

options.b: Entry of calcium into sarcoplasmic reticulum

explanation:
Binding of ATP on the free site of myosin leads to the detachment of myosin head from the thin
filament.
468

[Physiology-AIIMS-2019 (May & Nov)-11-exp-image]

subject: Physiology
question:
You are an intern and want to determine the quantity of IV fluids needed for a patient. How
much amount of transient loss/insensible loss per day would you account for in your
calculations?
explanation:
500-600 ml of transient loss/insensible loss per day would have to be accounted for in the
calculation.
Although water is lost through sweating especially in hot climates or during physical exertion, we
tend to lose water at rest as well. Physiologically, water evaporates from the skin and lungs at a
rate of about 600 to 700 ml/day. This is known as insensible water loss.

subject: Physiology
question:
A soldier posted in Siachen is brought to you in the ER. In order to rule out hypothermia,
how do you best measure his temperature?
explanation:
In the given clinical scenario rectal temperature would be the preferred site to measure the core
body temperature.
During low or high temperatures like in Siachen, the axillary and the oral temperatures are highly
misleading and should not be used to measure the core body temperatures
Infrared tympanic thermometer measures the temperature of the eardrum which is representative
of core body temperature. However, more often the thermometer is not inserted adequately or for
safety reasons, they are manufactured in such a way that they do not reach deep into the ear
canal, or the ear canal contains cerumen which can disturb the measurement. Hence the infrared
thermometer more commonly delivers the temperature of ear canal cerumen and hence not a
reliable method of measuring the core body temperature.

subject: Physiology
question: "Vasopressin escape" in SIADH results due to:
explanation:
Vasopressin escape in SIADH (Syndrome of inappropriate ADH secretion) results due to
downregulation of aquaporin 2 production.
469
Prolonged exposure to elevated levels of vasopressin (ADH) leads eventually to downregulation of
aquaporin-2 receptors. This prevents excessive reabsorption of water, despite high serum ADH
levels and hence maintains euvolemia. This is known as vasopressin escape. This helps prevent
excessive dilutional hyponatremia in SIADH.

subject: Physiology
question: Which of the following hormones do not act on the pancreas?
explanation:
Gastrin does not act upon the pancreas. Gastrin stimulates gastric acid secretion and gastric
mucosal growth.
GI hormones acting on the pancreas include:
GIP is also known as Glucose-dependent insulinotropic peptide.
CCK(Cholecystokinin)
Secretin
GIP(Gastric inhibitory peptid
e)
Stimulat

Pancreatic bicarbonate secret
ionGallbladder contractionGr
owth of exocrine pancreas
Pepsin secretionPancreatic bi
carbonate secretionBiliary bi
carbonate secretionGrowth of
 exocrine pancreas
Insulin release
Inhibits
Gastric emptying
Gastric acid secretion
Gastric acid secretion
