subject: Physiology
question:
A 65-year old patient visits his family practitioner for a yearly examination. On examination,
his BP was 190/100 mm Hg. His pulse pressure was 90 mm Hg. What would have decreased
resulting in this increase in pulse pressure?
explanation:
Pulse pressure increases with age due to decreased arterial wall compliance.
With advancing age, there is an increase in systolic blood pressure. Whilst diastolic blood pressure
falls after 60 years of age due to increased arterial stiffness (decreased compliance). As a result of
these two changes, pulse pressure increases (↑PP = ↑SBP - ↓DBP).

subject: Physiology
question:
Which of the following curves represents gamma-globin chain production?

[Physiology-AIIMS-2020 (May and Nov INI-CET)-2-image]
explanation:
In the given image, the Orange curve represents the gamma-globin chain production. Gamma
globin chain is produced in the fetal period and drops to undetectable levels 6 months postpartum
which is the pattern represented by the Orange curve.
Hemoglobin in blood has different variants throughout life, in different proportions. The type of
hemoglobin essentially depends on the combination of globin chains. Adult hemoglobin is made
of 2 alpha and 2 beta chains, whereas fetal hemoglobin is made of 2 alpha and 2 gamma-globin
chains. Other combinations of chains can be found in embryonic hemoglobin.
Here,
• Gamma chain - Orange curve
• Alpha chain - Red curve
• Beta chain - Green curve
• Epsilon chain - Black curve
• Delta chain - Blue curve

subject: Physiology
question:
471
Transcutaneous electric nerve stimulation is used to reduce pain. What is the mechanism
behind this?
explanation:
Transcutaneous electrical nerve stimulation (TENS) used for pain relief is based on the gate
theory of pain. This method uses electrodes to activate Aα and Aβ fibers near the injury to relieve
pain.
Gate control theory is based on the inhibition of pain pathway in the spinal cord. Various sensory
inputs from a particular dermatome reach the dorsal horn of the spinal cord. The pain is
transmitted via A-delta and C fibers, whereas, A-beta fibers carry tactile sensations (touch,
pressure) and A-alpha fibers carry proprioception.
When these large-diameter A-beta/A-alpha fibers are stimulated, they inhibit the pain pathway in
the spinal cord. This inhibition is thought to result from the local lateral inhibition of
interneurons in the spinal cord.
Gate control theory operates at the substantia gelatinosa of the dorsal horn of the spinal cord.
The given image depicts the gate control theory of pain:
479

[Physiology-AIIMS-2020 (May and Nov INI-CET)-3-exp-image]

subject: Physiology
question: Which of the following is not required for calculation of creatinine clearance?

options.d: Volume of serum passing through kidney in 24 hours

explanation:
The volume of plasma passing through the kidney in 24 hours is not used in the calculation of
creatinine clearance.
The parameters used to calculate creatinine clearance (CCr) are:
• Urine creatinine
• Serum creatinine
• 24hr urine output
The formula used to calculate creatinine clearance (CCr) is:
UCr × V ⁄ PCr  where,
UCr - Urine creatinine(mg/dl)
PCr - Serum creatinine(mg/dl)
V - Urine volume(ml/24 hrs)
Creatinine is a metabolite of muscle metabolism and is cleared from the body mostly by
glomerular filtration. Therefore, the clearance of creatinine is used to assess GFR.
Urine collection for the measurement of creatinine clearance may be subject to practical
constraints. Therefore, approximate changes in GFR can be obtained by measuring the serum
creatinine concentration, which is inversely proportional to GFR.

subject: Physiology
question: A neuron is receiving 400 inputs from 150 sensory neurons. This is an example of?
explanation:
A single neuron receiving 400 inputs from 150 sensory neurons is an example of convergence.
480
Convergence means signals from multiple inputs uniting to excite a single neuron. It can be of 2
types:
• Convergence from a single source
• Convergence from multiple sources
The importance of convergence is that neurons are almost never excited by an action potential
from a single input terminal. However, action potentials converging on the neuron from multiple
terminals provide enough spatial summation to bring the neuron to the threshold required
for discharge.

subject: Physiology
question:
Calculate the ECF volume in a volunteer in whom 10 gm of mannitol was injected in venous
blood. At equilibrium urinary excretion of mannitol was found to be 10% and the plasma
concentration of mannitol was 65 mg/100 ml.
explanation:
ECF volume of the above volunteer is calculated to be 14 L.
The ECF volume can be calculated using the formula:
Where,
• V = ECF volume
• Amount injected = 10 gm
• Amount of mannitol excreted = 10% of 10 gm = 1gm
• Plasma concentration of mannitol = 65 mg/100mL = 0.65 gm/L
Calculation:
ECF Volume = (10-1)/0.65
= 13.8 L = approximately 14 L
Mannitol cannot cross cell membranes, hence, it will be distributed in ECF but not in ICF. Thus
mannitol is a marker for ECF volume and therefore calculating the volume of distribution of
mannitol gives the value of ECF volume.

subject: Physiology
question: Gibbs-Donnan equilibrium is mainly due to?
explanation:
Gibbs-Donnan equilibrium is mainly due to intracellular proteins.
Donnan and Gibbs showed that "When an ion on one side of a membrane cannot diffuse through
the membrane, the distribution of other ions to which the membrane is permeable is affected in a
predictable way".
According to this, in the presence of intracellular proteins, which are negatively charged
non-diffusable ions, the distribution of other ions to which the membrane is permeable is affected
in a predictable way (Donnan Effect) so that at equilibrium their concentration ratios are equal.

subject: Physiology
question: Which of these hormones have intracellular receptors?
explanation:
481
Thyroxine hormone act by binding to intracellular nuclear receptors.
All other hormones mentioned in the options bind to receptors on the cell membrane.

subject: Physiology
question: Intercellular communication in eukaryotes is by?
explanation:
Intercellular communication in eukaryotes is by Exosome.
In the last decade, a class of small, heterogeneous, secreted vesicles, broadly termed
as extracellular vesicles have been identified.
These extracellular vesicles have been implicated as a new and important mediator of cell-cell
communication that likely contributes importantly to both normal and pathologic physiology.
These vesicles are enclosed by a lipid bilayer.
They are heterogeneous in size (30-2000 nm diameter), and are generated by at least two distinct
mechanisms:
• Microvesicles are generated by budding from the plasma membrane of a source cell.
• Exosomes are generated from the multivesicular body (MVB).
Note: The terms microvesicle and exosome are often lumped together as simply exosomes.

subject: Physiology
question: In skeletal muscle, dihydropyridine and ryanodine receptors are coupled by?
explanation:
In skeletal muscle, dihydropyridine and ryanodine receptors show mechanical coupling involving
structural interaction between the two.
Dihydropyridine receptors (DHPRs) are voltage-gated Ca2+ channels in the T tubule membrane.
The ryanodine receptor (RyR) is a ligand-gated Ca2+ channel with Ca2+ as its natural ligand.
Skeletal muscle excitation-contraction (E-C) coupling is thought to occur by a "mechanical
coupling" mechanism involving protein-protein interactions that lead to activation of the
sarcoplasmic reticulum ryanodine receptor by the voltage-sensing transverse (T-) tubule
dihydropyridine receptor.

subject: Physiology
question:
473
In negative feedback, feedback gain of infinity is seen in which of the following?
explanation:
In negative feedback, feedback gain of infinity could be seen in blood volume control by the
kidney.
When the blood volume falls below the equilibrium, the renal–body fluid mechanism causes the
blood volume returns back to the equilibrium point resulting in zero error and hence the gain of
kidney in its ability to regulate blood volume is infinity (∞).
The degree of effectiveness with which a regulatory system maintains constant conditions is
determined by the gain of the negative feedback system.
482
Gain= Correction/Error
Example: If an external or internal factor led to a decrease in mean blood pressure from 100 mm
Hg to 80 mm Hg. Activation of a negative feedback mechanism i.e. baroreflex leads to a
correction of blood pressure to 96 mm Hg. So in this case,
• Correction = 16 mm Hg
• Error = 4 mm Hg
• Gain = Correction/Error = 16/4 = 4
Note: A gain of 0 would imply a total failure of the regulatory system and a gain of infinity (∞)
would indicate complete restoration of the regulated variable.

subject: Physiology
question:
The image below shows simple muscle twitch along with the time trace, recorded in a frog
gastrocnemius muscle. Calculate the minimum tetanizing frequency. (The time tracer was
made with a 100Hz tuning fork).

[Physiology-AIIMS-2020 (May and Nov INI-CET)-12-image]
explanation:
The tetanizing frequency of the muscle, based on the given diagram, is 20 per second.
Tetanizing frequency = 1/contraction period. It is the frequency at which individual muscle fibres
twitch that fuse and cause sustained muscle contraction, where the contraction period is the time
taken from latent period to maximum force of contraction.
In this case, the time tracer was taken using a 100Hz tuning fork. Which means the tracer
recorded 100 waves per second.
Therefore, the duration of each wave in time tracer = 0.01 seconds.
In the image given in the question, between the point B (end of latent period) to point C
(maximum force of contraction), there are about 5 waves.
Hence, the duration of contraction = 5 x 0.01 = 0.05 seconds.
Substituting the value in the formula, we get:
Tetanizing frequency = 1/0.05 sec = 100/5 = 20/sec.

[Physiology-AIIMS-2020 (May and Nov INI-CET)-12-exp-image]
483
Tetanization:
The features of muscle contraction can be demonstrated by eliciting single muscle twitches. The
adding together of individual twitch contractions to increase the intensity of overall muscle
contraction is Summation. Summation occurs in two ways:
• by increasing the number of motor units contracting simultaneously, which is multiple Iber
summations.
• by increasing the frequency of contraction, which is called frequency summation and can lead to
tetanization.
When the frequency of a contraction reaches a critical level, the successive contractions eventually
fuse together, and the whole muscle contraction appears to be completely smooth and continuous,
this is called tetanization. Beyond this point, a further increase in the frequency, will not result in
increased force of contraction.
Tetanization occurs in two ways:
• Complete tetanus occurs when no relaxation occurs between stimuli.
• Incomplete tetanus when periods of incomplete relaxation take place between the summated
stimuli.

subject: Physiology
question: Least frequency of slow waves is seen in:
explanation:
Among the given options, the least frequency of slow waves is seen in the stomach (4/min).
The Basal electrical rhythm (BER) also called slow-wave, is a rhythmic fluctuation of membrane
potential in the gastrointestinal smooth muscle. It is initiated by the interstitial cells of Cajal. The
membrane potential fluctuates between -65mV and -45mV.
The BER is seen in all sites of gastrointestinal smooth muscle except in the esophagus and
proximal stomach.
The rate of BER in various sites is as follows:
• Stomach: 4/min
• Duodenum: 12/min
• Distal ileum: 8/min
• Cecum: 2/min
• Sigmoid colon: 6/min
The BER does not cause contraction of the smooth muscle. However, when the BER is
superimposed by the spike potentials, it increases muscle tension.
Acetylcholine causes an increase in the number of spike potentials and muscle
tension. Epinephrine causes a decrease in the number of spike potentials and muscle tension.
484

[Physiology-AIIMS-2020 (May and Nov INI-CET)-13-exp-image]
Muscle contraction can occur only when the motor stimulus is provided during the depolarisation
part of the BER.
Thus, BER does not cause muscle contraction by itself, but controls the rate and organization sites
of the muscle contractions, as in coordination of the peristaltic activity and setting the rhythm of
segmentation.

subject: Physiology
question: Macula densa is formed by which part of the nephron?
explanation:
Macula densa is formed by specialized tubular epithelial cells of the distal convoluted tubule.

[Physiology-AIIMS-2020 (May and Nov INI-CET)-14-exp-image]
The juxtaglomerular apparatus consists of the following parts:
• Macula densa: At the end of the thick ascending limb of Henle, and at the beginning of the distal
tubule, the tubular epithelial cells are specialized to form the macula densa. They detect sodium
485
concentration of the fluid in the tubule (chemoreceptor). In response to elevated sodium, the
macula densa cells trigger the contraction of the afferent arteriole, reducing the flow of blood to
the glomerulus and the glomerular filtration rate. This happens as follows: When there is
increased sodium, there is increased sodium-potassium ATPase activity which leads to increased
ATP hydrolysis causing more adenosine formation. The adenosine receptors on the macula densa
cells stimulate it to release more calcium, so that there is vasoconstriction of the afferent arteriole,
thereby decreasing the GFR.
• Lacis cells: Extraglomerular mesangial cells situated between afferent and efferent arteriole.
• Granular cells/JG cells: Modified smooth muscle cells of the afferent arteriole, they secrete
renin. Fall in pressure in afferent arteriole stimulates renin release (mechanoreceptor), which, in
turn, increases blood pressure.

subject: Physiology
question: As filtrate flows through PCT, the concentration of all of the following decreases except:
explanation:
As the filtrate flows through the proximal convoluted tubule, the concentration of the chloride
increases.
• The concentration of chloride in the early part of the pCT is ≈105 mEq/L.
• As the sodium is reabsorbed, it preferentially carries glucose, amino acids, and bicarbonate.
• This leaves behind the solute, that has a higher concentration of the chloride, which as it reaches
the second half of the PCT is ≈140 mEq/L.

subject: Physiology
question:
This is the graph depicting the filterability of dextran through the kidney, 1 representing
complete filtration and 0 representing no filtration. Choose the substance A, B, C
respectively.

[Physiology-AIIMS-2020 (May and Nov INI-CET)-16-image]
475
explanation:
In the given graph, the order of filterability is A>B>C, which likely indicates that the
substances are Polycationic, Neutral, Polyanionic.
The glomerular filtration barrier is negatively charged, which will repel most of the negative
charged (anionic substances). Whereas, the cationic substances are easily filtered.
Hence, the order of filterability would be: Polycationic > Neutral > Polyanionic, represented
by A, B, and C in the graph shown.
The glomerular filtration barrier consists of:
• Endothelium of the capillary
• Basement membrane:
• Lamina densa - electron-dense layer. It contains type IV collagen
• Lamina rara interna - contains glycosaminoglycan heparan sulfate which has a negative charge
• Lamina rara externa - contains glycosaminoglycan heparan sulfate which has a negative charge
• Layer of epithelial cells (podocytes) surrounding the outer surface of the capillary basement
membrane
486
The capillary endothelium is perforated by thousands of small holes called fenestrae, which
measure from 4 nm up to 8nm. Therefore, neutral substances with molecular diameters of less
than 4 nm are freely filtered, and filtration of those with diameters of more than 8 nm approaches
zero.
However, in addition to the size of the particles, the filtration also depends mainly on the charge
of the particle being filtered. The glomerular capillary wall consists of highly negatively charged
proteins called sialoproteins, which repel the negatively charged substances (anions). The
property can be studied by using charged dextran.
When the filtration of the negatively and positively charged dextrans was studied, the results
indicated that the negatively charged dextrans in the blood are repelled, with the result that
filtration of anionic substances 4 nm in diameter is less than half that of neutral substances of the
same size.
This explains, why albumin despite having an effective molecular diameter of approximately 7
nm, has a very low filtration rate.

subject: Physiology
question:
Calculate the respiratory quotient (RQ) in a patient with 50kg body weight, if the CO2 exhaled
is 200ml/min and O2 consumed is 250 ml/min.
explanation:
The respiratory quotient is 0.8.
The respiratory quotient is the ratio of carbon dioxide production to oxygen utilization.
In the given question, the CO2 production is 200mL/min, while the O2 consumption is
250mL/min.
Therefore,
Respiratory quotient = 200/250 = 0.8
The respiratory quotient (RQ) is used for determining the metabolic utilization of various foods.
• When carbohydrates are metabolized with one molecule of oxygen, one carbon dioxide molecule
is released. Therefore, the RQ of carbohydrates = 1.
• When fats are oxidized, for every 100 molecules of oxygen consumed, 70 molecules of CO2 are
released. Therefore, the RQ of fats is 0.70.
• Similarly, the respiratory quotient for proteins is 0.80.

subject: Physiology
question:
Which of the following types of amino acids are present in the transmembranous part of the
membrane receptor?
explanation:
The transmembranous part of the membrane receptor consists of hydrophobic amino acids.
A membrane protein can be either integral protein (spans the thickness of the cell membrane) or
peripheral protein (attached to the peripheral side of the membrane). The membrane-spanning
segment of integral proteins are usually hydrophobic alpha-helices.
The central core of the cell membrane is hydrophobic. Integral proteins have their hydrophobic
portions usually in the interior of the membrane and the hydrophilic portions towards the
surfaces. The presence of these hydrophobic amino acids in the interior is a special adaptation
487
that allows the protein to remain stable in the hydrophobic milieu of the cell membrane.

[Physiology-AIIMS-2020 (May and Nov INI-CET)-18-exp-image]

subject: Physiology
question: Which of the following is not a cause of secondary hyperparathyroidism?
explanation:
Parathyroid adenoma is a benign tumor of the parathyroid gland, which causes primary
hyperparathyroidism (not secondary).
Secondary hyperparathyroidism is characterized by high levels of parathyroid hormone, to
compensate for the low levels of serum calcium, due to some other underlying condition.
Causes of secondary hyperparathyroidism, include:
• Chronic kidney disease: The damaged kidneys are unable to produce sufficient amounts of the
active form of vitamin D, 1,25-dihydroxycholecalciferol. This further causes hypocalcemia and
triggers PTH secretion.
• Vitamin D deficiency: Causes hypocalcemia, which triggers the increased secretion of PTH.
• Lithium: May cause an increased set-point of calcium for parathyroid hormone suppression,
which leads to parathyroid hyperplasia.

subject: Physiology
question:
476
Which of the following events occur immediately when a person changes his/her posture from
supine to standing?

options.b: Vagal output to cardiac fibres increase

explanation:
There is a decreased firing from the baroreceptors.
• When a person stands up from a lying down position, it causes pooling of the blood in the legs,
due to gravity. This reduces the venous return and causes a decrease in cardiac output.
• Reduced cardiac output, leads to a fall in blood pressure, which in turn causes reduced firing
from baroreceptors.
• As a result, the vagal tone is suppressed leading to increased heart rate.
488

subject: Physiology
question: Which of the following statements is true about skeletal muscle contraction?
explanation:
During the contraction of the skeletal muscle, the Ca2+ is released from the sarcoplasmic
reticulum. Only statement 3 is correct.
Excitation contraction coupling refers to the process by which the membrane depolarization of the
muscle fibre (excitation) leads to the contraction of the muscle.
The steps of excitation-contraction coupling in skeletal muscle are as follows:
• Motor-neuron discharge causes the release of acetylcholine at the motor endplate, which binds
to the nicotinic acetylcholine receptors.
• This causes increased Na+ and K+ conductance in the endplate membrane, leading to the
generation of end-plate potential, which is a local potential.
• When the local endplate potential reaches a threshold for the muscle fibre, and an action
potential is generated.
• The generated action potential passes inward through the T tubules, leading to the spread of
depolarisation.
• The depolarization of the muscle fibre causes the release of Ca2+ from terminal cisterns of
sarcoplasmic reticulum and diffusion to thick and thin filaments.
• The calcium released, binds to troponin C, uncovering myosin-binding sites on actin.
• The formation of cross-linkages between actin and myosin and sliding of thin on thick filaments
causes contraction of the skeletal muscle.
Steps in relaxation:
• The cytoplasmic calcium is pumped back into the sarcoplasmic reticulum, by the action of
SERCA (sarcoplasmic endoplasmic reticulum calcium ATPase).
• Once the calcium concentration is lowered, the interaction between actin and myosin ceases, and
the muscle relaxes.
Other options:
Statement 1: Incorrect - Extracellular calcium levels play an important role in muscle contraction.
When there is a decrease in extracellular calcium as in hypocalcemia, the voltage-gated sodium
channels are activated, which causes sodium influx. The increased intracellular sodium
concentration causes sustained depolarization, which may lead to tetany.
Statement 2: Incorrect - Ca2+ is not released from mitochondria. It is released from the
sarcoplasmic reticulum.
Statement 4: Voltage-gated calcium channels on T-tubules (part of sarcolemma) are activated by a
depolarizing stimulus, which causes the release of calcium from the sarcoplasmic reticulum. The
increased calcium concentration will eventually lead to muscle contraction.

subject: Physiology
question:
Which of the following statements are correct regarding mechanisms of depolarisation,
repolarisation, and hyperpolarisation in a skeletal muscle action potential?
explanation:
489
Statements 1, 3, and 5 are correct.
Action potential in skeletal muscle:
• Motor-neuron discharge causes end-plate potential, which when reaches to a threshold value,
causes action potential in the muscle fibre.
• The depolarization of the muscle fibre is caused by the activation of the voltage-gated sodium
channels, which causes the influx of sodium.
• During repolarization, the sodium channels are closed, and voltage-gated potassium channels
open, which causes efflux of potassium.
• The voltage-gated potassium channels close slowly. As a result, the potassium efflux continues,
leading to hyperpolarization of the membrane.

[Physiology-AIIMS-2020 (May and Nov INI-CET)-22-exp-image]
The steps in an action potential in muscle fibre and the neuron, are similar except for the
quantitative differences, such as:
• RMP is -80 to -90 mV in skeletal muscle fibres, while it is about -70mV in neurons.
• The duration of the action potential in skeletal muscle is 1 to 5 msec, which is about 5 times
longer than in the largest myelinated nerve fibre.
• The velocity of conduction is about 3-5m/s, which is about 1/13th of the velocity of the nerve
fibre.

subject: Physiology
question: Arrange the following events in the sequence in which they occur, after 'p wave' in ECG:
explanation:
The correct sequence of events that occur after the p-wave is 1-2-4-3, ie:-
a wave in JVP → First heart sound  → T wave in ECG  → Rapid filling of ventricles.
The sequence of the events in this question can be better understood with the following diagram:
490

[Physiology-AIIMS-2020 (May and Nov INI-CET)-23-exp-image]
• The p wave in the ECG represents atrial depolarization, which causes contraction of the atria,
represented by the 'a' wave in the JVP. Atrial contraction causes final filling of the ventricles
(fourth phase of ventricular diastole)
• Atrial contraction is followed by the closure of the mitral valve, which causes the first-heart
sound.
• This is followed by the isovolumetric contraction of the ventricle, which causes c wave in JVP,
due to bulging of the tricuspid valve. This is followed by the rapid ejection phase of the ventricle.
• The T wave of ECG occurs during ventricular repolarization, which is followed by the opening of
the mitral valve, followed by rapid filling phase of the ventricle (second  phase of ventricular
diastole).
Note: Passive rapid filling occurs as the mitral valve opens just before atrial contraction occurs.

subject: Physiology
question:
477
Which of the following increases in COPD?
explanation:
In COPD, the functional residual capacity increases. Total lung capacity may be increased or
normal. Hence, both 1 and 2 are correct.
• In obstructive lung diseases, both FEV1 and FVC reduces, where the decrease in FEV1 is more
than the reduction in FVC. As a result, the ratio of FEV1/FVC also decreases.
• The total lung capacity is usually normal in chronic bronchitis, while increased in emphysema.
• Functional Residual Capacity (FRC) is the volume of air present in the lungs at the end of passive
expiration. In COPD, FRC is increased.
Lung Capacities:
The total lung capacity can be broken down into alternative capacities that help to define
functioning lungs.
491
• The vital lung capacity refers to the maximum amount of air expired from the fully inflated lung
or maximum inspiratory level.
• The inspiratory capacity is the maximum amount of air inspired from the end-expiratory level.
• The functional residual capacity represents the volume of the air remaining in the lungs after
expiration of a normal breath. Functional Residual Capacity (FRC) is expiratory reserve volume +
residual volume.
492

subject: Physiology
question: The predominant organ producing insulin-like growth factor I is
explanation:
Growth hormone acts on the liver to form small proteins called “somatomedins”
• Somatomedins increases bone growth in all aspect
• Effects are similar to insulin, hence referred to as insulin-like growth factor (IGF).
• Four types available:
• Most important is somatomedin C, specifically referred to as “insulin-like growth factor-1 (IGF-1)
• Binds to carrier protein in blood
• Hence, longer duration of action than growth hormone.
• Half-life - about 20 Hrs (compared to growth hormone - 20 mins)
• Blood concentration of IGF-1, follows the levels of growth hormone.
• Growth effects of GH are mostly attributed to somatomedin (rather than its direct effect on
bones & peripheral tissues)

subject: Physiology
question: What is Prosopagnosia?
explanation:
Prosopagnosia is difficulty in identifying known faces.
In humans, storage and recognition of faces is more strongly represented in the right inferior
temporal lobe in right-handed individuals, though the left lobe is also active. Damage to this area
can cause prosopagnosia.
Patients with this abnormality can recognize forms and reproduce them. They can recognize
people by their voices, and many of them show autonomic responses when they see familiar as
opposed to unfamiliar faces. However, they cannot identify the familiar faces they see. The left
hemisphere is also involved, but the role of the right hemisphere is primary.
The presence of autonomic response to a familiar face in the absence of recognition has been
explained by postulating the existence of a separate dorsal pathway for processing information
about faces that leads to recognition at only a subconscious level.
Note:
• Autotopagnosia - inability to recognize or to orient any part of one's own body
• Simultagnosia - inability to recognize multiple elements in a visual presentation, one object or
some elements of a scene can be appreciated but not the display as a whole
497
• Somatagnosia - inability to identify any part of the body, either one’s own or another’s body
• Tactile agnosia (astereognosis) -  inability to recognize objects by touch, in the presence of intact
cutaneous and proprioceptive hand sensation; caused by a lesion in the contralateral parietal lobe

subject: Physiology
question:
All of the following are components responsible for the countercurrent mechanism in the
kidney except _____.

options.a: Sodium outflow in thick ascending limb
options.b: Water outflow in thin descending limb
options.c: Sodium outflow in thin ascending limb
options.d: Flow of tubular fluid from PCT to DCT

explanation:
Thin ascending limb is not a component of the countercurrent mechanism.
The loop of Henle function as countercurrent multipliers and vasa recta as countercurrent
exchangers.
The functioning of the loop of Henle depends upon:
• High permeability of the thin descending limb to water ( via aquaporin-1)
• Active transport of Na+ and Cl- from the thick ascending limb
• Inflow of tubular fluid from proximal tubule and outflow to the distal tubule.
Note: The thin ascending limbs of juxtamedullary nephrons are permeable to Na+ and Cl- and
hence do contribute passively to countercurrent multiplication. However, among the options
provided, Option C is the best answer as the other options are directly responsible for the
countercurrent mechanism.

subject: Physiology
question: Glucose is absorbed in intestine by?
explanation:
Glucose is absorbed in the intestine by secondary active transport (sodium co-transport).
The energy for glucose transport is provided indirectly, by the active transport of Na+ out of the
cell. This maintains the concentration gradient across the luminal border of the cell so that more
Na+ and consequently more glucose enter.
Though both secondary active transport and facilitated diffusion play a role in glucose absorption,
it is the initial active transport of sodium through the basolateral membranes of the intestinal
epithelial cells that provide the eventual force for moving glucose through the membranes as well.
So, secondary active transport is the single best answer for this question.

subject: Physiology
question: In which of the following conditions is oxygen therapy not useful?
explanation:
Oxygen therapy is not beneficial in central respiratory depression.
High dose oxygen reduces the output of peripheral chemoreceptors. So, if given to patients who
already have central respiratory depression, it will indirectly reduce the hypoxic drive to breathe
even further, leading to a reduction in respiratory frequency and minute ventilation.
Note: In acute severe asthma, a high concentration of oxygen should be given by face mask to
achieve an oxygen saturation of >90%.
498

subject: Physiology
question: Va/Q = infinity means _________.

options.a: Partial pressure of O2 becomes zero
options.c: CO2 alone equilibrates with the venous blood
options.d: Partial pressure of both CO2 and O2 remain normal

explanation:
Va /Q = infinity means No exchange of O2 & CO2.
• Va represents the ventilation in alveoli.
• Q represents the blood flow through the alveolus.
• The ratio of Va and Q explains the respiratory exchange when there is an imbalance between
alveolar ventilation and alveolar blood flow.
• Ventilation-perfusion ratio is considered normal if there is an equal amount of blood flow &
ventilation through alveolus.
• When Va /Q ratio is infinity,
• There is adequate ventilation but no perfusion.
•  No exchange of gases through the respiratory membranes of corresponding alveoli.
• The alveolar air equilibrates the humidified inspired air
• No exchange of oxygen and carbon-di-oxide occurs.
• Partial pressures of the O2 and CO2 are 149 mmHg & 0 mm Hg respectively

subject: Physiology
question: 'c' wave in JVP is seen in

options.b: Slow filling at end of diastole

explanation:
'c' waves are produced by bulging of the tricuspid valve into the right atrium during the
Iso-volumetric contraction of the right ventricle.

[Physiology-NEET-2018-7-exp-image]
499

subject: Physiology
question: All of the following are true regarding the actions of endothelin-1 except ______
explanation:
Endothelin-1
• Potent vasoconstrictor agent.
• Produced by endothelial cells.
• They are secreted locally and act in a paracrine fashion.
Actions of Endothelin-1 (ET-1)
• Potent vasoconstrictor.
• Platelet activation.
• Inotropic effect.
• Closure of ductus arteriosus.
• Have a role in the pathogenesis of hypertension, myocardial infarction, and congestive heart
failure.
• In the brain, regulate the transport of substances across blood brain barrier (BBB).
• In the kidneys, they are involved in tubuloglomerular feedback→ ↓ GFR.
• In the lungs, it causes bronchoconstriction (mediated via ET-B receptors).
• In GIT, they are involved in the migration of myenteric plexus to distal colon (failure leads to
Hirschsprung’s/megacolon).
• Deletion of both alleles of ET-1 gene→craniofacial abnormalities and respiratory failure at birth.

subject: Physiology
question: Aldosterone secretion is maximally stimulated by______
explanation:
Hyperkalemia and activation of renin-angiotensin system are the most potent stimulators of
aldosterone secretion.

[Physiology-NEET-2018-9-exp-image]
500

subject: Physiology
question: cGMP is the second messenger of
explanation:
Vasodilatory actions of NO are mediated by cGMP.
NO is a hormone derived from the amino acid arginine. It activates guanylyl cyclases in vascular
smooth muscle cells resulting in the conversion of cGTP to cGMP (2nd messenger). cGMP
activates cGMP dependent protein kinase, which causes blood vessels to relax.
cGMP is the second messenger for nitric oxide and atrial natriuretic factor.

subject: Physiology
question: Transport molecule for Fe2+ into the enterocytes is
explanation:
Transport of Fe2+ into the enterocytes occurs via divalent metal transporter 1 (DMT1), through an
active transport process.
Protein DMT-1 (Divalent Metal Transporter-1) is expressed on the apical surface of enterocytes in
the initial part of the duodenum. DMT-1 is not specific to iron & transports other substances
like zinc, copper, cobalt & manganese.

[Physiology-NEET-2018-11-exp-image]

subject: Physiology
question: Nuclear signalling pathways are all except
explanation:
Rat proteins are not nuclear signalling pathways. Recombinant rat IFN- is a bioactive protein
intended for use in cell culture applications.
Importin:
• Transporting protein molecules into nucleus by binding to specific recognition sequences, called
Nuclear Localization Sequences (NLS).
501
• 2 subunits, importin α and importin β.
Caveolins:
• Family of integral membrane proteins that are principal components of caveolae membranes
• Involved in receptor-independent endocytosis.
• Acts as scaffolding proteins within caveolar membranes by compartmentalizing and
concentrating signaling molecules.
NF-kB
• Part of a prototypical proinflammatory signaling pathway
• based on the role of NF-κB in the expression of proinflammatory genes including cytokines,
chemokines, and adhesion molecules

subject: Physiology
question:
Which of the following statements is false about Cuthbertson's metabolic ebb and flow
responses to trauma?

options.a: The "ebb" phase is associated with a decline in body temperature and oxygen consumption.
options.b: The "flow" phase occurs after resuscitation from a state of shock.
options.c: The "ebb" phase is aimed at reducing posttraumatic energy depletion.
options.d:
The "ebb" phase leads to activation of the innate immune system and induction of the hepatic
acute-phase response.

explanation:
The "flow" phase (not "ebb") leads to activation of the innate immune system and induction of the
hepatic acute-phase response.
Cuthbertson's ebb and flow response to trauma:
The "ebb" phase lasts for a brief duration and is associated with a decline in body temperature and
oxygen consumption, aimed at reducing post traumatic energy depletion.
The "flow" phase occurs after resuscitation from a state of shock, which leads to an increased
metabolic turnover, activation of the innate immune system and induction of the hepatic
acute-phase response. This hypercatabolic condition leads to a significantly increased oxygen
consumption and energy expenditure.

subject: Physiology
question: Kidney responds to shock by _______.
explanation:
Kidney responds to shock by increasing afferent arteriolar resistance.
During shock, both afferent and efferent arterioles in kidney are constricted, however, the efferent
vessels are constricted much more.
The glomerular filtration rate and the plasma renal flow is decreased during shock. As plasma
renal flow is decreased to a greater extent, the filtration fraction increases. (Option 1 & 3)
502

subject: Physiology
question:
According to myogenic hypothesis of renal autoregulation the afferent arterioles contract in
response to stretch-induced by______
explanation:
508
The afferent arterioles contract in response to stretch induced by the opening of calcium channels.
The mechanism is called myogenic autoregulation of renal blood flow and GFR.
The stretch of the vascular wall allows increased movement of calcium ions from the extracellular
fluid into the cells, causing them to contract. This contraction prevents excessive stretch of the
vessel. This prevents excessive increase of renal blood flow and GFR by raising vascular
resistance.
This mechanism may be more important in protecting the kidney from hypertension-induced
injury.
Autoregulation has two main mechanisms:
• Metabolic mechanism: Tissues produce vasodilatory metabolites that regulate flow e.g.
prostaglandins, NO, CO2, H+ and K+
• Myogenic mechanism

[Physiology-NEET-2019-1-exp-image]

subject: Physiology
question:
According to Herrington classification, the decerebrate rigidity is characterized by all except
______

options.a: Rigidity occurs in all the muscles of the body
options.b: Increase in the rate of discharge of the I efferent neuron
options.c: Increased excitability of the motor neuron pool
options.d: Decerebration produces no phenomenon akin to spinal shock

explanation:
In decerebrate rigidity, there is rigidity in extensor muscles of all the four extremities (not all
muscles of the body).
Decerebration
• Complete transection of the brain stem between the superior and inferior colliculi permits the
brain stem pathways to function independently of their input from higher brain structures. This is
called a mid-collicular decerebration.
• This lesion interrupts all input from the cortex (corticospinal and corticobulbar tracts) and red
nucleus (rubrospinal tract), primarily to distal muscles of the extremities.
• The excitatory and inhibitory reticulospinal pathways (primarily to postural extensor muscles)
remain intact.
509
• The dominance of drive from ascending sensory pathways to the excitatory reticulospinal
pathway leads to hyperactivity in extensor muscles in all four extremities which are called
decerebrate rigidity.
• The excitatory input from the reticulospinal pathway activates γ-motor neurons which indirectly
activate α-motor neurons (via Ia spindle afferent activity). This is called the gamma loop.
Option 4. Spinal shock is different from decebrate rigidity as it is a transient state that can occur
immediately after a spinal cord injury. It is characterized by a temporary loss or depression of
spinal reflexes below the level of the injury.

subject: Physiology
question: Which of the following is true for decorticate rigidity?

options.a: It is produced by the removal of cerebral cortex and basal ganglia
options.b: Flexion of lower limbs and extension of upper limbs occurs
options.d: It is characterized by flexion of upper limbs and extension of lower limbs

explanation:
• Removal of the cerebral cortex (decortication) produces decorticate rigidity.
• Characterized by flexion of the upper extremities at the elbow and extensor hyperactivity in the
lower extremities.
• The flexion can be explained by active rubrospinal excitation of flexor muscles in the upper
extremities; the hyperextension of lower extremities is due to the same changes that occur after
midcollicular decerebration.

[Physiology-NEET-2019-3-exp-image]

subject: Physiology
question:
Acid Citrate Dextrose (ACD), used to store blood, preserves the RBCs for a period of 21 days.
The storage period when adding phosphate alone and when adding adenine and phosphate
together to ACD would respectively be?
503
explanation:
The storage period when adding phosphate alone to ACD would be 21 days and when adding
adenine and phosphate together would be 35 days.
When adding phosphate to ACD, Citrate Phosphate Dextrose (CPD) is formed – It has a storage
period of 21 days. There is no change in the shelf life as phosphate only increases the 2,3 DPG
levels of the stored blood.
510
When adding both phosphate and adenine to ACD, Citrate Phosphate Dextrose Adenine (CPDA-1)
is formed. It has a storage period of 35 days. It increases the shelf life by improving ATP synthesis
of RBCs.
Using additive solutions containing saline, adenine, and glucose, with or without mannitol
(SAGM) decreases RBC hemolysis, thus increasing the storage period to 42 days.

subject: Physiology
question: Functional residual capacity is:

options.b: Volume that remains after forced expiration

explanation:
Functional Residual Capacity (FRC) is expiratory reserve volume + residual volume.
From the picture given below, (FRC = ERV + RV).

[Physiology-NEET-2019-5-exp-image]
Lung Capacities:
The total lung capacity can be broken down into alternative capacities that help to define
functioning lungs.
• The vital lung capacity refers to the maximum amount of air expired from the fully inflated lung
or maximum inspiratory level.
• The inspiratory capacity is the maximum amount of air inspired from the end-expiratory level.
• The functional residual capacity represents the volume of the air remaining in the lungs after
expiration of a normal breath.

subject: Physiology
question: Which of the following is not a dietary fiber?
explanation:
Gum is the best answer among the given options. Even though all of the given options are dietary
fibers, the consumption of gums through diet is very low and hence, it is the best answer.
511
Dietary fibers consist of both soluble and insoluble forms and include lignin, pectin, inulin,
cellulose, hemicellulose, etc.
Advantages of fibers:
• Forms bulk of the stool and reduces stool transit time
• Reduces incidence of carcinoma colon, DM, CAD, diverticulitis

subject: Physiology
question: Lesion in the medial lemniscus at the level of pons causes which of the following?

options.a: Pain and temperature loss in the same side
options.b: Pain and temperature loss in the opposite side
options.c: Loss of tactile and proprioception on the same side
options.d: Loss of tactile and proprioception on the opposite side

explanation:
Lesion in the medial lemniscus at the level of pons causes loss of tactile and proprioception on the
opposite side.
Fibers of medial lemniscus ascend ipsilaterally in the dorsal columns of the spinal cord to the
medulla, where they synapse in the gracilus and cuneate nuclei.
The second-order neurons from these nuclei cross the midline and ascend in the medial lemniscus
to end in the contralateral ventral posterior lateral (VPL) nucleus and related specific sensory
relay nuclei of the thalamus.

subject: Physiology
question: Which neurotransmitter is responsible for anterograde dilatation of the small intestine?
explanation:
VIP is responsible for anterograde dilatation of the small intestine.
Peristalsis involves the contraction of a segment of intestine behind the bolus of food and
relaxation of a segment of the intestine in front of the bolus of food.
Cholinergic neurons passing in anterograde direction produce NO and VIP which cause relaxation
of a segment of the intestine in front of the bolus.
Cholinergic neurons passing in retrograde direction produce substance P and acetylcholine which
cause contraction of a segment of the intestine behind the bolus.

[Physiology-NEET-2019-8-exp-image]
512
Note: In achalasia cardia, there is decreased release of NO and VIP in the distal part of the
esophagus.

subject: Physiology
question: Reflex responsible for tachycardia during right atrial distension is:
explanation:
Reflex responsible for tachycardia during right atrial distension is Bainbridge reflex.
Atrial Reflex Control of Heart Rate: The Bainbridge Reflex
• The stretch receptors of the atria that elicit the Bainbridge reflex transmit their afferent signals
through the vagus nerves to the medulla of the brain.
• Then efferent signals are transmitted back through vagal and sympathetic nerves to increase
heart rate and strength of heart contraction.
Bezold-Jarisch Reflex:
• Activation of chemosensitive vagal C fibres in the cardiopulmonary region (e.g. Juxta capillary
region of alveoli, ventricles, atria, great veins, and pulmonary artery) causes profound brady-
cardia, hypotension and a brief period of apnea followed by rapid shallow breathing.
Cushing Reflex:
• When ICP is increased, the blood supply to RVLM neurons is compromised.
• The local hypoxia and hypercapnia causes an increase in discharge from the neurons, producing
hypertension, bradycardia and irregular respiration.
• The resultant rise in systemic arterial pressure tends to restore the blood flow to the medulla.

subject: Physiology
question: Testosterone production is mainly contributed by ______
explanation:
Testosterone production is mainly contributed by Leydig cells.
Testosterone is synthesized from cholesterol in the Leydig cells and also from androstenedione
secreted by the adrenal cortex.
The diagnosis of testosterone deficiency should be made only on the basis of clinical signs and
symptoms consistent with androgen deficiency, and consistently subnormal serum testosterone
concentrations at 8 to 10 AM on three occasions.
Testosterone has been used to treat primary or secondary hypogonadism in adult men.

subject: Physiology
question: What is the function of tubuloglomerular feedback?
explanation:
Tubuloglomerular feedback regulates glomerular filtration rate (GFR).
As the rate of Iow through the ascending limb of the loop of Henle and first part of the distal
tubule increases, glomerular filtration in the same nephron decreases, and, conversely, a decrease
513
in Iow increases the GFR.

subject: Physiology
question:
Based on the agglutination test shown below, what is the blood group?
505

[Physiology-NEET-2019-12-image]
explanation:
Based on the agglutination test shown in the image, the blood group is O positive.
As agglutination is present only in 'Rh' and not with 'A' and 'B' hence the blood group is O Rh
positive.

[Physiology-NEET-2019-12-exp-image]

subject: Physiology
question: Which of the following neurons predominantly fire during forceful expiration?
explanation:
Ventral groups of neurons fire, when forceful expiration is needed like during heavy exercise.
Dorsal group of neurons control the normal inspiration and expiration.
Pneumotaxic center limits the duration of inspiration.

subject: Physiology
question: ADH acts through which aquaporin channel?
explanation:
The antidiuretic hormone acts through aquaporin channel 2.
ADH binds to V2 receptors in the late distal tubules, collecting tubules, and collecting ducts, and
stimulates the movement of an intracellular protein, called aquaporin-2 (AQP-2), to the luminal
side of the cell membranes.

explanation:

Postganglionic sympathetic (type C) has the least conduction velocity among the given options.

subject: Physiology
question: Which part of the liver stores vitamin A?
explanation:
Vitamin A is stored in the liver perisinusoidal stellate (Ito) cells as retinyl ester (retinol palmitate).
Kupffer cells, also known as stellate macrophages and Kupffer-Borowicz cells; are specialized
macrophages located in the liver that form part of the mononuclear phagocyte system. These are
present, lining the walls of the sinusoids, not the perisinusoidal space (Space of Disse).

subject: Physiology
question: Liver synthesizes all of the following except:
explanation:
Immunoglobulins are not synthesized by the liver. They are produced by plasma cells.
Plasma proteins synthesized by the liver are:
• Albumin
• Coagulation/clotting factors
• Acute-phase proteins
• Steroid binding and other hormone-binding proteins

subject: Physiology
question: Which of the following ions is involved in peripheral oxygen sensing chemoreceptors?
explanation:
Potassium is involved in peripheral oxygen sensing chemoreceptors.
Type I glomus cells (carotid and aortic body) have O2-sensitive K+ channels, whose conductance
is reduced in proportion to the degree of hypoxia.
515
This reduces the K+ efflux, depolarizing the cell and causing Ca2+ inIux.
The Ca2+ influx triggers action potentials and consequent excitation of the afferent nerve endings.

subject: Physiology
question:
507
Brown sequard syndrome is characterized by _____

options.a: Fine touch lost on the opposite side
options.b: Pain and temperature lost on the same side
options.c: Pain and temperature loss on the opposite side
options.d: Only pain lost on the same side

explanation:
Hemisection of the spinal cord causes damage to ascending sensory (dorsal column pathway,
ventrolateral spinothalamic tract) and descending motor (corticospinal tract) pathways, which is
called the Brown- Séquard syndrome.
The lesion to fasciculus gracilus or fasciculus cuneatus leads to ipsilateral loss of discriminative
touch, vibration, and proprioception below the level of the lesion.(fibers from the sacral cord are
positioned most medially and those from the cervical cord are positioned most laterally. )
The loss of the spinothalamic tract leads to contralateral loss of pain and temperature sensation
beginning one or two segments below the lesion.
Damage to the corticospinal tract produces weakness and spasticity in certain muscle groups on
the same side of the body.
516

subject: Physiology
question:
A person develops headache and breathlessness on a trekking expedition following rapid
ascent to over 3000 metres above the sea level. Which of the following is not used in the
treatment of this condition?
explanation:
The symptoms of headache and breathlessness following rapid ascent indicate acute
mountain/altitude sickness and IV digoxin is not used to treat this condition.
On ascent to high altitudes, the low partial pressure of oxygen leads to hypoxia and systemic
arteriolar dilatation. Consequently, the capillary pressure rises with increased transudation of
fluid into the brain tissue and the following symptoms are seen:
• Headache
• Irritation
• Insomnia
• Breathlessness
• Nausea and vomiting
Treatment involves administration of oxygen, descent to a lower altitude and administration of
acetazolamide. Acetazolamide counteracts hypoxia by stimulating respiration and also reduces the
formation of cerebrospinal fluid, thereby reducing intracranial tension.
Portable hyperbaric chambers are increasingly being used in the treatment of high-altitude
illnesses when rapid descent is not possible. These chambers are available in armed forces
hospitals in high altitude areas

subject: Physiology
question: V/Q = infinity holds true in which of the following conditions?

options.a: Foreign body obstruction in the bronchus
options.c: When O2 and CO2 ratio is equal
options.d: The PO2 of alveolar air is 159 mmHg and PCO2 is 40 mmHg

explanation:
VA /Q= infinity means that there is no exchange of O2 & CO2, which means the volume of
the lung equals to dead space and the physiological effort of breathing is wasted.
VA represents the ventilation in alveoli and Q represents the blood flow through the alveolus. The
ratio of VA and Q explains the respiratory exchange when there is an imbalance between alveolar
ventilation and alveolar blood flow. The ventilation-perfusion ratio is considered normal if there is
an equal amount of blood flow & ventilation through the alveolus.
VA /Q ratio is infinity when:
• There is adequate ventilation but no perfusion.
• No exchange of gases ( O2 and CO2 )through the respiratory membranes of corresponding
alveoli.
• The alveolar air equilibrates the humidified inspired air.
• Partial pressures of the O2 and CO2 are 149 mmHg & 0 mm Hg respectively.
522

subject: Physiology
question: Prolactin level is highest during :
explanation:
Among the given options, the prolactin level is highest 24 hours after delivery.
The maximum level of prolactin is seen during pregnancy and in the immediate postpartum
period. During pregnancy, the secretion is increased reaching a peak at the time of delivery. After
delivery, the plasma concentration falls to nonpregnant levels in a few weeks. Suckling produces
surges of prolactin release.

subject: Physiology
question: C wave of JVP represents:

options.a: Tricuspid bulge into right atrium during isovolumetric ventricular contraction

explanation:
'C' wave in JVP represents tricuspid bulge into right atrium during isovolumetric ventricular
contraction.
Characteristics of JVP :
• a wave corresponds to atrial systole which causes regurgitation of some blood into the great
veins.
• c wave occurs due to the increase in atrial pressure caused by bulging of the tricuspid valve into
the right atrium during isovolumetric ventricular contraction.
• x descent occurs due to the fall in atrial pressure during ventricular systole due to pulling of
valve leaflets.
• v wave is seen at the end of ventricular contraction and occurs due to the slow venous filling
which causes an increase in atrial pressure.
• y descent occurs due to the passive emptying of the right atrium into the right ventricle.

[Physiology-NEET-2020-4-exp-image]
523

subject: Physiology
question:
The protein in the glomerular basement membrane responsible for charge-dependent
filtration is?
explanation:
The protein in the glomerular basement membrane responsible for charge-dependent filtration is
heparan sulfate. It prevents filtration by repelling negatively charged proteins.
Collagen type IV, fibronectin, entactin are structural components of basement membrane.

subject: Physiology
question:
Which of the following clotting factors would have decreased gamma carboxyglutamate
residue in a patient on warfarin therapy?
explanation:
Factor 2 would have decreased gamma carboxyglutamate residue in a patient on warfarin therapy.
Activation of coagulation factors II, VII, IX, X is done by carboxylation of their glutamate
residues. This is done by the enzyme γ-glutamyl carboxylase with the reduced form of vitamin K
as a co-factor.
This reduced form of vitamin K needs to be constantly recycled back from its oxidized form for the
reaction to continue. This recycling is done by the enzyme vitamin K epoxide reductase. This
enzyme is inhibited by warfarin.
Hence, warfarin indirectly inhibits the γ-carboxylation of vitamin K dependent clotting factors
- Factors II, VII, IX, X by directly inhibiting vitamin K epoxide reductase. Thus leading to
decreased gamma carboxyglutamate residue in these factors.

[Physiology-NEET-2020-6-exp-image]

subject: Physiology
question: Which of the following conditions would have the highest levels of ACTH and cortisol?
explanation:
Among the given conditions, the levels of ACTH and cortisol will be highest in Cushing's disease.
Cushing’s disease is caused by an ACTH secreting pituitary adenoma. The increased ACTH causes
increased cortisol production.
Note: In normal physiologic state, ACTH levels are higher in the early morning but not as high as
in Cushing's disease.
524

[Physiology-NEET-2020-7-exp-image]

subject: Physiology
question: Following complete ileal and partial jejunal resection, the patient is most likely to have
explanation:
After complete ileal and partial jejunal resection, the patient is likely to have vitamin B12
deficiency.
Vitamin B12 is absorbed in the ileum. Vitamin B12 binds to intrinsic factor, a protein secreted by
the parietal cells of the stomach, and the complex is absorbed across the ileal mucosa. Hence after
ileal resection, there is impaired absorption of vitamin B12.

subject: Physiology
question: Respiratory distress syndrome is due to defective
explanation:
Respiratory distress syndrome is due to defective type 2 pneumocytes which produce lung
surfactants. The surfactants reduce the surface tension in the alveoli. A defect in type 2
pneumocytes or surfactant deficiency can lead to respiratory distress syndrome.
The primary alveoli develop and the production of the surfactant begins by week 20-24. However,
it does not reach the lungs until later. The surfactant appears in the amniotic fluid between 28
and 32 weeks of gestation. Mature levels of the pulmonary surfactant are present usually after 35
weeks of gestation.
Antenatal corticosteroids are given to increase production of surfactant.

subject: Physiology
question: Which of the following hormones use receptor tyrosine kinase signalling?
explanation:
Insulin uses the receptor tyrosine kinase signalling to exert its functions.
Other hormones acting in a similar fashion includes
• Growth hormone
525
• Growth factors like VEGF, PDGF, FGF, NGF
• Prolactin
• Leptin

subject: Physiology
question: What is the site of lesion in Wernicke's hemianopic pupil?
explanation:
The site of lesion in Wernicke's hemianopic pupil is optic tract.
Wernicke hemianopic pupil is the light reflex, where, illumination of the blind half of the retina
shows no pupillary reaction while stimulating the normal side of the retina shows a pupillary
reaction. It is seen in lesions involving the optic tract. It is also seen in cases such as TB, syphilis
and tumours of the optic thalamus.
Homonymous hemianopia (HH) involves loss of vision on the same side of the visual field in both
eyes. HH is characteristically seen in lesions involving the visual pathway posterior to the optic
chiasma.

subject: Physiology
question:
In which of the following reflexes, gastric emptying is delayed when acidic content enters the
duodenum?
explanation:
In the enterogastric reflex, gastric emptying is delayed when acidic content enters the duodenum.
When the food enters the duodenum, multiple reflexes are initiated from the duodenal wall which
passes back to the stomach. These reflexes slow or stop the stomach emptying if the volume of the
chyme in the duodenum becomes too much. These reflexes are called enterogastric reflexes.
This reflex can be triggered due to the following reasons:
• Presence of irritants and acids in duodenal chyme (most sensitive factor)
• Distension of duodenum due to the presence of an excessive volume of chyme
• Rapid entry of hypertonic or hypotonic fluids into the duodenum
• Presence of breakdown products of protein digestion and sometimes fat

subject: Physiology
question:
A lady presents with vascular injury to the inferior frontal gyrus. Which functional area is
likely to be affected?
explanation:
Broca's motor speech area is likely to be affected in the vascular injury of the inferior frontal gyrus
A lesion in this region leads to motor/Broca's aphasia. Speech is non-fluent and interrupted by
many word-finding pauses. The speech is impoverished in function words that contribute to the
syntax but is enriched in meaningful nouns and verbs. However, comprehension of speech is
intact and the patient knows what he wants to say.
For example, a patient with Broca's aphasia may say "walk dog" instead of "I want to take the dog
for a walk".
526

subject: Physiology
question:
A man falls into a deep sleep with one arm under his head. He finds he is not able to move
this arm when he awakens. However, it tingles and pain sensation in the arm is still intact.
What is the reason for the loss of motor function without loss of pain sensation is that in the
nerves to his arm?

options.a: B fibers are more susceptible to pressure than A fibers
options.b: A fibers are more sensitive to pressure than C fibers
options.c: C fibers are more sensitive to pressure than A fibers
options.d: Motor nerves are more affected by sleep than sensory nerves

explanation:
The reason for the loss of motor function without loss of pain sensation is that in the nerves to his
arm, A-fibers are more sensitive to pressure than C fibers.
Sleeping for a long period with the arms under the head puts pressure on a nerve. It leads to the
loss of conduction in large-diameter motor, touch, and pressure fibers, while pain sensation
remains relatively intact. A-fibers (includes the motor fibers) are affected.
The susceptibility of the nerve fibers to pressure is A>B>C.
This is responsible for Saturday night or Sunday morning paralysis.

subject: Physiology
question: Which of the following has the highest oxygen concentration in fetal circulation?
explanation:
Inferior vena cava has the highest oxygen concentration in fetal circulation.
The blood gets oxygenated in the placenta and this passes to the umbilical vein. From the
umbilical vein, 50% of the blood enters the hepatic circulation and 50% bypasses the liver to enter
the inferior vena cava. From here, the blood enters the heart via the right atrium. The right atrium
also receives deoxygenated blood from the superior vena cava. This mixed blood is distributed to
the body via aorta.
Therefore, inferior vena cava has the highest concentration of oxygen in the fetal circulation.

[Physiology-NEET-2020-15-exp-image]

subject: Physiology
question:
520
Respiratory distress syndrome is due to the deficiency of
explanation:
Respiratory distress syndrome is due to the deficiency of
dipalmitoyl-phosphatidyl-choline(DPPC) /dipalmitoyl lecithin. It is the major
pulmonary surfactant.
527
Clinical relevance- Test to assess fetal lung maturity is L/S ratio - lecithin/sphingomyelin ratio. A
ratio of ≥ 2 is considered normal.

subject: Physiology
question: What makes CPDA superior than ACD for blood storage:

options.a: The fall in 2, 3 DPG is less

explanation:
The fall in 2, 3 DPG is less when the blood is stored in CPDA compared with ACD.
2, 3 DPG (2,3-diphosphoglycerate) binds to deoxygenated haemoglobin. Therefore, an increase in
2,3 DPG causes increased release of O2 to the tissues. The equilibrium between Hb and O2 can be
understood from the following equation:
HbO2 + 2,3-DPG I Hb−2,3-DPG + O2
In stored blood, the 2,3 DPG levels usually fall. As a result, stored blood releases less oxygen to
the tissues than the normal circulating blood, thus limiting its use in hypoxic patients (Eg:
anaemia).
However, such a fall in 2,3 DPG is less when it is stored in a phosphate-containing solution like
CPDA (Citrate, phosphate, dextrose, adenine), rather than ACD (Acid citrate dextrose). Therefore,
the blood stored in CPDA, in comparison with blood stored in ACD has:
• Higher 2,3 DPG concentration
• More amount of deoxygenated blood bound to 2,3 DPG
• Decreased affinity of Hb to the oxygen.
• Increased release of oxygen to the tissues.
• Raised P50 (PO2 at which Hb is half saturated)
• Shift in O2-Hb dissociation curve to the right
528

subject: Physiology
question: Which of the following is not involved in renal auto-regulation?
explanation:
Renal sympathetic system fibres is not involved in the autoregulation of renal blood flow, because
a denervated kidney also maintains the auto regulation of renal blood flow as that of a normal
kidney.
Tubulo glomerular feedback, myogenic reflex, and renin-angiotensin system are all involved in the
autoregulation of renal blood flow.
Tubulo glomerular feedback:
• When the renal artery pressure increases, the renal blood flow and glomerular filtration rate
(GFR) increases
• This increased GFR results in increased delivery of solute and water to the early distal tubule,
particularly the macula densa
• The macula dense, then secretes a vasoactive substance that constricts afferent arteriole via a
paracrine mechanism
• This vasoconstriction then reduces renal blood flow and GFR back to normal
Myogenic reflex:
• The increase in renal arterial pressure stretches the walls of the afferent arterioles, which then
contracts
535
• This contraction increases the afferent arteriolar resistance
• This increase in resistance then compensates for the increase in blood pressure, thereby keeping
the renal blood flow constant.
Renin-angiotensin system:
• Angiotensin II is a potent vasoconstrictor of both afferent and efferent arterioles
• However efferent arterioles are more sensitive to angiotensin II than afferent arteriole
• Thus low levels of angiotensin II produce an increase in GFR by constricting efferent arterioles
• High levels of angiotensin II decreases GFR by constricting afferent and efferent arterioles,
thereby decreasing the renal blood flow

subject: Physiology
question: The formula for urea clearance is U*V/P, where U is
explanation:
‘U’ in the urea clearance stands for urine concentration of urea in mg /100ml.
The formula for renal clearance of a substance is,
Clearance (C) = U × V / P,
where
U = Urine concentration of the substance in mg / ml
V = Urine flow rate in ml/min
P = Plasma concentration of substance in mg /ml.
Renal clearance of a substance is the volume of plasma that is completely cleared of the substance
by the kidneys per unit of time.

subject: Physiology
question:
The relaxation of the intestinal segment distal to the segment with the bolus of food during
peristalsis is because of
explanation:
The relaxation of the intestinal segment distal to the segment of a bolus of food during peristalsis
is due to Vasoactive Intestinal Peptide (VIP).
Peristaltic contractions in the small intestine are designed to propel the food towards the large
intestine.
The food bolus in the small intestine is sensed by enterochromaffin cells of the intestinal mucosa,
which release 5-hydroxytryptamine (5-HT)(serotonin). The 5-HT then binds to the receptors on
intrinsic primary afferent neurons (IPANs), which when activated, initiate the peristaltic reflex in
that segment of the small intestine. Behind the bolus, excitatory transmitters like acetylcholine,
Substance P, neuropeptide Y are released in the circular muscle, while these pathways are
inhibited in the longitudinal muscle; thus this segment of small intestine (behind the bolus)
contracts and lengthens.
(Recall that contraction of circular muscle decreases the diameter of that small intestinal segment
whereas contraction of the longitudinal muscle decreases the length of that segment. To prevent
the conflict that would occur if circular and longitudinal are contracted at the same time, they are
536
reciprocally innervated)
In front of the bolus, inhibitory pathways like vasoactive intestinal peptide (VIP) and nitric oxide
(NO) are activated in the circular muscle, while excitatory pathways are activated in the
longitudinal muscle;thereby relaxing and shortening this segment of small intestine.

subject: Physiology
question:
A patient presented with severe watery diarrhea resembling that of rice gruel stool. Damage
of which structure is likely to have resulted in this diarrhea?
explanation:
The given clinical picture of a patient having rice gruel stools is suggestive of the diagnosis of
‘cholera’. Vibrio cholerae in addition to the cholera toxin (CT) produces some additional toxins
such as,
• Zonula occludens toxin, which alters the intestinal permeability by acting on intestinal epithelial
cell tight junctions (which are also called zonula occludens)
• Accessory cholera enterotoxin.
Zonula occludens:
• Also known as tight junctions
• Characteristically surround the apical margins of the cells in epithelia such as the intestinal
mucosa, the walls of the renal tubules, and the choroid plexus.
• Also important to the endothelial barrier function and endothelium-dependent vasodilation.
• Made up of ridges- half from one cell and a half from another cell- which adhere so strongly that
there is no space between the cells.
• Three families of transmembrane proteins are involved:
• Occludin
• Junctional adhesion molecule
• Claudins
• Paracellular pathway - these tight junctions permit the passage of ions, solutes, and intracellular
signaling molecules in between adjacent cells.
537

[Physiology-INI-CET-2021-4-exp-image]
Other options:
B) Gap junctions:
• The gap between the two cells in these types of junctions is about 4nm.
• These junctions are formed by units called connexons, which are present in the membrane of
each cell.
• Each connexon is made up of six protein subunits called connexins.
• They permit substances to pass between the cells, without entering the ECF.
• The pore diameter is around 0.8 to 1.4  nm, which permits the passage of ions, sugars, amino
acids, and other solutes which weigh up to 1000 Da.
• They also permit rapid propagation of electrical activity from cell to cell, as well as the exchange
of various chemical messengers and intracellular signaling molecules.
C) Adherent junctions:
• Adherent junctions or Zonula adherens is a continuous structure on the basal side of the zonula
occludens.
• It contains cadherins.
D) Hemidesmosomes:
• They look like half-desmosomes.
• They attach cells to the underlying basal lamina and are connected intracellularly to
intermediate filaments.
• They contain integrins.

subject: Physiology
question: Beta hCG is structurally similar to which biochemical moiety?
explanation:
538
Beta hCG (human chorionic gonadotropin) has biological activity similar to that of
luteinizing hormone (LH).
hCG is a glycoprotein produced by syncytiotrophoblasts. It is made up of α and β subunits.
Though the α subunit of hCG is similar to the α subunits of LH, FSH, and TSH, the β subunit has
structural similarity to LH only. Hence, LH is a better answer than FSH.
hCG is primarily luteinizing and luteotrophic. It appears to act on the same receptor as that of LH.
The function of hCG is to prevent regression of the corpus luteum. It can be detected in blood as
early as 6 days after conception and forms the basis of various tests used to identify pregnancy.
Its level peaks at gestational week 9 and then declines gradually but is present in lower
concentration till the end of pregnancy. But hCG is not specific for pregnancy as it is secreted in a
variety of gastrointestinal and genital tumors in both males and females.

subject: Physiology
question: What is the duration of short term memory?
explanation:
Short-term memory lasts for seconds to minutes, hence of the given 4 options, 30-300 seconds is
the most appropriate answer.
Memory is usually divided into two forms: Explicit or declarative memory which is associated with
consciousness or awareness. Implicit or nondeclarative memory does not involve awareness and
its retention does not usually involve processing in the hippocampus.
Explicit and many forms of implicit memory involve
• Short-term memory and
• Long-term memory.
Short-term memory:
It lasts seconds to minutes; the encoding process for short-term explicit memory involves the
hippocampus. It lasts only till the person continues to think about the number or facts.
Working memory is a form of short-term memory that keeps information available, usually for
very short periods of time, until the individual plans action based on it.
Long-term memory:
It stores memories that can be recollected after years or even for a lifetime. These memories are
stored in various parts of the neocortex. The various parts of the memories such as, visual,
auditory, olfactory, etc., are localised to the cortical regions concerned with these functions. All
these parts of the memories are brought together by long-term changes in the strength of
transmission at relevant synaptic junctions so that all the components are brought to
consciousness when that particular memory is recalled.
Neural basis of memory:
The basis of memory is the alteration in the strength of the selected synaptic connections. Second
messenger systems contribute to the changes in the neural circuits which are required for learning
and memory. Alterations in the cellular membrane channels also contribute to it.
539

subject: Physiology
question: The stroke volume depends on end-diastolic volume. This is best represented as
explanation:
The relationship between end-diastolic volume and stroke volume is best represented by
length-tension relationship.
This is explained by the ‘Frank-Starling law of heart’ which states that ‘ the volume of blood
ejected by the ventricle depends on the volume present in the ventricle at the end of the diastole’.
The length of the myocytes depend on the volume (and hence tension) present at the end of the
diastole, which in turn, depends on the volume returned to the heart, or the venous return.
• The Frank-Starling law governs the normal ventricular function and ensures that the cardiac
output equals venous return, within physiological limits.
• There is a curvilinear relationship between stroke volume or cardiac output and the ventricular
end-diastolic volume.
• Agents that increase the contractility of the cardiac muscle have a positive inotropic effect and
produce increased stroke volume and thereby increased cardiac output for a given end-diastolic
volume. (Eg: Digoxin)
• Agents that decrease the contractility of the cardiac muscle have a negative inotropic effect and
produce decreased stroke volume (and cardiac output) for a given end-diastolic volume.

subject: Physiology
question:
Which of the following ions in the thick ascending limb of the loop of Henle is responsible for
the resorption of divalent ions?
explanation:
Potassium (K+) in the thick ascending limb of loop of Henle is responsible for the resorption of
divalent ions such as, calcium, magnesium, etc.
This resorption is driven by the lumen-positive potential difference across the cells of the thick
ascending limb, created by the potassium leak into the lumen.
Mechanism of resorption in thick ascending limb:
• The luminal membrane contains a Na+-K+-2Cl- cotransporter.
• There is net resorption of Na+, K+ and Cl- in the thick ascending limb as follows:
• All three ions are transported into the cell on the cotransporter.
• Na+ is extruded from the cell by the Na+-K+ ATPase in the basolateral membrane.
• K+ and Cl- diffuse through channels in the basolateral membrane, down their respective
electrochemical gradients.
• Most of the K+ leaves the cell across the basolateral membrane; the remaining portion of K+
however diffuses back into the lumen.
• This K+ leak into the lumen generates a positive electrochemical potential difference in the
lumen.
• This lumen-positive potential difference drives the resorption of ions such as sodium(Na+),
magnesium (Mg2+), calcium (Ca+),etc.,
540

subject: Physiology
question: Which of the following sensory receptors are responsible for tactile discrimination?
explanation:
Meissner’s Corpuscles are responsible for discriminative touch.
Meissner’s corpuscles are encapsulated receptors found in the dermis of the non-hairy skin, most
commonly on the fingertips, lips, and other locations where tactile discrimination is very good.
Their field of reception is very small and hence used for two-point discrimination. These are
rapidly adapting receptors that encode
• Point discrimination
• Precise location
• Tapping
• Flutter

subject: Physiology
question: All of the following increases gastric acid secretion except:
explanation:
All of the above increase gastric acid secretion except serotonin.
Serotonin is localized in the enterochromaffin cells of the gastrointestinal (GI) mucosa and within
neurons of the GI tract. Serotonin inhibits gastric acid secretion. It stimulates the production and
release of gastric and colonic mucus. Serotonin acts as an enteric neurotransmitter and increases
intestinal motility.
The main factors that stimulate gastric secretion are acetylcholine, gastrin, and
histamine. Parasympathetic stimulation releases Ach which stimulates the secretion of
• pepsinogen by peptic cells
• hydrochloric acid by parietal cells
• mucus by mucous cells.
Both gastrin and histamine strongly stimulate acid secretion by parietal cells but have little effect
on other cells.
The stomach mucosa has two important types of tubular glands:
• The oxyntic (gastric) glands secrete hydrochloric acid, pepsinogen, intrinsic factor, and mucus.
• The pyloric glands secrete mainly mucus for protection of the pyloric mucosa from the stomach
acid. They also secrete the hormone gastrin.
Note- Serotonin released from the small intestine triggers vomiting by stimulation of 5HT3
receptors. Therefore, 5HT3 antagonists like ondansetron are used as antiemetics.

subject: Physiology
question: Pacinian corpuscle is stimulated by which of the following?
explanation:
Pacinian corpuscle is stimulated by pressure.
Pacinian corpuscles are the largest cutaneous mechanoreceptors. They are present in the dermis
of glabrous and hairy skin. They are composed of a nerve ending which is encapsulated by
541
concentric layers of connective tissue that give it an onion-like appearance. These receptors
respond to fast vibration and deep pressure and adapt very rapidly. The sensory nerves from
cutaneous mechanoreceptors are myelinated Aα and Aβ fibers.
Image shows histology of Pacinian corpuscle.

[Physiology-INI-CET-2021-11-exp-image]
Other mechanoreceptors:
• Meissner's corpuscles are present mainly in the dermis of non-hairy skin particularly in the
fingertips, lips, and other locations where tactile discrimination is well developed. They have
small receptive fields and can be used for two point discrimination. Meissner corpuscles are
rapidly adapting receptors that sense point discrimination, precise location, tapping, and flutter.
These are encapsulated nerve endings of a large myelinated sensory nerve (Aβ type).
The image shows the histology of Meissner's corpuscle in the dermis.

[Physiology-INI-CET-2021-11-exp-image]
• Merkel discs are seen in the epidermis of glabrous skin. They are slowly adapting receptors and
have very small receptive fields. These receptors detect vertical indentations of the skin. They
542
are expanded dendritic endings.
• Ruffini corpuscles are capsulated, enlarged, dendritic endings. They are located in the dermis of
glabrous and hairy skin, and in joint capsules. They are slowly adapting receptors. Ruffini
corpuscles detect stretch and joint rotation.
Pain and temperature sensations arise from receptors located on unmyelinated sensory neurons
present throughout the glabrous skin, hairy skin, and deep tissue. Impulses from nociceptors are
transmitted via thinly myelinated Aδ fibers and unmyelinated C fibers.
Activation of Aδ fibers is responsible for first pain (also called fast pain). It is a rapid response and
helps in the discrimination of pain or the ability to localize the site and intensity of the noxious
stimulus. It is mediated by the release of glutamate. An example is pinprick.
Activation of C fibers is responsible for delayed second pain(also called slow pain). It is a dull,
intense, diffuse, and unpleasant feeling associated with a noxious stimulus. . Second pain is
mediated by the release of a combination of glutamate and substance P. Example is visceral pain.
Types of nociceptors :
• Mechanical nociceptors respond to strong pressure (eg, from a sharp object).
• Thermal nociceptors are activated by skin temperatures above 45°C or by severe cold (<20°C).
