subject: Biochemistry
question:
A patient who has been diagnosed with multiple gastrointestinal polyps and carcinoma, with
a positive family history/history of hereditary non-polyposis colon carcinoma has a defect in
which of the following?
explanation:
Mismatch repair will be defective in a patient with multiple gastrointestinal polyps and
carcinoma, with a positive family history/history of hereditary non-polyposis colon carcinoma
(HNPCC)
Mismatch repair is a DNA repair mechanism that corrects single-nucleotide mismatches,
insertions, and deletions introduced by the DNA polymerase during DNA replication.
HNPCC or Lynch syndrome is an autosomal dominant genetic condition. It occurs due to defects
in DNA mismatch repair genes like MLH1 and MSH2. The genomes of these patients show
microsatellite instability. HNPCC is associated with a high risk of colon cancer. It may also be
associated with a risk of developing carcinomas of the endometrium, stomach, small bowel, ovary,
pancreas, and transitional cell carcinoma of the ureter and renal pelvis.
The diagnostic criteria for HNPCC are referred to as the Amsterdam criteria or the 3-2-1-0 rule. It
includes:
• Three or more relatives with HNPCC-associated cancer (colorectal cancer, endometrial cancer,
cancer of the small bowel, ureter, or renal pelvis), one of whom is a first-degree relative of the
other two.
• At least two successive generations affected
• At least one case diagnosed before age 50 years
• Familial adenomatous polyposis excluded
• Tumors verified by pathologic examination.
Other options:
Option B: Patients with xeroderma pigmentosum have a defect in the nucleotide excision repair
pathway.
Option C: Defect in base excision repair pathway is seen in patients with MUTYH-associated
polyposis.
Option D:  Sickle cell disease and beta-thalassemia are examples of diseases seen in patients with
point mutations.

subject: Biochemistry
question:
The enzymes that convert pyruvate to acetyl CoA are given in the diagram. What are the
enzymes marked as E1 and E2 respectively?

[Biochemistry-NEET-2021-8-image]
explanation:
E1 is pyruvate dehydrogenase and E2 is dihydrolipoyl transacetylase in the image showing
oxidative decarboxylation of pyruvate. This is catalyzed by the pyruvate dehydrogenase (PDH)
complex where E1, E2, and E3 are the three enzyme components of the complex.
The pyruvate dehydrogenase (PDH) complex is a multienzyme complex that is associated with the
inner mitochondrial membrane. It catalyzes the oxidative decarboxylation of pyruvate to
acetyl-CoA which enters the citric acid/Kreb’s cycle.
Steps of pyruvate decarboxylation:
• Pyruvate is transported into the mitochondria by a proton symporter.
837
• Pyruvate is decarboxylated by pyruvate dehydrogenase (E1). TPP is required for this step. The
decarboxylated pyruvate remains bound to the enzyme, as hydroxyethyl thiamine pyrophosphate.
• The hydroxyethyl group is oxidized to form an acetyl group. It is then transferred from TPP to
lipoamide to form acetyl lipoamide. This step is catalyzed by dihydrolipoyl transacetylase (E2).
• Acetyl lipoamide reacts with coenzyme A to form acetyl-CoA and reduced lipoamide.
• The reduced lipoamide is reoxidized by FAD-dependent dihydrolipoyl dehydrogenase (E3).
The overall reaction is : Pyruvate + NAD+ + CoA → Acetyl-CoA + NADH + H+ + CO2
Enzyme components of PDH 
complex
Co-factors
Pyruvate dehydrogenase (E1)
Thiamine pyrophosphate (TP
P)
Dihydrolipoyl transacetylase 
(E2)
Lipoic acidCoenzyme A
Dihydrolipoyl dehydrogenase
 (E3)
Flavin adenine dinucleotide (
FAD)Nicotinamide adenine d
inucleotide (NAD+)

[Biochemistry-NEET-2021-8-exp-image]
Clinical significance:
• Dietary deficiency of thiamine inhibits PDH. Chronic alcoholics may develop potentially fatal
pyruvic and lactic acidosis. Lactic acidosis is a common presentation in patients with inherited
pyruvate dehydrogenase deficiency too.
• In arsenic and mercury poisoning, the heavy metal ions react with -SH groups of lipoic acid and
inhibit pyruvate dehydrogenase.
Other multienzyme complexes include alpha-ketoglutarate dehydrogenase and branched-chain
ketoacid dehydrogenase. All the five coenzymes of PDH are common to these two complexes too.
838

subject: Biochemistry
question:
A young girl presented to the OPD for examination. She is tall and thin with slender fingers,
and dislocated lenses. Workup revealed cystathionine beta-synthase deficiency. Which amino
acid supplement is required in this case?
explanation:
The clinical picture of a tall and thin young girl with slender fingers, dislocated lenses, and
cystathionine beta-synthase deficiency is suggestive of classic homocystinuria. The amino acid
supplement required in this scenario is cysteine.
Classic homocystinuria is a metabolic disorder of methionine metabolism, due to cystathionine
beta-synthase deficiency. It is an autosomal recessive condition.
Cystathionine beta-synthase is a pyridoxal phosphate-dependent enzyme that condenses
homocysteine with serine to form cystathionine. The absence of this enzyme will cause cysteine
deficiency, hence cysteine needs to be supplemented in the diet.
Clinical features:
• Affected children are initially asymptomatic.
• Ectopia lentis (subluxation of the lens) is seen first, after 3 years of age.
• Intellectual disability and psychiatric and behavioral disorders.
• Thromboembolic episodes.
• Skeletal abnormalities resembling those of Marfan syndrome: They are tall and thin with
elongated limbs and arachnodactyly (slender fingers).
Elevations of both methionine and homocysteine in body fluids are the diagnostic laboratory
findings. The diagnosis is established by molecular analysis of cystathionine β-synthase (CBS) or
by assay of the enzyme in cultured fibroblasts, phytohemagglutinin-stimulated lymphocytes, or
liver biopsy specimens. Treatment includes restriction of methionine intake and supplementation
with cysteine, betaine, vitamins B6, B12, and folate.

[Biochemistry-NEET-2021-9-exp-image]
The image below shows arachnodactyly.
839

[Biochemistry-NEET-2021-9-exp-image]
Methionine supplementation is required in non-classical homocystinuria. It is due to impaired
re-methylation of homocysteine to methionine. This can be caused by defective methionine
synthase or reduced availability of two essential cofactors, 5-methyltetrahydrofolate, and
methylcobalamin.
Elevated homocysteine levels with low levels of methionine will be seen. Treatment includes
methionine supplementation and administration of methyl folate, hydroxocobalamin, and
betaine.

subject: Biochemistry
question:
A patient presented with joint pain and on evaluation, urine turns black on standing. What is
the likely enzyme deficiency?
explanation:
The urine turning black in color, on exposure to air, along with joint pain is suggestive of
alkaptonuria. It occurs due to the defect in the homogentisic acid 1, 2 - dioxygenase enzyme.
Alkaptonuria is an autosomal recessive disorder of tyrosine metabolism, that develops due to the
defect in homogentisate oxidase/homogentisic acid 1, 2 - dioxygenase, which oxidizes
homogentisate to maleylacetoacetate. The defect in this enzyme leads to the accumulation of
homogentisate, which is then excreted in the urine.
Alkaptonuria is characterized by homogentisic aciduria, ochronosis, and arthropathy (joint pain).
Homogentisic aciduria is the black discoloration of urine on prolonged exposure to air caused due
to oxidation of the excreted homogentisate.
Ochronosis is the accumulation of oxidized homogentisic acid pigment (alkaptone bodies) in
connective tissues leading to bluish-black discoloration of tissues mostly involving ear cartilage,
ocular tissue, the skin of the eyelids, forehead, cheeks, and hands, nail beds, and intervertebral
discs. The compound that accumulates in the tissues in ochronosis is benzoquinone acetate.
Painful arthritis (arthropathy) may be seen from the fourth or fifth decade.
Diagnosis: Blackish discoloration of urine on standing is diagnostic of alkaptonuria (shown
below). The ferric chloride test will be positive.
840
The image further below shows Ochronosis.

[Biochemistry-NEET-2021-10-exp-image]

[Biochemistry-NEET-2021-10-exp-image]
Treatment:  No specific treatment is required. Symptomatic treatment with analgesics, spinal
surgery, and arthroplasty along with ascorbic acid and protein restriction can be
considered. Nitisinone (p-hydroxyphenylpyruvic acid (pHPPA) oxidase inhibitor) reduces urinary
excretion of homogentisic acid.
Other options:
Option B: Tyrosine transaminase deficiency causes type II tyrosinemia (Richner-Hanhart
syndrome). Patients present with painful corneal erosions with photophobia, learning disability,
and palmoplantar keratosis.
Option C: Fumarylacetoacetate hydrolase deficiency causes type I tyrosinemia (Tyrosinosis).
Patients generally present with failure to thrive, neuropathy, boiled-cabbage urine, and liver
failure secondary to cirrhosis.
841
Option D:  4-hydroxy-phenyl pyruvate dioxygenase deficiency causes type III tyrosinemia
(neonatal tyrosinemia). Patients might rarely have a mental disability. The liver function is
generally normal.
The steps of tyrosine metabolism are shown below.

[Biochemistry-NEET-2021-10-exp-image]

subject: Biochemistry
question:
A 5-year-old boy presented with easy fatigability, irritability and inability to concentrate. Labs
revealed the following findings a and b (light and oil immersion respectively) on the bone
marrow aspiration. Which of the following is the most likely enzyme deficient in this
condition?
825

[Biochemistry-NEET-2021-11-image]
explanation:
The given clinical scenario of a child with easy fatiguability, irritability, and inability to
concentrate with the images showing a crumpled tissue paper appearance(Gaucher's cell) is
suggestive of Gaucher's disease. The deficient enzyme is glucocerebrosidase (also known as
beta-glucosidase). I
Gaucher's disease is an inborn error of metabolism caused due to a deficiency of beta
glucocerebrosidase. It is the most common lysosomal storage disorder and the mode of
inheritance is autosomal recessive. There is an accumulation of lipid-laden macrophages in the
spleen, liver, and other tissues.
There are three clinical subtypes - type I (chronic non-neuronopathic), type II (acute
neuronopathic), and type III (intermediate). Clinical features include anemia, thrombocytopenia,
hepatosplenomegaly, and neurological features depending on the subtype.
Gaucher's cells - These are formed as a result of the accumulation of massive amounts of
glucocerebrosides within the phagocytes. The cells have a fibrillary cytoplasm and give a crumpled
tissue paper appearance.
Option A: Deficiency of beta-hexosaminidase A is seen in Tay-Sachs disease. The deficiency of
beta-hexosaminidase A and B causes Sandhoff's disease. Clinical features are seizures,
macrocephaly, and hyperacusis.
Option C: Deficiency of sphingomyelinase leads to Niemann-Pick disease. It is characterized by
the presence of foam cells in the bone marrow. There may be pulmonary infiltrates leading to lung
failure.
842
Option D: Deficiency of N-acetylglucosaminidase leads to Sanfillipo's disease. It is a type of
mucopolysaccharidosis (MPS - III). There is an accumulation of heparan sulfate leading to severe
cognitive degeneration.

subject: Biochemistry
question:
A farmer presented with loose stools and rashes over the sun-exposed areas. Careful diet
history reveals a diet rich in maize. Which of the following deficiencies would you suspect?
explanation:
History of a maize-rich diet along with loose stools and rashes over the sun-exposed areas is
suggestive of niacin/vitamin B3 deficiency.
Niacin deficiency causes pellagra, found mostly among people eating corn/maize-based diets,
which is low in niacin and tryptophan. Excess leucine in jowar and maize disrupts the conversion
of tryptophan to niacin.
Niacin can be synthesized in the body from tryptophan. Two compounds- nicotinic acid, and
nicotinamide have the biological activity of niacin. The nicotinamide ring of the coenzymes NAD (
nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide) and NADP (nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide phosphate)
participate in oxidation/reduction reactions.
The initial symptoms of pellagra include loss of appetite, generalized weakness, irritability,
abdominal pain, and vomiting. This is followed by bright red glossitis and a characteristic
pigmented, scaly skin rash in sun-exposed areas. This rash is known as Casal’s necklace when it
forms a ring around the neck.
The 4Ds of pellagra:
• Diarrhea
• Dermatitis
• Dementia, leading to
• Death
It is diagnosed by measuring urinary levels of 2-methyl nicotinamide and 2-pyridone.
It is treated by oral supplementation of 100–200 mg of nicotinamide or nicotinic acid three times
daily for 5 days.
Casal’s necklace finding in niacin deficiency
843

[Biochemistry-NEET-2021-12-exp-image]
Other options:
Option B: Dietary pyridoxine deficiency (vitamin B6) is rare but has been observed in alcoholics,
newborn infants formulas low in vitamin B6, and women taking oral contraceptives. Isoniazid, an
anti-TB drug, leads to pyridoxine deficiency resulting in peripheral neuropathy. Hence pyridoxine
supplementation is included in ATT regimens.
Option C: Thiamine deficiency (vitamin B1) can manifest as wet beriberi (CVS features like heart
failure and edema) or Shoshin beriberi, an acute and fulminant form of wet beriberi, dry beriberi
(peripheral neuritis), or as Wernicke encephalopathy with Korsakoff psychosis (in
alcoholics). Dietary deficiency of thiamine inhibits pyruvate dehydrogenase. Chronic alcoholics
may develop potentially fatal pyruvic and lactic acidosis.
Option D: Dietary biotin deficiency is unknown but rarely, dermatitis, glossitis, loss of appetite,
and nausea may occur after consumption of large amounts of uncooked egg white, which contains
the protein avidin. It binds biotin and makes it unavailable for absorption.
Note: A similar image-based question on vitamin B3 deficiency was previously asked in NEET PG
2020. Vitamin deficiencies are a high-yield topic and are repeatedly tested every year.
844

subject: Biochemistry
question:
Which is the correct sequence of steps in the isolation of desirable protein using recombinant
DNA technology?
explanation:
The correct sequence of isolation of desirable protein in recombinant DNA technology is 2, 1, 3, 5,
4.
I) Incorporation of genes into bacteria: The gene needed to produce the desired proteins is to be
incorporated into the bacteria. This is done using cloning vectors like bacterial plasmids, phages,
or cosmids. The DNA of interest is ligated into the vector DNA and is called recombinant DNA.
This vector is then inserted into a competent host cell (eg: E.coli bacteria) where it is replicated.
II) Expression of protein and lysis of bacterial cell:  After incorporation of recombinant DNA,
bacteria express the inserted gene and produce the desired protein on a large scale along with
other indigenous bacterial proteins. To release these proteins bacterial cell is lysed.
III) SDS-PAGE (sodium dodecyl sulfate-polyacrylamide gel electrophoresis): It is the most widely
used method for determining the purity of a protein. After the proteins are released, to
separate the desirable protein, we subject it to SDS-PAGE. It separates proteins based on
molecular weight.
IV) Hybridization and blotting: It is done to confirm that the isolated protein is the desired
protein. It is done using western blotting techniques on nitrocellulose paper.
V) Protein elution - extraction of the produced protein.
The practical applications of recombinant DNA technology include:
• Gene mapping using techniques such as fluorescence in situ hybridization (FISH).
• Producing proteins such as:
854
• Interferons, tPA (tissue plasminogen activator)
• Insulin, growth hormone
• Hepatitis B vaccine
• Molecular analysis of disease, to detect gene variations and mutations.
• Restriction Fragment Length polymorphism (RFLP) and single nucleotide polymorphism (SNP)
• Microsatellite DNA polymorphisms
• Gene therapy and bone marrow transplantation

subject: Biochemistry
question: Which of the following techniques is used to detect DNA-protein interaction?
explanation:
The technique used to detect DNA-protein interaction is DNA footprinting.
The parts of DNA that are protein-bound (Eg: RNA polymerase bound to the promoter region of
DNA) are resistant to digestion by nuclease enzymes. Since the nucleases are unable to cleave
the protein-bound regions of DNA, when the cleaved DNA fragments are run on polyacrylamide
gel electrophoresis, there are missing bands indicating, protected sites of protein-bound DNA.
These missing areas represent the footprint of the protected DNA-protein complexes. Hence DNA
footprinting technique helps to identify the specific binding site of protein on a DNA sequence.
The image below illustrates the DNA footprinting technique.

[Biochemistry-INI-CET-2022-2-exp-image]
Other options:
Option B: DNA fingerprinting uses RFLPs (restriction fragment length polymorphism) or repeat
sequence DNA to identify a person's unique pattern of DNA. Polymorphisms are inherited
differences found among individuals. with this technique, individuals can be differentiated by
variations in their DNA sequence, using restriction endonucleases followed by gel electrophoresis,
to visualize RFLPs. DNA fingerprinting is used for forensic analysis of crime scenes, and for
paternity testing.
855
Option C: ELISA uses antibodies linked to certain enzymes to detect certain antigens present in
the sample.
Option D: Northern blotting technique can be used to detect RNA. Here, the mixture
of mRNA molecules is separated by electrophoresis and then transferred to a nitrocellulose
membrane and hybridized with radiolabeled probes.

subject: Biochemistry
question:
A patient was diagnosed with Ewing sarcoma with EWSR1 gene mutation. The technique
shown in the image was performed to confirm the diagnosis. Identify the technique shown in
the image.
845

[Biochemistry-INI-CET-2022-3-image]
explanation:
The technique shown in the image is FISH demonstrating a positive EWSR1 gene rearrangement,
characteristic of Ewing sarcoma. The fused region denoted by Yellow corresponds to a
normal intact gene, while a split signal pattern showing separate red and green signals is
indicative of EWSR1 gene rearrangement.
Fluorescent In Situ Hybridisation (FISH) is a simple technique for the detection of specific genetic
information on morphologically intact tissue using fluorescent probes. FISH can be performed on
prenatal samples, peripheral blood cells, touch preparations from cancer biopsies, and even fixed
archival tissue sections. It is used to detect
• numeric abnormalities of chromosomes (aneuploidy)
• subtle microdeletions and rearrangements.
• complex translocations that are not demonstrable by routine karyotyping
• gene amplification (e.g., HER2 in breast cancer or NMYC amplification in neuroblastomas)
Other options:
Option B: Karyotyping is the process by which chromosomes are isolated, stained, and examined
to find chromosomal anomalies. It is used in prenatal diagnosis, investigation of repeat pregnancy
loss, investigation of children and adults suspected of having a chromosomal disorder, and
subtyping of lymphomas and leukemias for the purpose of exact diagnosis, giving a prognosis, and
treatment.
Option C: IHC (Immunohistochemistry) is a staining method for histopathological specimens. It
helps in the diagnosis and classification of tumors based on their distinct protein expression
patterns. Eg: The expression of estrogen receptor (ER), progesterone receptor (PR), and
HER2/neu in breast cancer cells is mainly assessed by immunohistochemistry.
Option D: Sanger's technique: This was the first technique used to determine the sequence of
proteins. The first protein to be sequenced by this method was insulin. Sanger’s reagent is 1-
Flouro 2,4 Dinitro benzene. Only dipeptides and tripeptides can be sequenced by this method.

subject: Biochemistry
question:
In a patient with maple syrup urine disease, all of the following amino acids should be
restricted in diet except?
explanation:
In maple syrup urine disease (MSUD), only branched-chain amino acids are restricted, hence,
methionine does not need any restriction in diet.
Branched-chain amino acids are:
856
• Leucine (L)
• Isoleucine (I)
• Valine (V)
MSUD is an autosomal recessive disorder in which there is a partial or complete deficiency
of branched-chain ketoacid dehydrogenase complex (BCKD). This impairs the metabolism of
branched-chain amino acids. These amino acids, particularly leucine and their metabolite
accumulate in the blood, causing toxic effects on the brain. Features include feeding problems,
vomiting, ketosis, and a characteristic burnt sugar odor /maple syrup-like odor found
in urine, sweat, and cerumen.

[Biochemistry-INI-CET-2022-4-exp-image]
Laboratory investigations will show increased levels of branched-chain amino acids in the plasma
and urine. Leucine levels are usually higher than isoleucine, and valine.
Treatment involves restricted intake of branched-chain amino acids using commercially available
formulas and judicious supplementation of isoleucine and valine. Very high levels of leucine are
removed by hemodialysis.

subject: Biochemistry
question: Which of the following statements about the human genome project is false?
explanation:
The human genome project did not use next-generation sequencing.
The Human Genome Project (HGP) was an international, collaborative research program, which
involved agencies such as UNESCO, HUGO, the National Institute of Health/Department of
Energy (USA), the Medical Research Council (UK), Genethon (France), and the European Union.
The primary goal of the Human Genome Project was to determine the sequence of all nucleotide
base pairs, that make up human DNA and to create a single linear map of all genes from the
human genome. The project was active from 1990 to 2003, taking around 13 years to complete. A
total of 20500 genes were identified and mapped. Scientists used various methods
of first-generation sequencing technologies including sanger's technique, SGS technique, etc. The
HGP has produced a curated and accurate reference sequence for the majority of human
857
chromosomes. The reference sequence has provided the foundation for the use of
second-generation sequencing technologies such as array techniques.
Next-generation sequencing (NGS) is a new technology used for sequencing DNA and RNA and
mutation detection. NGS can sequence the genome faster and in a shorter period of time than
first-generation sequencing technologies. NGS allows for the detection of a spectrum of
mutations, including single nucleotide substitutions, small insertions and deletions, large genomic
duplications and deletions, and rare variations. Next-generation DNA sequencing technologies
include
• Illumina sequencing
• Roche 454 sequencing
• Ion torrent: Proton/PGM sequencing
• SOLID sequencing.

subject: Biochemistry
question: Banding technique used for dicentric chromosomes is:
explanation:
The banding technique used for dicentric chromosomes is C banding.
Heterochromatin includes non-coding regions of DNA, such as centromeric regions. The staining
of these constitutive heterochromatins, is done by C-banding using silver salt staining. A dicentric
chromosome having two centromeres can hence be identified by C banding.
Various techniques are used in karyotyping to study chromosomes. Staining with various
compounds produces various results and helps us to understand the chromosomes better.
Common techniques used are:
• Q banding - Fluorescence staining with quinacrine mustard.
• G banding - Giemsa staining after partial denaturation of chromatin. It is the most common
technique used in cytogenetics to produce a visible karyotype by staining condensed
chromosomes.
• R banding - Reverse Giemsa staining. In this, the light and dark areas are reversed.
• C banding - staining of heterochromatin with silver salts.
• T banding - staining the telomeres of chromosomes.

subject: Biochemistry
question: Affinity maturation of antibodies is because of?
explanation:
Affinity maturation of antibodies is because of somatic hypermutation.
Affinity maturation is the process by which antibodies gain
increased affinity, avidity, and anti-pathogen activity. It is a result of somatic hypermutation of
immunoglobulin genes in B cells, coupled with selection for antigen binding.
Somatic hypermutation is a cellular response by which the immune system adapts to the new
foreign elements that confront it. They do so by adding nucleotides in V gene regions of heavy and
858
light chains, resulting in a better fit of antigen with the antibodies. This hence increases the
affinity of antibodies to antigens. Due to this, the subset of plasma cells with these improved
hypervariable regions are more strongly
selected by the antigen and therefore constitute a larger part of the population of
antibody-producing cells. This process is called affinity maturation.
This process occurs in the germinal centers and proceeds for weeks after acute infection or
vaccination, or for many cycles during chronic infection. The resulting antibodies can be highly
mutated from their germline-encoded counterparts, with an increase in several orders of
magnitude in affinity for antigen compared to the corresponding naïve B cell receptors (BCRs).

subject: Biochemistry
question: Best method for detection of mutations with low allele frequency is
explanation:
Best method for detection of mutation with low allele frequency is Droplet digital PCR (ddPCR)
It is an advanced and more precise type of quantitative PCR which gives more consistent and
reproducible results. It can accurately quantify the target nucleic acid sequence within a sample. It
is useful for determining somatic mutation with low allele frequency. Mutant alleles of frequency
as low as 0.25% can also be detected using this technique.
Polymerase chain reaction (PCR) is a rapid automated method for the amplification of specific
DNA sequences (or genes). Reverse transcriptase PCR- The enzyme reverse transcriptase (RT) is
used to synthesize a complimentary copy of DNA (cDNA) from RNA and this cDNA is then used
as a template for PCR.
Other special PCR types:
Nested PCR- It involves the sequential use of two primer sets. The first primer set is used for
amplification and the target sequence for the second primer is present only on the amplicon with
the target sequence. Nested PCR is intended to reduce non-specific binding due to the
amplification of unexpected primer binding sites.

[Biochemistry-INI-CET-2022-8-exp-image]
859
Multiplex PCR- Multiple primers are used to amplify several DNA sequences simultaneously. As
this process targets multiple genes at once, a single test run gives additional information that
would otherwise require several times the reagents and technician time to perform.
The Sanger sequencing technique is used for DNA sequencing. It is based on controlled
termination of synthesis of DNA by using chain terminating agents like dideoxynucleotides.
Fluorescent In Situ Hybridisation (FISH) is a simple technique for the detection of specific genetic
information on morphologically intact tissue using fluorescent probes. FISH can be performed on
prenatal samples, peripheral blood cells, touch preparations from cancer biopsies, and even fixed
archival tissue sections.

subject: Biochemistry
question: In Sanger sequencing, ddNTP is used because:

options.a: It is used to increase DNA polymerase activity
options.c: They exhibit higher affinity for DNA polymerases
options.d: It doesn't allow further elongation of DNA

explanation:
In Sanger sequencing, dideoxynucleotides triphosphates (ddNTP) are used because it doesn't
allow further elongation of DNA.
The Sanger method is used for DNA sequencing. This method uses the mechanism of synthesis of
DNA by DNA polymerases. In the process of synthesis of DNA, the 3'-hydroxyl group of primer
reacts with an incoming dNTP to form a new phosphodiester bond. This reaction is catalyzed by
DNA polymerase.
In the Sanger sequencing method, ddNTP is used because it halts the DNA synthesis. ddNTPs can
bind to the template strand but lack the 3'-hydroxyl group which is required to add the next
nucleotide. The products of this reaction are a mixture of DNA strands of varying lengths. This
mixture is then separated using polyacrylamide gel electrophoresis and autoradiography. This
produces a pattern of bands from which the DNA sequence can be read.
The image given below shows the steps involved in Sanger's method.

[Biochemistry-INI-CET-2022-9-exp-image]
Sanger’s technique was the first technique used to determine the sequence of proteins. The first
protein to be sequenced by this method was insulin. Sanger’s reagent is 1- fluoro 2,4 dinitro
benzene.
860

subject: Biochemistry
question:
847
Misincorporation of uracil into DNA is seen in deficiency of:
explanation:
Misincorporation of uracil into DNA is seen in the deficiency of folate.
Folate deficiency leads to decreased thymidylate synthesis from deoxyuridylate due to deficient
methylation of deoxyuridine monophosphate (dUMP) to thymidine monophosphate (TMP). The
methylation of dUMP to TMP is catalyzed by thymidylate synthase and
requires tetrahydrofolate (THFA) as a methyl group donor. This decreased TMP synthesis
can result in an imbalance of deoxyribonucleotides and hence leading to excessive uracil
misincorporation into DNA during replication and repair.
Folic acid (vitamin B9) is a water-soluble vitamin, and its active form is tetrahydrofolate (THFA).
It acts as a carrier of one-carbon groups. Tetrahydrofolate receives one-carbon
fragments from donors such as serine, glycine, and histidine and transfers them to intermediates
in the synthesis of amino acids, purines, and pyrimidines.

[Biochemistry-INI-CET-2022-10-exp-image]
Folate deficiency results in:
• Megaloblastic anemia
• Homocystenemia
• Neural tube defects (spina bifida, Anencephaly) in the fetus.

subject: Biochemistry
question: Deamination of methylated cytosine forms which of the following?
explanation:
Deamination of methylated cytosine forms thymine.
Deamination is the removal of an amino group(NH2) from a chemical compound. Enzymes that
catalyze this reaction are called deaminases. Deamination of 5-methylcytosine forms thymine.
Other significant deaminations are given below:
861
• Deamination of adenosine forms inosine. This is a metabolic step involved in uric acid formation
by purine catabolism. It is mediated by the enzyme adenosine deaminase (ADA). ADA deficiency
leads to sparse or defective T cells and B cells and patients suffer from Severe combined
immunodeficiency (SCID).
• Deamination of cytosine leads to uracil formation - The production of apolipoprotein B-48
(apoB-48) in the intestine is the result of RNA editing in the intestine. This involves
post-transcriptional deamination of cytosine to uracil by enzyme cytidine deaminase.
This converts sense codon (CAA) in the mRNA to nonsense codon (UAA)at a single specific
site. This edited mRNA translates to apoB-48.
• Deamination of guanine forms xanthine. This is a metabolic step involved in uric acid formation
by purine catabolism.

subject: Biochemistry
question:
Which of the following types of inhibition is depicted in the given Lineweaver-Burk plot?

[Biochemistry-INI-CET-2022-12-image]
explanation:
The given graph shows competitive inhibition, in which the value of Vmax remains constant and
the Km value increases.
• Km is the substrate concentration at which velocity is half maximum.
• Vmax is the maximum velocity of a reaction.
Competitive inhibition is when the inhibitor binds reversibly to the substrate-binding site and
competes with the substrate for the active enzyme-binding site. This effect of competitive
inhibition can be overcome by increasing the substrate concentration by which maximum velocity
can still be achieved. Therefore, Vmax is unaltered in this type of inhibition. However, the
concentration of the substrate required to achieve half of the maximum velocity is increased.
Therefore, Km is increased in this condition.
The graph shows the changes in Vmax and Km in competitive inhibition.
Inhibition

ses

ses
862

[Biochemistry-INI-CET-2022-12-exp-image]
Other options:
Option B: Non-competitive inhibition is by binding inhibitors to enzymes in locations
remote from the active site. Increasing the concentration of the substrate does not overcome
non-competitive inhibition. Thus, noncompetitive inhibitors decrease the Vmax of the reaction.
They do not interfere with the binding of the substrate to the enzyme. Thus, the enzyme shows
the same Km in the presence or absence of noncompetitive inhibitors.
The graph shows the changes in Vmax and Km in non-competitive inhibition.

[Biochemistry-INI-CET-2022-12-exp-image]
Option C: In uncompetitive inhibition, inhibitors bind with the enzyme-substrate intermediate
complex, and inhibit it. This leads to decrease in both Vmax and Km. An example is the inhibition
of placental alkaline phosphate (ALP) by phenylalanine.
Option D: Suicidal inhibition is a special type of irreversible inhibition. The inhibitor here makes
use of the enzyme's own reaction mechanism to inactivate it and is hence known as
mechanism-based inactivation. An example is allopurinol, which is oxidized by xanthine oxidase
863
to alloxanthine. Alloxanthine in turn is a more potent inhibitor of xanthine oxidase.

subject: Biochemistry
question: Glycogen phosphorylase is activated by:
explanation:
Glycogen phosphorylase is activated by calcium in the muscle.
Glycogen phosphorylase is the rate-limiting enzyme of glycogenolysis and is decreased in the fed
state.
In the muscle, the increased cytosolic calcium ion concentration initiates both contraction and
glycogenolysis. Muscle phosphorylase kinase has four different subunits, α, β, γ, and δ. The α and
β subunits are phosphorylated by cAMP-dependent protein kinase. The δ subunit is identical to
the calcium-binding protein calmodulin and binds four calcium ions. Calcium coordinates the
activation of Glycogen Phosphorylase with the contraction of the muscle.
Glycogen phosphorylase catalyzes the cleavage of α1 → 4 linkages of glycogen to single units of
glucose 1-phosphate. It uses pyridoxal phosphate as the coenzyme. Different isoenzymes of
glycogen phosphorylase are seen in the liver, muscles, and brain.
Other options:
Option B: During glycogenolysis, glucose 6-phosphate allosterically inhibits glycogen
phosphorylase.
Option C: Insulin entirely suppresses glycogenolysis in the skeletal muscle and liver.
Option D: In glycogenolysis protein phosphatase is inactivated by inhibitor proteins in response to
cAMP, to maintain the glycogen phosphorylase in an active state.

subject: Biochemistry
question: Mutation of the gene of cystic fibrosis occurs with:
explanation:
The mutant gene of cystic fibrosis (CF gene) produces a mutant CFTR protein with the deletion of
a phenylalanine residue at 508 (F508del), characterised by impaired chloride secretion.
Cystic fibrosis is a condition with an autosomal recessive mode of inheritance. It results due to a
defective mutation in the gene encoding for cystic fibrosis transmembrane conductance regulator
(CF gene). It plays an important role in mucociliary transport along the respiratory mucosa. It also
helps to regulate the amount of exocrine gland secretion.
The defect in the ion channel results in the production of hyper-viscous, adherent secretions
which can obstruct the airways. Organisms such as Pseudomonas aeruginosa and Staphylococcus
aureus can grow in the sputum. The exocrine pancreas can undergo fibrotic scarring, cyst
formation, and acinar tissue loss. The viscid secretions can lead to pancreatic duct obstruction.
The flow of digestive enzymes to the duodenum is hence impaired.
Symptoms include sinusitis, steatorrhea, and abdominal pain. Distal intestinal obstruction is a
complication due to difficulty in excreting intestinal contents. In neonates, it can present as
meconium ileus.
Detection of high levels of trypsinogen is used in newborn screening. Chloride level >60mEq/L
in the sweat of affected patients is diagnostic. Hyponatremic hypochloremic alkalosis is seen.
864
Postural drainage, short-term antibiotics, and inhaled bronchodilators can be used for medical
management. The definitive treatment is lung transplantation.
The image below shows the features of the disease.

[Biochemistry-INI-CET-2022-14-exp-image]

subject: Biochemistry
question:
A baby is brought to the OPD with complaints of lethargy and poor feeding. On examination,
it is noted that the urine had a smell of burnt sugar. A defect in which of the following
enzymes causes this disease?
explanation:
The diagnosis, in this case, is that of maple syrup urine disease (MSUD). This disease occurs due
to a defect of the enzyme branched-chain keto acid dehydrogenase.
MSUD is an autosomal recessive disorder that occurs due to a deficiency of branched-chain
ketoacid dehydrogenase complex. This impairs the metabolism of branched-chain amino acids
valine, leucine, isoleucine, and alloisoleucine. These amino acids, particularly leucine and its
metabolite accumulate in the blood, causing toxic effects on the brain.
The affected child develops poor feeding and vomiting a few days after birth. There is a period of
hypertonicity with a period of flaccidity in between. These appear as repetitive boxing and
bicycling movements. The babies may also have hypoglycemia, but this is not corrected by the
administration of glucose. The urine, sweat, and cerumen of the baby have a sweetish maple
syrup-like odor.
Laboratory investigations will show increased levels of branched-chain amino acids in the plasma
and urine. Leucine levels are usually higher than isoleucine, valine, and alloisoleucine. Since the
patient excretes ketone bodies in the urine, the urine will be positive for the
dinitrophenylhydrazine test.
Treatment involves controlled intake of branched-chain amino acids using commercially available
formulas and judicious supplementation of isoleucine and valine. Very high levels of leucine are
removed by hemodialysis.

subject: Biochemistry
question: All are true about the hexose monophosphate shunt, except:
explanation:
865
The hexose monophosphate (HMP) shunt produces ribose and does not utilize it.
The pentose phosphate pathway, also known as the HMP shunt pathway, is an alternative route
for the metabolism of glucose. It contributes in two major ways:
• Formation of NADPH for the synthesis of fatty acids and steroids and maintenance of reduced
glutathione for antioxidant activity.
• Formation of ribose for the synthesis of nucleic acid and nucleotides
The enzymes for this pathway are found in the cytosol. There are two phases:
• Irreversible oxidative phase: Glucose-6-phosphate is dehydrogenated and decarboxylated to
form ribulose-5-phosphate which in turn gets converted to ribose-5-phosphate.
• Reversible non-oxidative phase: Ribulose-5-phosphate is converted back to glucose-6-phosphate.

[Biochemistry-INI-CET-2022-16-exp-image]

subject: Biochemistry
question: Which of the following enzymes use selenocysteine?
explanation:
The enzymes that use selenocysteine are glutathione peroxidase, deiodinase, and thioredoxin
reductase.
Selenocysteine is the 21st amino acid coded by the UGA codon, which is normally a stop codon. It
is formed by co-translational modification. The protein synthetic apparatus can identify a
selenocysteine-encoding UGA by the presence of the selenocysteine insertion sequence (SECIS) in
the untranslated region of mRNA.
Enzymes such as thioredoxin reductase, glutathione peroxidase, and deiodinase use
selenocysteine to catalyze redox reactions. Defects in human selenoproteins are associated with
tumorigenesis and atherosclerosis. It can cause cardiomyopathy due to selenium deficiency, which
is known as Keshan disease.
Glutathione synthase is the second enzyme in the synthesis of glutathione. Glutathione plays an
important role in oxidative stress, redox signalling, and regulates cell proliferation and apoptosis.
866

subject: Biochemistry
question:
A patient presented with reduced levels of high-density lipoprotein, and ABCA1 mutation. On
examination, tonsils appeared as shown in the image. What is the diagnosis?

[Biochemistry-INI-CET-2022-18-image]
explanation:
The diagnosis in this case with reduced levels of high-density lipoprotein (HDL), ABCA1 mutation,
and orange tonsils on examination is Tangier's disease. It is caused by a defect in ABCA1
receptors.
ABCA1 plays a role in the efflux of cholesterol from peripheral cells of HDL. LCAT (lecithin
cholesterol acyltransferase) converts cholesterol to cholesterol esters, which accumulate in the
core of HDL. Thus, defective ABCA1 will lead to low levels of HDL in the blood.
Other options:
Option B: Abetalipoproteinemia is a hypolipoproteinemia caused by a defect in the MTP gene.
Here, loading apolipoprotein B with lipids is defective. Hence, few very low density-lipoproteins
(VLDLs) or chylomicrons are formed, leading to the accumulation of triacylglycerols in the liver
and intestine.
Option C: Hyperlipoproteinemia is a disorder that causes the body to metabolize lipids
incorrectly. This results in hyperlipidemia and can lead to the development of multiple small
xanthomas in the skin.
Option D: Acid lipase deficiency can lead to Wolman's disease which is a lysosomal storage
disease. The pathognomic feature of this condition is adrenal calcification. So patients generally
present with adrenal insufficiency and are neonates.

subject: Biochemistry
question:
A baby is being evaluated for delayed developmental milestones. On examination, the child
has hepatosplenomegaly. A microscopic image of the bone marrow evaluation is shown
below. What is the diagnosis?
850

[Biochemistry-INI-CET-2022-19-image]
explanation:
The microscopic image of the bone marrow biopsy shows Gaucher cells and the diagnosis is
Gaucher disease.
Gaucher disease is a lysosomal storage disease with autosomal recessive inheritance. The
condition results due to the deficiency of acid β-glucosidase. This results in the accumulation of
glucosylceramide (a glycolipid substrate) in the reticuloendothelial cells. There is progressive
infiltration of the liver, spleen, and bone marrow.
There are three clinical types:
• Type 1: Adult (non-neuronopathic form)
• Type 2: Infantile (acute neuronopathic form)
• Type 3: Juvenile (subacute neuronopathic form)
Type 1 is the most common. It presents with a myriad of symptoms, such as bruising secondary
to thrombocytopenia, fatigue due to anemia, hepatosplenomegaly, and bone pain. Lytic lesions
and osteosclerosis of the femur, ribs, and pelvis may also be seen. A few patients may have
pulmonary involvement. Growth retardation may be observed in cases presenting in the first
decade.
Type 2 is rare, and presents with hypertonia, strabismus, and organomegaly. Neurodegeneration
is the characteristic feature. Stridor and failure to thrive can also be the presenting features.
867
Ultimately, death can occur due to respiratory arrest.
Type 3 presents with intermediate symptoms, as seen in the other two types. By the age of 10-15
years, the child succumbs to death.
Gaucher cells  are reticuloendothelial cells with glucosylceramide, and have a wrinkled-paper
appearance due to intracytoplasmic substrate inclusions. These cells stain strongly with periodic
acid-Schiff (PAS) stain. This is characteristic of Gaucher's disease.
Other options:
Option B - Acid sphingomyelinase deficiency results in Niemann-Pick disease types A and B. Both
are autosomal recessive conditions. Type A has a neurodegenerative course, while type B is
non-neuronopathic. The former presents with hepatosplenomegaly, lymphadenopathy, and
psychomotor retardation. Type B is associated with hepatosplenomegaly and mild pulmonary
involvement.
Option-C - Hunter disease is an X-linked inherited disorder due to a deficiency of the enzyme
iduronate sulfatase. It leads to the accumulation of dermatan and heparan sulfate. Clinical
features involve coarse facies, cardiovascular involvement, and distinctive skin lesions.
Option-D - Hurler/Scheie disease is a mucopolysaccharidosis that has childhood and adult onset.
It presents with cognitive degeneration.

subject: Biochemistry
question: Refsum disease is due to:

options.a: Absence of alpha oxidation in peroxisomes
options.b: Absence of beta oxidation in peroxisomes
options.c: Absence of alpha oxidation in lysosomes
options.d: Absence of beta oxidation in lysosomes

explanation:
Refsum disease is due to the absence of alpha oxidation in peroxisomes.
Refsum's disease is a rare autosomal recessive disorder. The defective enzyme is phytanoyl CoA
hydroxylase, which causes alpha oxidation of fatty acids and leads to elevated levels of phytanic
acid in the blood.
Clinical features are dysmorphic facies and severe neurological symptoms such
as polyneuropathy, retinitis pigmentosa, nerve deafness, ichthyosis, and cerebellar ataxia.
Restricted dietary intake of phytanic acid reduces the symptoms. Management includes avoiding
sources of phytanic acid such as dairy products, meat, nuts, and green leafy vegetables.

subject: Biochemistry
question: Choose the right sequence of steps in the conversion of cholesterol to testosterone.
explanation:
The correct sequence of steps in the conversion of cholesterol to testosterone is:
b. 20, 22 desmolase
d. 3 beta-hydroxy steroid dehydrogenase
a. 17 alpha-hydroxylase
c. 17, 20 lyase
The step of synthesis of sex steroids is illustrated in the diagram below.
868

[Biochemistry-INI-CET-2022-21-exp-image]

subject: Biochemistry
question:
Which of the following describes the relationship between the melting temperature (Tm) of
DNA and the concentration of G-C and A-T?

options.a: No relation of GC with Tm

explanation:
The correct statement about the melting temperature of DNA (Tm) is that it increases with more
G-C concentration.
On heating the DNA, the temperature at which one-half of the helical structure loses its structure
defines the melting temperature Tm. The two strands of the double helix structure of the DNA get
separated when the hydrogen bonds between the base pairs are disrupted. Thus, the Tm is
influenced by the concentrationbase composition of the DNA.
Since G-C pairing has 3 hydrogen bonds and A-T pairing has 2 hydrogen bonds, DNA rich in G-C
base pairs requires higher temperatures to denature in comparison to DNA rich in A-T base pairs.

subject: Biochemistry
question:
An elderly patient on a vegetarian diet presenting with easy fatigability and pallor was
investigated. Peripheral smear showed findings of macrocytic anemia. What is the probable
cause of anemia in this patient? Other lab findings are as follows:
explanation:
The probable cause of anemia in this patient of folate deficiency.
The given clinical scenario of macrocytic anemia in a vegetarian with elevated homocysteine levels
is due to megaloblastic anemia, which can be caused by the deficiency of vitamin B12 or folic acid.
Vitamin B12 deficiency is associated with raised serum methylmalonic acid (MMA) levels, which
are normal in this patient; hence, folic acid deficiency is the cause of megaloblastic anemia in this
patient.
Folic acid deficiency is associated with the following clinical and laboratory findings:
• Raised MCV (normal range: 80–100 fL) - macrocytosis
• Normal MCHC (normal range: 33–36 g/dL)
• Increased MCH
• Elevated RDW
869
• Decreased reticulocyte count - due to destruction of abnormal erythroid precursors
• Elevated homocysteine level
• Normal MMA level
where MCV, mean corpuscular volume; MCHC, mean corpuscular hemoglobin concentration;
mean corpuscular hemoglobin, MCH; RDW, red cell distribution width
In vitamin B12 deficiency, neurological abnormalities, elevated homocysteine, and elevated MMA
levels are seen.

subject: Biochemistry
question: Zellweger syndrome is due to a defect in the biogenesis of:
explanation:
Zellweger syndrome is due to a defect in the biogenesis of peroxisomes.
Zellweger syndrome is also known as cerebrohepatorenal syndrome. It is caused by mutations in
the PEX family of genes. This results in defective beta-oxidation of very long-chain fatty acids.
The child presents with
• Dysmorphic facial features.
• Weakness, hypotonia.
• Neonatal seizures
• Eye abnormalities.
Biochemical findings will show :
• Accumulation of very-long-chain fatty acids, phytanic acid
• Abnormalities in the synthesis of bile acids
• Marked reduction of plasmalogens
There is no appropriate treatment for Zellweger syndrome. It is very severe and infants rarely
survive beyond a few months.

subject: Biochemistry
question: True statement about the action of enhancers of gene expression?

options.a: Work only when in close conjugation with promoter region
options.b: Work only when in upstream of promoter region
options.c: They are very specific to the gene
options.d:
Recruit chromatin and modify regulatory mechanisms
852

explanation:
The correct statement about the action of enhancers is that they recruit chromatin and modify
regulatory mechanisms.
A promoter is a sequence on DNA where RNA polymerase binds and acts as the start signal for
transcription. They are present upstream or downstream but close to the gene that is undergoing
transcription.
Enhancers or repressors are proteins that regulate the expression of a gene
by recruiting chromatin(DNA bound with histones) and modifying regulatory
mechanisms and making it more or less accessible to transcription by RNA polymerase. They may
be located upstream or downstream and can be present at any distance from the promoter region.
These are not specific to any single gene and interact with many other proteins.
870

subject: Biochemistry
question: Increased H+ ions in the intermembrane space of mitochondria are due to?

options.d: Water retention in inner mitochondrial space

explanation:
Increased H+ ions in the intermembrane space of mitochondria are due to decreased oxidative
phosphorylation.
ATP is synthesized in mitochondria by a process called oxidative phosphorylation. The oxidation
of substrates like carbohydrates, amino acids, or fats produces reducing equivalents (NADH,
FADH2). These equivalents enter the electron transport chain and transfer a proton (H+) to the
inter-membrane space. The inner mitochondrial membrane is not permeable to protons leading to
the accumulation of H+ in the intermembrane space. The flow of these protons through the Fo
releases the energy that phosphorylates ADP to form ATP.
Drugs that inhibit oxidative phosphorylation like oligomycin inhibit the flow of protons through
Fo causing an increase in H+ ions in the intermembrane space.

subject: Biochemistry
question: Fructosamine is obtained by glycation of:
explanation:
Fructosamine is obtained by the glycation of albumin.
Fructosamine is a measure of non-enzymatic glycation of circulating proteins including albumin,
globulins, and lipoproteins. It is now used as a  reasonable alternative to HbA1c measurement
when it is not reliable. Since albumin is the most abundant of the serum proteins, fructosamine is
predominantly a measure of glycated albumin.
Fructosamine is used as a short-term marker of glucose control. It reflects the average glycemia
over the preceding 2 to 3 weeks. HbA1c reflects glucose control over a period of the preceding 8 to
12 weeks.
871

subject: Biochemistry
question:
A patient from a Mediterranean country visits Africa, where he develops malaria. He is
treated with primaquine and later develops hemolytic anemia. Deficiency of an enzyme
involved in which of the following pathways could be the cause?
explanation:
The deficiency of glucose-6-phosphate dehydrogenase (G6PD) enzyme, which is involved in the
hexose monophosphate (HMP) pathway, could be the cause behind this presentation.
G6PD is an enzyme that plays a critical role in redox reactions that occur in all aerobic cells. It is
the only source of NADPH in erythrocytes, which helps in the formation of reduced glutathione.
This reduced form protects erythrocytes against oxidative stress.
G6PD deficiency is an X-linked inheritance disorder. Most patients with this condition are
asymptomatic. But when exposed to oxidative agents, they are prone to develop acute hemolytic
anemia. The various oxidative triggers include:
• Fava beans
• Infections
• Drugs such as primaquine, dapsone, and nitrofurantoin
A hemolytic attack can present with weakness and abdominal/ lumbar pain. Later, the patient can
develop jaundice, hemoglobinemia, and hemoglobinuria. Investigations will show elevated levels
of lactate dehydrogenase (LDH) and low levels of plasma haptoglobin. Anisocytosis and
spherocytes can be seen on peripheral smear. Acute renal failure is a complication seen in adults.
Other options:
875
Option A - Glycolysis, the main pathway of glucose, can occur either aerobically or anaerobically.
A deficiency of the enzyme pyruvate kinase can present as hemolytic anemia. Fatigue can be the
presentation when there is phosphofructokinase. Both these are enzymes of glycolysis.
Option B - Gluconeogenesis is the process by which non-carbohydrate precursors are used to
synthesize glucose. Pyruvate carboxylase, phosphoenolpyruvate carboxykinase, and
glucose-6-phosphatase are the enzymes in the pathway. Glucose-6-phosphatase deficiency leads to
von Gierke disease, which is a glycogen storage disorder. Defective gluconeogenesis results in
hypoglycemia.
Option D - Luebering–Rapoport pathway is a shunt mechanism that generates and
dephosphorylates 2,3-bisphosphoglycerate (2,3-BPG). 2,3-BPG mutase is the enzyme involved. A
deficiency of this enzyme can cause erythrocytosis.

subject: Biochemistry
question: Which of the following disorders follows autosomal recessive inheritance pattern?
explanation:
Cystic fibrosis is a condition with an autosomal recessive mode of inheritance.
It results due to a defective mutation in the gene encoding for cystic fibrosis transmembrane
conductance regulator (CFTR gene). It functions as an epithelial anion channel. It plays an
important role in mucociliary transport along the respiratory mucosa. It also helps regulate the
amount of exocrine gland secretion.
The defect in the ion channel results in the production of hyper viscous, adherent secretions
which can obstruct the airways. Organisms such as Pseudomonas aeruginosa and Staphylococcus
aureus can grow in the sputum. The exocrine pancreas can undergo fibrotic scarring, cyst
formation, and acinar tissue loss. The viscid secretions can lead to pancreatic duct obstruction.
The flow of digestive enzymes to the duodenum is hence impaired.
Symptoms include sinusitis, steatorrhea, and abdominal pain. Distal intestinal obstruction is a
complication due to difficulty in excreting intestinal contents. In neonates, it can present as
meconium ileus.
Detection of high levels of trypsinogen is used in newborn screening. Chloride level >60mEq/L
in the sweat of affected patients is diagnostic. Hyponatremic hypochloremic alkalosis and
hypokalemia may also be seen.
Postural drainage, short-term antibiotics, and inhaled bronchodilators can be used for medical
management. The definitive treatment is lung transplantation.
In autosomal recessive inheritance, multiple affected members are seen in the same generation.
Other generations are not affected. The following pedigree chart depicts an autosomal recessive
pattern of inheritance.
876

[Biochemistry-NEET-2022-2-exp-image]
Other options:
Option A - Huntington's disease is a trinucleotide (CAG) repeat disorder with an autosomal
dominant mode of inheritance. It is characterized by involuntary movements known as chorea.
Option B - Treacher Collins syndrome is an autosomal dominant disorder that presents as
mandibular and malar hypoplasia.
Option D - Achondroplasia has an autosomal dominant inheritance pattern and presents with
short limbs, saddle nose, and spinal deformity.

subject: Biochemistry
question:
Which of the following helps in the transport of fatty acids across the inner mitochondrial
membrane?
explanation:
Carnitine is responsible for the transport of fatty acids or long-chain acyl-CoA across the inner
mitochondrial membrane.
Fatty acids are oxidized in the mitochondria to produce acetyl-CoA to produce energy. They must
first be converted to acyl-CoA (active fatty acid) before they can be catabolized.
Carnitine palmitoyltransferase - I (CPT- I) transports acyl-CoA across the inner mitochondrial
membrane only in the presence of carnitine. The acyl group of acyl-CoA is transferred to carnitine.
The acylcarnitine so formed can penetrate the inner mitochondrial membrane via the
carnitine-acylcarnitine translocase. This transporter transfers the acyl group back to CoA,
restoring acyl-CoA and liberating carnitine, which is moved back to the intermembrane space.
Carnitine palmitoyltransferase -II catalyzes this reaction. The deficiency of this enzyme is a
common cause of recurrent myoglobinuria.
Carnitine deficiency can lead to hypoglycemia and restrictive or dilated cardiomyopathy. It can
also cause hepatic steatosis.
877

[Biochemistry-NEET-2022-3-exp-image]
Other options:
Option A - Acyl carrier protein is a component of the fatty acid synthase complex involved in the
biosynthesis of fatty acids. It has vitamin pantothenic acid (vitamin B5).
Option C - Lecithin-cholesterol acyltransferase (LCAT) is present in the plasma and plays an
important role in the esterification of cholesterol. Deficiency leads to low levels of lysolecithin and
cholesteryl esters.
Option D - The long-chain fatty acids are bound to albumin in the plasma. But albumin does not
transport fatty acids across the inner mitochondrial membrane. Albumin is a negative acute-phase
reactant synthesized by the liver. It is a carrier for acidic drugs.

subject: Biochemistry
question: A single mutation in a nucleotide base pair resulting in a termination codon is known as?
explanation:
A single mutation in a nucleotide base pair resulting in a termination codon is known as
a nonsense mutation.
A codon is a sequence of three bases present on the mRNA (messenger RNA), and it codes for
amino acids. Out of the 64 codons possible, only 61 code for the 20 amino acids. Three codons,
UAA, UAG, and UGA  do not code for any of the 20 amino acids. These are known as
nonsense/punctator/termination/stop codons and function as translation termination signals. The
UGA codon is traditionally considered a stop codon, but it codes for selenocysteine (21st amino
acid).
The tRNA (transfer RNA) contains the anticodon which recognizes specific codons present on the
mRNA. The sequence of the anticodon is complementary to the codon. The pairing between the
codon and anticodon can wobble at the third base. For example, GGU, GGC, and GGA all pair
with the anticodon CCI. The wobble phenomenon along with degeneracy reduces the effect of
mutations.
A mutation involving a single nucleotide base is known as a point mutation. A nonsense mutation
is a type of point mutation where a codon previously coding for an amino acid is changed to a stop
878
codon. This results in premature termination of translation and the formation of a truncated
protein. mRNA containing premature stops are removed by the nonsense-mediated degradation
pathway.
Other options:
Option A: A missense mutation is a type of point mutation where the new codon, formed by the
changed base, codes for a different amino acid.
Option C: Stop mutation is a term that can be used interchangeably with nonsense mutation.
Here, nonsense mutation is the best answer.
Option D: Silent mutations are point mutations where the new codon formed still codes for the
same amino acid as the codon before the mutation.

subject: Biochemistry
question: DNA packing is done by which of the following ?
explanation:
The proteins involved in DNA packing are histones.
Chromatin contains double-stranded DNA molecules and small basic proteins known as histones.
In addition, nonhistone proteins are present that are involved in DNA replication and repair, and
RNA synthesis. Histones play a role in condensing the DNA.
Nucleosomes are the organizational unit of chromatin and contain an octamer of histones around
which 150 base pairs of DNA are packed. There are various types of histone proteins. H2A, H2B,
H3, and H4 groups are components of nucleosomes and are known as core histones. They
undergo post-translational modifications such as acetylation and methylation. This plays a critical
part in chromatin structure and function.
The DNA in the nucleosome is arranged in a left-handed helical configuration over the histone
octamer.

[Biochemistry-NEET-2022-5-exp-image]
Other options:
879
Option B - Glycoproteins are proteins with oligosaccharide chains (glycans) bound to amino acids.
Certain plasma proteins, peptide hormones, and cell membrane proteins are examples. Structural
changes in the glycoproteins present in cancer cells may play a role in metastasis.
Option C - Nucleic acids can be either DNA or RNA. DNA is thought to be arranged in a
double-stranded helix model. RNA on the other hand exists as a single strand. There are different
types of RNA such as mRNA (messenger RNA), tRNA (transfer RNA), and rRNA (ribosomal RNA).
Option D - Adenine is a purine base that when linked to ribose forms adenosine, a nucleoside.
When a nucleoside is esterified with a phosphate group a nucleotide is formed. Guanine is the
other purine base. Cytosine is the pyrimidine base common to DNA and RNA. Thymine is present
only in DNA and Uracil in RNA.

subject: Biochemistry
question:
An adolescent male patient presents to you with exercise intolerance. He gives a history of
developing cramps on exertion. Which of the following enzyme deficiencies could be the
cause?
explanation:
The clinical scenario points to a diagnosis of McArdle disease, which results due to a deficiency of
the enzyme myophosphorylase (muscle glycogen phosphorylase).
McArdle disease, also known as type V glycogen storage disorder, is an autosomal recessive
condition. It typically presents during late childhood or in the second decade of life. The muscle
glycogen phosphorylase enzyme deficiency interferes with ATP generation by glycogenolysis in the
muscles. This causes muscle glycogen accumulation.
Symptoms are exercise intolerance associated with muscle cramps. A second wind phenomenon,
where the symptoms subside after a short period of rest, can also be seen. Myoglobinuria
secondary to rhabdomyolysis leads to the passage of burgundy-colored urine in some patients.
Rarely, it can present during infancy as hypotonia, muscle weakness, and respiratory
complications. It is generally fatal.
Muscle biopsy to measure glycogen content is the standard diagnosis. Avoiding strenuous exercise
prevents symptoms. Glucose administration before exercise can improve tolerance. Vitamin B6 is
also recommended.
Other options:
Option B - Hexokinase is a glycolytic enzyme. Its deficiency leads to hemolytic anemia.
Splenomegaly may be another presenting feature.
Option C - Glucose-6-phosphatase is an enzyme involved in gluconeogenesis. Its deficiency leads
to von Gierke disease also known as type I glycogen storage disease.
Option D - Hepatic glycogen phosphorylase deficiency results in type VI glycogen storage disease,
also known as Hers disease.

subject: Biochemistry
question:
Which of the following enzyme activities can be estimated in red blood cells to diagnose
vitamin B2 deficiency?
explanation:
Glutathione reductase activity in red blood cells can be estimated to diagnose vitamin B2
deficiency.
880
Vitamin B2, also known as riboflavin, is a water-soluble vitamin. Its active forms are FMN (flavin
mononucleotide) and FAD (flavin adenine dinucleotide). Glutathione reductase is a
FAD-containing enzyme (flavoenzyme). Hence, riboflavin deficiency can be detected by measuring
erythrocyte glutathione reductase activity. Red blood cell and urinary concentrations of riboflavin
can also be estimated to detect a deficiency.
Deficiency of riboflavin can present as anemia, corneal vascularization, mucocutaneous lesions,
and personality changes. Milk and other dairy products are important dietary sources.
Other options:
Option A - Transketolase contains thiamine diphosphate as a coenzyme. Estimation of erythrocyte
apotransketolase (enzyme protein) after in vitro addition of thiamine diphosphate, can be used to
assess thiamine nutritional status. Thiamine deficiency can result in peripheral neuritis, beriberi,
and Wernicke encephalopathy with Korsakoff psychosis.
Option C - Kynureninase is an enzyme that requires pyridoxal phosphate as a coenzyme. It
converts 3-hydroxykynurenine, a tryptophan metabolite, to 3-hydroxyanthranilate. In vitamin B6
deficiency, this is impaired and results in the formation and subsequent urinary excretion of
xanthurenate. This can be used for diagnosis.
Option D - Pyruvate is decarboxylated by pyruvate dehydrogenase for which thiamine
diphosphate acts as the cofactor. Therefore thiamine deficiency results in the accumulation of
pyruvate. This leads to pyruvic and lactic acidosis, commonly seen in alcoholics.

[Biochemistry-NEET-2022-7-exp-image]

subject: Biochemistry
question:
The following is due to the deficiency of?
873

[Biochemistry-NEET-2022-8-image]
explanation:
The given image depicts Bitot's spots characteristically seen in deficiency of vitamin A. Bitot's
spots are seen as white patches of keratinized epithelium on the sclera. It is more commonly
bilateral and on the temporal side.
Vitamin A deficiency arises mostly due to the inadequate amounts of vitamin A in diet. It can
present as night blindness, conjunctival xerosis, Bitot's spots, and keratomalacia. In later stages, it
881
can lead to xerophthalmia. It also increases the risk of developing measles, diarrhea, and
dysentery. Skin manifestations include xerosis, scaling, and dermomalacia. Phrynoderma may
also be seen as keratotic follicular papules affecting the thighs and upper arms. It is shown in the
image below.

[Biochemistry-NEET-2022-8-exp-image]
In patients above the age of 1 year, vitamin A deficiency is treated with 200,000 IU of vitamin A
orally or 100,000 IU by intramuscular route. It should be repeated the next day and two weeks
later.
Vitamin A is a fat-soluble vitamin that plays an essential role in vision, reproduction, and
maintenance of epithelial integrity. The various forms of vitamin A include retinol (storage form),
retinal, retinoic acid, and beta-carotene. It is stored in the liver perisinusoidal stellate (Ito)
cells as retinyl ester (retinyl palmitate).
Other options:
Option A - Vitamin C is a water-soluble vitamin that aids in iron absorption and collagen
maturation. It cannot be synthesized in humans because of the absence of L-gulonolactone
oxidase. Vitamin C deficiency results in scurvy.
Option C - Vitamin B deficiency has clinical manifestations pertaining to each type.
Option D - Vitamin E deficiency can present as posterior column and spinocerebellar symptoms.
Peripheral neuropathy, ophthalmoplegia, and pigmented retinopathy are other features seen.

subject: Biochemistry
question:
A child presents to you with anemia, thrombocytopenia, and bony pain. On examination,
hepatosplenomegaly was noticed. If light microscopy of bone marrow aspirate reveals
"crumpled tissue paper" appearance, what is the enzyme defect?
explanation:
The clinical scenario points to a diagnosis of Gaucher's disease, where there is a defect in the
enzyme glucocerebrosidase.
Gaucher's disease is a lysosomal storage disease with autosomal recessive inheritance. The
condition results due to the deficiency of beta-glucocerebrosidase. This results in the
accumulation of glucosylceramide (glycolipid substrate) in the reticuloendothelial cells. There is
progressive infiltration of the liver, spleen, and bone marrow.
882
There are three clinical types:
• Type 1: Adult (non-neuronopathic) form
• Type 2: Infantile (acute neuronopathic) form
• Type 3: Juvenile (subacute neuronopathic) form
Type 1 is the most common. It presents with a myriad of symptoms, such as bruising secondary to
thrombocytopenia, fatigue due to anemia, hepatosplenomegaly, and bone pain. Pathologic
fractures and lytic lesions of the femur, ribs, and pelvis may also be seen. A few patients may have
pulmonary involvement. Growth retardation may be observed in cases presenting in the first
decade.
Type 2 is rare, and presents with hypertonia, strabismus, and organomegaly. Neurodegeneration
is the characteristic feature. Stridor and failure to thrive can also be the presenting features.
Ultimately, death can occur due to respiratory arrest.
Type 3 presents with intermediate symptoms, as seen in the other two types. By the age of 10–15
years, the child succumbs to death.
The Gaucher cells, which are the reticuloendothelial cells with glucosylceramide, have a
wrinkled-paper appearance due to intracytoplasmic substrate inclusions. These cells stain strongly
with periodic acid-Schiff (PAS) stain. This is characteristic of Gaucher's disease.
Other options:
Option B - Acid sphingomyelinase deficiency results in Niemann-Pick disease types A and B. Both
are autosomal recessive conditions. Type A has a neurodegenerative course, while type B is
non-neuronopathic. The former presents with hepatosplenomegaly, lymphadenopathy, and
psychomotor retardation. Death occurs by the age of 3 years. Type B is associated with
hepatosplenomegaly and mild pulmonary involvement.
Option C - Hexosaminidase deficiency causes Sandhoff disease. It has an autosomal recessive
pattern of inheritance. Clinical features include ataxia, dysarthria, and mental retardation.
Option D - Glucose-6-phosphatase deficiency leads to von Gierke disease, which is a glycogen
storage disorder.  It is an autosomal recessive condition. The affected children have characteristic
doll-like facies. Hyperuricemia, lactic acidosis, and triglyceridemia can be
seen. Hepatomegaly and renal enlargement is also associated.

subject: Biochemistry
question:
The electron transport chain is a series of redox reactions that result in ATP synthesis. Which
of the following is a cytochrome complex IV inhibitor?
explanation:
Cyanide is an inhibitor of cytochrome complex IV or cytochrome a+a3 (cytochrome c oxidase). It
is a component of the electron transport chain.
The electron transport chain (ETC) is a specialized set of electron carriers that can accept
electrons. Energy-rich molecules like glucose are oxidized to produce water and carbon dioxide.
The intermediate substrates in these reactions can pass on electrons to certain coenzymes such as
nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide (NAD+) and flavin adenine dinucleotide (FADH2). The
reduced forms can then donate electrons to the ETC by which they lose most of their free energy
which is used to move H+ through the inner mitochondrial membrane. The H+ gradient thus
produced results in the production of ATP. ATP synthesis together with electron transport is
883
known as oxidative phosphorylation.
The reactions in oxidative phosphorylation and the arrangement of complexes that form the ETC
are shown below.

[Biochemistry-NEET-2022-10-exp-image]
These complexes can accept or donate electrons to coenzyme Q (CoQ) and cytochrome c, which
are mobile electron carriers. Certain site-specific inhibitors can inhibit ATP synthesis.
Other options:
Option B - Carbon dioxide along with water is produced by the oxidation of energy-rich molecules
in oxidative phosphorylation.
Option C - Oligomycin, an antibiotic, blocks the flow of protons through ATP synthase. This
inhibits oxidative phosphorylation.
Option D - Ouabain is a cardiac drug that inhibits the Na+ - K+ ATPase of cell membranes by
acting on the extracellular surface.
Complexes of 
ETC
Inhibitors
Complex I
Piericidin A, amobarbital, rot
enone
Complex II
TTFA (thenoyltrifluoroaceton
e), carboxin, malonate
Complex III
BAL(British anti-lewisite), an
timycin A
Complex IV
Hydrogen sulfide, carbon mo
noxide,cyanide
884

subject: Biochemistry
question: Which of the following zymogens requires gamma-glutamyl carboxylase?

options.b: Factors II, VII, IX, and X

explanation:
Clotting factors II, VII, IX, and X are zymogens of serine proteases that contain glutamic acid
residues. These residues undergo vitamin-K-dependent carboxylation via gamma-glutamyl
carboxylase to form gamma-carboxyglutamic acid residues. These residues chelate calcium ions,
allowing the binding of clotting factors to the platelet surface.
Zymogens are the proprotein form of enzymes (proenzymes) that require proteolytic activation to
carry out their function.
The reduced form of vitamin K acts as a cofactor in the post-synthetic modification of these
zymogens and their activation. This reduced form of vitamin K is recycled back from its oxidized
form by the enzyme vitamin K epoxide reductase, which is inhibited by warfarin.
The absence of vitamin K or the presence of warfarin inhibits carboxylation of the glutamate
residues, leading to the release of abnormal precursors of clotting factors in the blood.

[Biochemistry-NEET-2023-1-exp-image]

subject: Biochemistry
question:
A patient presented with diarrhea, dermatitis, and dementia. Which of the following vitamin
deficiencies would you suspect in this patient?
explanation:
The given clinical scenario of a patient with diarrhea, dermatitis, and dementia is suggestive of
niacin/vitamin B3 deficiency.
890
Niacin deficiency causes pellagra, found mostly among people eating corn/maize-based diets,
which are low in niacin and tryptophan. Excess leucine in jowar and maize disrupts the
conversion of tryptophan to niacin.
Niacin can be synthesized in the body from tryptophan. Two compounds, nicotinic acid and
nicotinamide, have the biological activity of niacin. The nicotinamide ring of the coenzymes
nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide (NAD) and nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide phosphate
(NADP) participate in oxidation/reduction reactions.
The initial symptoms of pellagra include loss of appetite, generalized weakness, irritability,
abdominal pain, and vomiting. This is followed by bright red glossitis and a characteristic
pigmented, scaly skin rash in sun-exposed areas. This rash is known as Casal’s necklace when it
forms a ring around the neck.
The 4Ds of pellagra:
• Diarrhea
• Dermatitis
• Dementia, leading to
• Death
It is diagnosed by measuring urinary levels of 2-methyl nicotinamide and 2-pyridone.
It is treated by oral supplementation of 100–200 mg of nicotinamide or nicotinic acid three times
daily for 5 days.
Casal’s necklace finding in niacin deficiency:

[Biochemistry-NEET-2023-2-exp-image]
Other options:
Option B: Vitamin B1/Thiamine deficiency can manifest as wet beriberi (CVS features like heart
failure and edema) or Shoshin beriberi, an acute and fulminant form of wet beriberi, dry beriberi
(peripheral neuritis), or as Wernicke encephalopathy with Korsakoff psychosis (in
alcoholics). Dietary deficiency of thiamine inhibits pyruvate dehydrogenase. Chronic alcoholics
may develop potentially fatal pyruvic and lactic acidosis.
891
Option C: Vitamin B6/Pyridoxine deficiency is rare but has been observed in alcoholics, newborns,
formulas low in vitamin B6, and women taking oral contraceptives. Isoniazid, an anti-TB drug,
leads to pyridoxine deficiency resulting in peripheral neuropathy. Hence pyridoxine
supplementation is included in ATT regimens.
Option D: Vitamin B12/Cobalamin deficiency causes megaloblastic anemia, dementia, spinal cord
degeneration, homocysteinemia, etc.

subject: Biochemistry
question:
A young boy presents to the OPD with hypoglycemia and muscle cramps, on exertion or while
playing. Then he becomes normal after resting for a while. These episodes are recurrent after
a period of activity. He has decreased serum lactate and glucose levels. Which of the following
diseases is he most likely to be suffering from?
explanation:
A clinical scenario of a patient with muscle cramps, pain, weakness with exertion, myoglobinuria,
and the muscle biopsy specimen showing vacuolar myopathy are suggestive of McArdle's
disease, which is due to the deficiency of muscle glycogen phosphorylase.
McArdle disease, also known as type V glycogen storage disorder, is an autosomal
recessive condition. It typically presents during late childhood or in the second decade of life.
The muscle glycogen phosphorylase enzyme deficiency interferes with ATP generation by
glycogenolysis in the muscles. This causes muscle glycogen accumulation.
Symptoms are exercise intolerance associated with muscle cramps. Due to high-intensity exercise,
sudden persistent muscle contraction occurs. This leads to severe muscle damage resulting in a
massive release of muscle proteins and myoglobin in the blood. Myoglobinuria secondary
to rhabdomyolysis leads to the passage of burgundy-colored urine in some patients.
A unique feature associated with McArdle is the improvement in symptoms after rest for a few
minutes. This is termed as the second wind phenomenon. This occurs due to the increased blood
flow to the muscles and increased utilization of free fatty acids (FFAs) for energy production after
resting for a few minutes. This compensates for the impaired ATP generation from glycogenolysis.
Patients with McArdle disease can benefit from engaging in regular aerobic exercises, such as
walking or cycling, which can help increase their exercise tolerance and cardiovascular fitness.
However, they should avoid high-intensity exercises or activities that can trigger muscle cramps
and rhabdomyolysis. Glucose administration before exercise can improve tolerance. Vitamin B6 is
also recommended.
Standard diagnosis includes muscle biopsy, which shows periodic acid Schiff (PAS)-positive
vacuoles (Shown in the image below) of high glycogen content due to lack of myophosphorylase
and genetic testing (PYGM gene sequencing).
892

[Biochemistry-NEET-2023-3-exp-image]
Other options:
Option B: Hers disease (type VI glycogen storage disorder) - It is caused by the deficiency of liver
glycogen phosphorylase. Patients with Hers disease exhibit growth retardation, hepatomegaly,
and mild hypoglycemia. They do not typically present with muscle cramps and myoglobinuria.
Option C: Cori's disease (type III glycogen storage disorder) - It is caused by a deficiency of
glycogen debranching enzyme. Patients with Cori's disease present with hepatomegaly, growth
retardation, and hypoglycemia. Muscle weakness may be present but not muscle cramps or
myoglobinuria.
Option D: Andersen disease (type IV glycogen storage disorder) - It is caused by the deficiency of
glycogen branching enzyme. This disease presents with hepatomegaly, cirrhosis, and liver failure.
It does not cause muscle cramps or myoglobinuria.

subject: Biochemistry
question:
885
A family consumes only polished rice. Which of the following combination of vitamin
deficiency and enzymatic defect will be present in this family?
explanation:
The consumption of polished rice leads to thiamine deficiency which leads to defective
transketolase activity.
Polished rice is rice that has had its husk, bran, and germ removed, leaving only the endosperm.
This processing method results in a loss of nutrients, including thiamine.
Thiamine pyrophosphate (Vit B1) is the cofactor of transketolase. This reaction requires two
cofactors:
• Mg2+
• Thiamine diphosphate (vitamin B1)
Transketolase and transaldolase are involved in the non-oxidative phase of the HMP pathway.
Thiamine deficiency can lead to beriberi, a condition characterized by peripheral neuropathy,
