915

[Biochemistry-INI-CET-2023-11-exp-image]

subject: Biochemistry
question:
Which of the following is not involved in the repair of DNA following double-stranded
breaks?
explanation:
MSH2 is involved in mismatch repair and is not involved in the repair of double-stranded DNA
breaks as the repair involves the correction of a single mismatch base pair e.g. C to A, instead of T
to A.
DNA mismatch repair proteins function as spell checkers during DNA replication by correcting
the incorrect pairing. In HNPCC, the proofreading function of the mismatch repair systems
(majorly MLH1 and MSH2) is lost, leading to the accumulation of errors throughout the genome.
The genomes of these patients show microsatellite instability, which is characterized by changes in
the length of short repeats.
Double-strand breaks (DSBs) in DNA pose a significant concern.  Genetic recombination events
can give rise to chromosomal translocation, chromosomal breakage, and ultimately cellular
demise. Double-strand breaks (DSBs) have the potential to undergo repair through two distinct
mechanisms: homologous recombination and non-homologous end joining. Homologous
recombination is observed in yeasts, whereas in mammals, non-homologous end joining
predominates.
916

[Biochemistry-INI-CET-2023-12-exp-image]
Non-homologous end-joining (NHEJ) is a mechanism by which a group of proteins mediates the
recognition, processing, and direct ligation of the ends of two DNA fragments where a
double-strand break in DNA has occurred. This is in contrast to homologous repair (HR), another
mechanism, where an undamaged homologous DNA template is required to replace an adjacent
damaged one.
The type of repair depends on the phase of cell cycles:
Other options:
Option A: The protein known as ataxia telangiectasia mutated (ATM) serves as a crucial kinase
that triggers a wide range of cellular stress responses through its signalling activity. The activation
of ATM is induced by DNA double-strand breaks (DSBs) and oxidative stress, resulting in the
phosphorylation of a wide range of target proteins. Recent advancements in molecular biology
have brought to light the participation of the ataxia telangiectasia mutated (ATM) protein in a
range of cellular mechanisms triggered by DNA damage. These mechanisms encompass the repair
of double-strand breaks (DSBs), the halting of cell cycle progression through checkpoint
activation, the initiation of programmed cell death (apoptosis), and the cessation of
transcriptional activity. The compromised precision of repair resulting from the combinational
failure of stress responses leads to heightened sensitivity to ionizing radiation (IR) in
ataxia-telangiectasia (A-T) cells.
Option B: CtIP which is a DNA repair protein involved in Homologous Repair whose function is
dependent on phosphorylation at serine 327 as well as the recruitment of BRCA 1.
Option D: The Ku heterodimer, consisting of Ku70 and Ku80 subunits, plays a pivotal role in the
nonhomologous end-joining (NHEJ) pathway, which is responsible for the repair of DNA
double-strand breaks (DSBs). Ku protein mediates the binding of the damaged DNA fragment and
orchestrates the recruitment of additional proteins to enable the subsequent processing and
ligation of the broken DNA end.
917
Phaseofthecellcycl
e
Repairmechanism
G0 /G1 Phase
Nonhomologous End Joining
 Repair
S, G2, M Phase
Homologous Repair

subject: Biochemistry
question: 2-3 BPG binds to?
explanation:
2,3 bisphosphoglycerate binds to the globin chains of the hemoglobin while oxygen binds to the
heme part of the hemoglobin.
A single unit of 2,3-BPG forms a complex with one tetrameric unit of deoxyhemoglobin within the
central cavity of its four subunits. The centre pocket of this structure contains two -globin chains
that are positively charged, such as histidine and lysine. Ionic bonds, also known as salt bridges,
are established through the interaction between amino acids that carry a positive charge,
specifically those found in globins, and phosphate groups that possess a negative charge,
specifically those present in 2,3-BPG.  The stabilization of deoxygenated haemoglobin (in its
T-form) is achieved through the cross-linking of the α-chains by the binding of 2,3-BPG. Upon
oxygenation of haemoglobin, the molecule 2,3-BPG is released from its binding site, resulting in
the transformation of oxyhemoglobin into the R-form conformation.
A relaxed (R) configuration, exposes more O2 binding sites. The quaternary structure of
haemoglobin determines its affinity for O2. In deoxyhemoglobin, the globin units are tightly
bound in a tense (T) configuration, which reduces the affinity of the molecule for O2. When O2 is
first bound, the bonds holding the globin units are released, producing a relaxed (R)
configuration, which exposes more O2 binding sites. The net result is a 500-fold increase in O2. In
tissues, these reactions are reversed, resulting in O2  dissociation.

[Biochemistry-INI-CET-2023-13-exp-image]
918

subject: Biochemistry
question: Which of the following is complementary to the active site of the enzyme?
explanation:
The substrate is complementary to the active site of the enzyme.
The Lock and Key concept was put forth by Emil Fischer. This model is the initial proposition
aimed at elucidating the mechanism of an enzymatic catalytic process. Based on this concept, it
can be inferred that the enzyme's structure or conformation exhibits a state of rigidity. The
substrate effectively conforms to the binding site, which is now referred to as the active site, in a
manner analogous to a key fitting precisely into its corresponding lock or a hand fitting snugly
into a well-fitting glove. The active site of an enzyme can be described as a stiff and pre-configured
template that selectively accommodates a certain substrate for binding.
The induced fit or Koshland theory states that the active site does not possess a firm and
pre-determined conformation. The emerging active site exhibits the fundamental characteristics
of the substrate binding site. The substrate's contact with the enzyme elicits a conformational
change in the enzyme, leading to the establishment of a robust binding site for the substrate.
Moreover, as a result of induced fit, the enzyme undergoes conformational changes that lead to
the repositioning of the specific amino acids involved in the formation of the active site, hence
facilitating the catalytic process. The induced fit model has garnered substantial empirical support
through X-ray diffraction studies. Koshland's model additionally elucidates the mechanisms by
which allosteric modulators and competitive inhibition exert their effects on enzymes.
The substrate strain theory states that the substrate experiences strain as a result of the
conformational alteration generated by the enzyme. It is plausible that upon the binding of a
substrate to the preexisting active site, the enzyme elicits a strain on the substrate. The presence
of a stressed or strained substrate facilitates the generation of the desired product. The enzymatic
action is believed to involve a combination of the induced fit model and substrate strain.

[Biochemistry-INI-CET-2023-14-exp-image]
Other options:
Option A: The complementary active site of the enzyme binds to the substrate to form the
product.
Option C: The active site of the enzyme is not complementary to the allosteric activator. Allosteric
sites are separate and specific sites on the enzyme that are modulated with allosteric effect
919
modifiers and hence are complementary to them.
Option D: The active site of the enzyme binds to the substrate which form a transitional state
which is unstable and later dissociates to form a product.

subject: Biochemistry
question:
906
Cytochrome P450 monooxygenase utilises which of the following cofactors during the phase 1
reaction of detoxification of xenobiotics?
explanation:
The microsomal cytochrome P450 system, located in the liver, facilitates the process of drug and
foreign substance detoxification through hydroxylation processes that rely on the availability of
NADPH.
Phase 1 reactions include oxidation, reduction, or hydrolytic reaction. CYPs- Cytochrome P family
of enzymes play a major role in phase 1 reactions. Reactions carried out by phase 1 enzymes
usually lead to the inactivation of a drug. The addition of functional groups does little to increase
the water solubility of the drug but alters its biological properties.
The importance of NADPH is enumerated as follows:
• NADPH is needed for the reductive biosynthesis of fatty acids and steroids, hence HMP shunt is
more active in lipogenesis tissues like adipose tissue and the liver.
• Along with Glutamate dehydrogenase helps in the synthesis of various amino acids.
• Maintaining reduced glutathione is critical for RBC membrane integrity. NADPH keeps
haemoglobin's ferrous iron (Fe2+) lowered to prevent methemoglobin (Fe3+) buildup.
• High concentrations of NADPH are also required to maintain the transparency of the lens.
The cytochromes are a class of proteins that possess a conjugated heme group. The latter is
comprised of a porphyrin ring containing an iron atom. The heme group present in cytochromes
exhibits distinct characteristics when compared to the heme group observed in the structures of
haemoglobin and myoglobin. The heme iron within cytochromes undergoes a cyclic process of
oxidation (Fe3+) and reduction (Fe2+), playing a crucial role in facilitating electron movement
throughout the electron transport chain (ETC). This stands in opposition to the heme iron found
in haemoglobin and myoglobin, which remains in the ferrous (Fe2+) form. The cytochromes were
initially assigned the designations of a, b, and c based on the specific heme type they contained
and their corresponding absorption spectra. Subsequent discoveries led to the identification of
additional cytochromes, including c1, b1, b2, a3, among others.
Other options
Option A: Flavin adenine mononucleotide (FMN) is a phosphorylated form of riboflavin which
functions as a cofactor for various enzymes. Some of these include as a cofactor for blue-light
photoreceptors and oxidoreductases.
Option C:  Flavin adenine dinucleotide (FAD) is nothing but phosphorylated riboflavin and is used
in the electron transport chain and also is a cofactor for various enzymes. Some of these include
oxidative phosphorylation, DNA repair, beta-oxidation of fatty acids as well as amino acid
catabolism.
Option D: Nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide (NAD) consists of the vitamin B3 (niacin) and also
serves as a coenzyme for various enzymatic reactions in the body. Some of these include oxidative
phosphorylation (ATP production), DNA repair and intracellular calcium signalling.
920

subject: Biochemistry
question: Which biochemical cycle/process is used by monoclonal antibodies in hybridoma technology?
explanation:
It is the purine salvage pathway that is utilized by monoclonal antibodies in hybridoma
technology.
Monoclonal antibodies refer to the antibodies produced by a group of lymphocytes that produce
antibodies against a single antigen. These antibodies are produced by hybridoma technology.
In this technology, the B-lymphocytes of mice spleen are immunized with an antigen to produce a
specific antibody. These specific antibody-producing B lymphocytes are then fused with myeloma
cells that can multiply indefinitely.
The myeloma cells are deficient in the hypoxanthine-guanine-phosphoribosyltransferase (HGPRT)
gene and do not produce immunoglobulins. Therefore, the resulting hybrid cell is producing
antibodies against a single antigen indefinitely. These hybrid cells are cultured in a HAT medium
(containing hypoxanthine, aminopterin, and thymidine) and the monoclonal antibodies are
obtained. It is well-established that nucleotides present in DNA and RNA consist of purine and
pyrimidine bases. The synthesis of these nucleotides can occur through either the De novo
pathway or the salvage pathway. Consequently, the inclusion of aminopterin in the HAT medium
effectively inhibits the De novo pathway, thereby rendering the synthesis of purine dependent on
the salvage pathway. However, it should be noted that the salvage pathway exclusively takes place
in the presence of the enzymes HGPRT and thymidine kinase. The hybridoma cell is uniquely
capable of proliferating on the HAT medium due to its distinct composition. Specifically, the
myeloma component of the hybridoma possesses the capacity for repetitive cellular division, while
the plasma cell component contains the enzyme HGPRT. Consequently, the hybridoma cells can
sustain growth and viability over an extended period when cultivated on the HAT medium.
Hybridoma technology is a method for producing large numbers of identical antibodies
(monoclonal antibodies).
Hybridomas are somatic cell hybrids produced by fusing the antibody-forming spleen cells with
myeloma cells. The resultant hybrid thus has both the antibody-producing ability of the B-cell and
the ability to multiply indefinitely of the myeloma.
The following are the steps involved in hybridoma technology:
• Mice are immunized with an antigen.
• After several weeks of immunization, blood samples are obtained from mice for measurement of
serum antibodies.
• When the antibody titer is high enough, mice are commonly boosted by injecting antigen
without adjuvant, intraperitoneally or intravenously.
• Then the mice are euthanized and their spleens removed for in vitro hybridoma cell production.
• Fusing antibody-producing spleen cells, which have a limited life span, with myeloma cells
(derived from an immortal tumour of lymphocytes) results in a hybridoma that is capable of
unlimited growth.
• The cells are cultured in a medium containing hypoxanthine, aminopterin, and thymidine (HAT
medium) which allows only the fused cells to survive in culture.
921
• Monoclonal antibodies are then harvested.
Applications of hybridoma technology
• Listing lymphocyte subpopulations
• Specific cell quantitative preparation
• As an immunosuppressant in therapeutic settings
• Blood component nephelometric assays
• ELISA testing
• Quantitative purification of antigens.
• Common therapeutic applications for monoclonal antibodies

[Biochemistry-INI-CET-2023-16-exp-image]

subject: Biochemistry
question: Glycogen phosphorylase requires which of the following for its activation?
explanation:
Glycogen phosphorylase requires the presence of calcium to attain its fully activated state.
Glycogen phosphorylase is an enzyme required in glycogenolysis.
In the muscle: During muscle contraction → Ca++ is released → Ca++ binds to calmodulin
forming Ca++-Calmodulin complex → activates phosphorylase kinase from b form (inactive form)
without phosphorylation → significant Ca++ is required to activate glycogen phosphorylase.
Hormonal regulation of glycogenolysis:
Glucagon (in liver) and epinephrine (in liver and muscle) acts via GPCR to activate adenylyl
cyclase to catalyse synthesis of cAMP. cAMP dependent protein kinase activates phosphorylase
kinase from inactive(b) to active(a) form, which in turn activates glycogen phosphorylase to
active(a) form, hence leading to increased glycogenolysis. (Option D)
Option A: Insulin is a known inhibitor of glycogen phosphorylase which dephosphorylates the
glycogen phosphorylase enzyme thus rendering it inactive.
922
Option B: Glucose 6 phosphate is also an inhibitor of glycogen phosphorylase which
dephosphorylates it thus rendering it inactive.
The regulation of glycogen phosphorylase is shown in the image below:

[Biochemistry-INI-CET-2023-17-exp-image]
923

subject: Biochemistry
question:
The following Lineweaver-Burk plots depict enzymatic action in the presence of enzyme
inhibitors. The enzyme action without inhibitor is represented by a blue line. What type of
inhibitors are depicted in images 1 and 2?

[Biochemistry-INI-CET-2024-1-image]
explanation:
929
The Lineweaver-Burk plot in image 1 depicts non-competitive inhibition, and that in image 2
depicts competitive inhibition.
Important parameters in a reaction include:
• The affinity of the enzyme for its substrate, given by the Michaelis constant or Km. A low Km
reflects a high affinity of the enzyme for substrate and vice versa. The value of Km is given by the
substrate concentration at which the reaction velocity is equal to one-half Vmax. It does not
change with changes in enzyme concentration.
• Vmax is the maximum velocity of a reaction.
Competitive inhibition is when the inhibitor binds reversibly to the substrate-binding site and
competes with the substrate for the active enzyme-binding site. This effect can be overcome by
increasing the substrate concentration by which maximum velocity can still be achieved.
Therefore, Vmax is unaltered in this type of inhibition. However, the concentration of the
substrate required to achieve half of the maximum velocity is increased. Therefore, Km is
increased in this condition.
In a Lineweaver-Burk plot, the addition of a competitive inhibitor causes plots with the same
y-intercept (Vmax is unaffected) but different x-intercepts (due to increased Km).

[Biochemistry-INI-CET-2024-1-exp-image]
Noncompetitive inhibition is by binding of inhibitors to enzymes in locations remote from the
active site. Increasing the concentration of the substrate does not overcome non-competitive
inhibition. Thus, noncompetitive inhibitors decrease the Vmax of the reaction. They do not
interfere with the binding of the substrate to the enzyme. Thus, the enzyme shows the same Km in
the presence or absence of noncompetitive inhibitors.
In a Lineweaver-Burk plot, the addition of a non-competitive inhibitor causes plots with the same
x-intercept (Km is unaffected) but different slopes and y-intercepts (due to decreased Vmax).
930

[Biochemistry-INI-CET-2024-1-exp-image]
In uncompetitive inhibition, inhibitors bind with the enzyme-substrate intermediate complex and
inhibit it. This leads to a decrease in both Vmax and Km.

[Biochemistry-INI-CET-2024-1-exp-image]

subject: Biochemistry
question: FISH technique helps to identify all except?
explanation:
Fluorescent in situ hybridization (FISH) is not useful in identifying point mutation. Point
mutations are identified by other means like sequencing studies and restriction fragment length
polymorphism.
FISH is a method of molecular analysis of genetic lesions involving large deletions, duplications,
or more complex rearrangements that are not readily amenable to detection using polymerase
chain reaction or DNA sequencing approaches.
931
FISH uses DNA probes that recognize sequences specific to particular chromosomal regions. To
perform FISH, large fragments of cloned genomic DNA spanning up to 200 kb are labeled with
fluorescent dyes and applied to metaphase chromosome preparations or interphase nuclei. The
probe hybridizes to its homologous genomic sequence and thus labels a specific chromosomal
region that can be visualized under a fluorescent microscope.
FISH can be performed on prenatal samples, peripheral blood cells, touch preparations from
cancer biopsies, and even fixed archival tissue sections. It is used to detect numeric abnormalities
of chromosomes (aneuploidy), subtle microdeletions and complex translocations that are not
demonstrable by routine karyotyping, and gene amplification (e.g., HER2 in breast cancer or
NMYC amplification in neuroblastomas).

[Biochemistry-INI-CET-2024-2-exp-image]

subject: Biochemistry
question:
924
Which of the following micronutrients are associated with decreased sexual maturation and
hypogonadism?
explanation:
Zinc deficiency is associated with decreased sexual maturation and hypogonadism.
Zinc is the second most common trace element in the body after iron. As a component of zinc
finger proteins, zinc regulates gene transcription and participates in nucleic acid metabolism,
protein synthesis and thereby cell growth. Zinc is a major antioxidant being part of the enzyme
superoxide dismutase.
Zinc is derived mainly from animal protein. Diets based on cereals, plants and legumes are
associated with zinc deficiency due to dietary phytic acid decreasing the bioavailability of zinc.
Zinc is absorbed throughout the small intestine by a process of facilitated diffusion. Almost 90%
of the total body zinc is localized in bone and skeletal muscle.
Deficiency -
• Poor physical growth is an important feature of zinc depletion in preschool and school-age
children. Delayed sexual maturation and hypogonadism are prominent features of zinc deficiency
in adolescents. Other features include anemia, anorexia, diarrhea, alopecia, impaired immune
function and poor wound healing.
932
• Acrodermatitis enteropathica is an autosomal recessive disorder of severe zinc deficiency, caused
by impaired intestinal absorption due to a defect in intestinal zinc transporter protein. It presents
in early infancy with vesiculobullous, dry, scaly, or eczematous skin lesions chiefly in the
periorificial and acral areas.
Normal requirements for children range between 3.5 and 5.0 mg/day. Acquired zinc deficiency
stats can be treated with 0.5 to 1.0 mg/kg/day for several weeks. Malnourished children have
much higher requirements of 2-4mg/kg/day.
Other options :
Option B - Copper deficiency results in microcytic, hypochromic anemia unresponsive to iron
therapy, depigmentation of hair, neutropenia, neurological problems and osteoporosis. Disruption
in copper transport is linked to Wilson's disease and Menke's disease.
Option C - Selenium deficiency is a cause of Keshan disease, a form of cardiomyopathy in young
children seen in some regions of China. In combination with iodine deficiency, lack of selenium
can result in myxedematous endemic cretinism.
Option D - Iron deficiency causes anemia, irritability, anorexia, pica, angular stomatitis, leg
cramps and congestive heart failure.

subject: Biochemistry
question: Which of the following is not a heme breakdown product?
explanation:
Aminolevulinic acid (ALA) is not a product of heme breakdown; rather it is an intermediate in the
synthesis of heme molecules.
The process of heme breakdown occurs over a series of reactions. In the reticuloendothelial
system of the liver, spleen and bone marrow, heme is degraded by microsomal heme oxygenase. It
requires NADPH and cytochrome c. In this process, the alpha methenyl bridge of the heme
molecule is liberated as carbon monoxide. The Fe+ liberated is oxidized to Fe3+++ and taken up
by transferrin.
The linear tetrapyrrole formed is biliverdin (green pigment), which is further reduced to bilirubin
(red-yellow pigment) by an NADPH-dependent biliverdin reductase. The bilirubin is transported
in the plasma bound to albumin and is taken up by the liver, where it is conjugated with
glucuronic acid to make it water-soluble, which is finally excreted in the bile.
The intestinal bacteria deconjugate the bilirubin, which is reduced to urobilinogen. It is further
reduced to stercobilinogen, which is excreted through feces.
933

[Biochemistry-INI-CET-2024-4-exp-image]

subject: Biochemistry
question:
Which type of mutation results in the reversal to wild type of phenotype when the mutant
gene is suppressed?
explanation:
Mutations in tRNA are known to suppress the mutant gene and cause reversal to wild phenotype.
Such mutations are called suppressor mutations.
Suppressor mutations are secondary mutations that alter the phenotypes caused by an existing
mutation. This is not to be confused with reversion mutations in which the original mutation has
been lost. Instead, suppressor mutations return the phenotype caused by the original mutation to
a more wild-type phenotype despite the presence of the original mutation. The suppressor
mutation’s effect on the phenotype can vary from partial restoration to full restoration of the
wild-type phenotype.
In prokaryotic and lower eukaryotic organisms, abnormally functioning tRNA molecules have
been discovered that are themselves the results of mutations. Some of these abnormal tRNA
molecules are capable of binding to and decoding altered codons, thereby suppressing the effects
of mutations in the structural genes.
These suppressor tRNA molecules, usually formed as a result of alterations in their anticodon
regions, are capable of suppressing certain missense mutations, nonsense mutations, and
frameshift mutations. An example of suppressor mutation is illustrated below.
A primary mutation occurs changing the original codon sequence from UGG (Trp) to UAG (Stop).
A secondary mutation occurs in the gene for a glutamine tRNA leading to the anticodon switch
from a GUC to AUC. This mutant tRNA can recognize the UAG stop codon and insert glutamine in
place of the stop, allowing translation to continue past the stop and suppressing the original
mutation.
934

[Biochemistry-INI-CET-2024-5-exp-image]
In the laboratory context, such suppressor tRNAs, coupled with mutated variants of aminoacyl-
tRNA synthetases, can be utilized to incorporate unnatural amino acids into defined locations
within altered genes that carry engineered nonsense mutations. The resulting labeled proteins can
be used for in vivo and in vitro cross-linking and biophysical studies.

subject: Biochemistry
question: Which of the following is not a post-transcriptional modification?
explanation:
Glycosylation is not a post-transcriptional modification; it is a post-translational modification.
A primary transcript is the initial, linear, RNA copy of a transcription unit. The collection of all
the primary transcripts synthesized in the nucleus by RNA polymerase II is known as
heterogeneous nuclear RNA (hnRNA). The pre-mRNA components of hnRNA undergo extensive
co- and posttranscriptional modification in the nucleus and become mature mRNA. These
modifications usually include the following :
• Addition of a 5′ cap - This is the first of the processing reactions for pre-mRNA. The cap is a
7-methylguanosine attached to the 5′ terminal end of the mRNA and is resistant to most
nucleases. Creation of the cap requires the removal of the γ-phosphoryl group from the 5′
triphosphate of the pre-mRNA, followed by the addition of guanosine monophosphate.
Methylation of this terminal G occurs in the cytosol and is catalyzed by guanine-
7-methyltransferase. Additional methylation steps may occur (options A and B).
• Addition of a 3′-poly-A tail - Most eukaryotic mRNA have a chain of 40 to 250 adenylates
(adenosine monophosphates) attached to the 3′ end. This poly-A tail is added by the nuclear
enzyme, polyadenylate polymerase. The poly-A tail terminates eukaryotic transcription. In
addition, it helps stabilize the mRNA, facilitates its exit from the nucleus, and aids in translation.
• Splicing - Maturation of eukaryotic mRNA usually involves removal from the primary transcript
of RNA sequences (introns) that do not code for protein (endonuclease cleavage). The remaining
coding (expressed) sequences, the exons, are joined together to form the mature mRNA. The
process of removing introns and joining exons is called splicing. The molecular complex that
accomplishes these tasks is known as the spliceosome (option D).
935

subject: Biochemistry
question:
925
Match the following conditions :
explanation:
The correct answer is option B: 1 -c, 2 -d, 3 -b, 4 -a
Von Gierke disease (type I glycogen storage disorder) occurs due to the deficiency of
glucose-6-phosphatase enzyme in the liver, kidney, and intestinal mucosa. Patients present
commonly by 3-4 months of age with hypoglycemia (leading to seizures), lactic acidosis, and
hepatomegaly. Affected children often have a doll-like face with fat cheeks, relatively thin
extremities, short stature, and a protuberant abdomen due to massive hepatomegaly. The kidneys
are also enlarged, whereas spleen and heart are not involved. Laboratory findings include
hypoglycemia, lactic acidosis, hyperuricemia, and hyperlipidemia.
Pompe's disease (type II glycogen storage disorder) occurs due to a mutation in
alpha-1,4-glucosidase (also known as acid maltase), a lysosomal enzyme. A defect in this enzyme
leads to the accumulation of glycogen in the lysosomes of peripheral, voluntary muscles and
progresses to involve the cardiac muscle and diaphragm. Clinical features include hypotonia
(floppy baby syndrome), cardiomyopathy, hepatomegaly, and failure to thrive. Myozyme can
prevent worsening or can reverse cardiac and skeletal abnormalities.
Cori disease (type III glycogen storage disorder) occurs due to a deficiency of the glycogen
debranching enzyme. The features of Cori disease include hypoglycemia, hepatomegaly, and
hyperlipidemia but with normal blood lactate and uric acid levels. Muscle weakness and atrophy
can also occur in these patients. Liver biopsy shows the accumulation of glycogen in the form of
limit-dextrins (branched structures).
McArdle disease (type V glycogen storage disorder) typically presents during late childhood or in
the second decade of life. The muscle glycogen phosphorylase enzyme deficiency interferes with
ATP generation by glycogenolysis in the muscles. This causes muscle glycogen accumulation.
Symptoms are exercise intolerance associated with muscle cramps. This leads to severe muscle
damage resulting in a massive release of myoglobin in the blood.
1) Glycogen accumulation in l
iver and renal cells and lactic
 acidemia, associated with hy
poglycemia
c) Von Gierk diseas
e
2) Glycogen accumulation in 
lysosomes
d)Pompe disease
3) Exercise intolerance
b)McArdle disease
4) Accumulation of branched
 structures
a)Cori disease

subject: Biochemistry
question: All are true statements regarding folate deficiency except

options.a: Increased levels of homocysteine and normal methylmalonic acid
options.b: Folate supplementation on diagnosis of pregnancy prevents neural tube defect
options.c: It is important to treat co-existing vitamin B12 deficiency before initiating folate therapy
options.d: Folate deficiency is commonly seen in children with hemolytic anemia

explanation:
Folate deficiency is seen in children with macrocytic anemia and not with hemolytic anemia.
936
Folic acid (vitamin B9) is the synthetic form of folate used in supplements and fortification of
foods. Folic acid plays a key role in one-carbon metabolism, essential for the biosynthesis of
several compounds. Tetrahydrofolate (THF), the reduced, coenzyme form of folate, receives
one-carbon fragments from donors such as serine, glycine, and histidine and transfers them to
intermediates in the synthesis of amino acids, purine nucleotides, and thymidine monophosphate
(TMP), a pyrimidine nucleotide incorporated into DNA.
Leafy vegetables such as spinach, turnip, lettuce and fortified cereal products are rich sources.
Folate deficiency is caused by increased demand (e.g., pregnancy and lactation), poor absorption
caused by pathology of the small intestine, alcoholism, or treatment with drugs (e.g.,
methotrexate) that are dihydrofolate reductase inhibitors. Children with hemolytic anemia have
high folate requirements due to increased erythropoiesis predisposing them to deficiency (option
B). Its manifestations are :
• Anemia -  A primary result of folic acid deficiency is megaloblastic anemia, caused by diminished
synthesis of purine nucleotides and TMP, which leads to an inability of cells to make DNA and,
therefore, an inability to divide.
• Neural tube defects - Maternal deficiency of folate, during pregnancy, is implicated in neural
tube defects. Periconceptional folate supplements, begun 1 month before conception and
continued for 3 months after, are recommended to reduce the risk.
• Hyperhomocysteinemia - Folate, along with vitamin B12, is involved in converting homocysteine
to methionine, as depicted below. Hence, folate deficiency results in increased levels of
homocysteine.

[Biochemistry-INI-CET-2024-8-exp-image]
However, levels of methylmalonic acid are normal. Methylmalonic acid levels are elevated in
deficiency of vitamin B12, which is required as a coenzyme in the conversion of methylmalonyl
CoA to succinyl CoA (option A).
937

[Biochemistry-INI-CET-2024-8-exp-image]
Treatment: In patients with megaloblastic anemia, it is imperative to exclude and treat vitamin
B12 deficiency before treating with folate. Otherwise the neurological signs of vitamin B12
deficiency may develop and progress irreversibly (option C). Deficiency is corrected using folic
acid at a dose of 0.5-1mg/day orally for 3-4 weeks.

subject: Biochemistry
question: Congenital lactic acidosis is seen due to a defect in which of the following enzymes?
explanation:
Congenital lactic acidosis is seen due to a defect in pyruvate dehydrogenase.
The major source of acetyl CoA for the tricarboxylic acid (TCA)cycle is the oxidative
decarboxylation of pyruvate by the multienzyme pyruvate dehydrogenase complex (PDHC). The
PDHC is a protein aggregate of multiple copies of three enzymes, pyruvate decarboxylase (E1),
dihydrolipoyl transacetylase (E2), and dihydrolipoyl dehydrogenase (E3). Each catalyzes a part of
the overall reaction.
A deficiency of the E1 component of the PDHC is the most common biochemical cause of
congenital lactic acidosis. The deficiency results in a decreased ability to convert pyruvate to acetyl
CoA, causing pyruvate to be shunted to lactate via lactate dehydrogenase. This creates particular
problems for the brain, which relies on the TCA cycle for most of its energy and is particularly
sensitive to acidosis. Symptoms include neurodegeneration, muscle spasticity, and, in the
neonatal-onset form, early death. The inheritance pattern is X- linked dominant, with both males
and females affected. Although there is no proven treatment for PDHC deficiency, dietary
restriction of carbohydrates and supplementation with thiamine may reduce symptoms in select
patients.
Other options :
Option B - Branched-chain ketoacid dehydrogenase deficiency results in maple syrup urine
disease. There is defective oxidative decarboxylation of leucine, isoleucine and valine. The disease
is characterized by feeding problems, ketoacidosis, changes in muscle tone, and coma.
938
Option C -  Defective transketolase is a rare disorder of pentose phosphate metabolism with
symptoms apparent in infancy including developmental delay and intellectual disability, delayed
or absent speech, short stature, and congenital heart defects.
Option D -  Isocitrate dehydrogenase is an enzyme of the TCA cycle which converts isocitrate to
alpha-ketoglutarate. Defects in this enzyme are linked to carcinogenesis.

subject: Biochemistry
question: Which of the following is not associated with G6PD deficiency?

options.a: Equal incidence in males and females
options.c: Predisposes the patient to oxidative stress

explanation:
G6PD Deficiency does not have an equal incidence in males and females. It is an X-linked
recessive (XLR) disorder, hence males are more affected than females.
Glucose-6-phosphate dehydrogenase (G6PD) deficiency causes congenital hemolytic anemia due
to enzyme deficiency. G6PD is required to generate NADPH while oxidizing glucose-6-phosphate
in the hexose monophosphate pathway (HMP). G6PD deficiency leads to reduced NADPH levels
(option B) which then reduces the level of reduced glutathione. Reduced glutathione is required to
protect the cells against oxidative stress to RBCs and hemolysis.
Clinically, most patients are asymptomatic. When exposed to triggers like fava beans, infections,
drugs such as primaquine, chloroquine, sulfonamides/sulfones, dapsone, and nitrofurantoin
patient develops:
• Episodic hemolysis, both intravascular and extravascular
• Hemolytic anaemia
• Hemoglobinemia
• Hemoglobinuria
Increased oxidative damage to RBCs leads to the formation of denatured precipitates known as
Heinz bodies. These bodies can damage the RBC membrane leading to intravascular hemolysis.
Additionally, when such RBCs pass through the splenic cords, macrophages pluck out these
precipitates, giving them the characteristic appearance of bite cells.
The images below show the presence of Heinz bodies and bite cells.

[Biochemistry-INI-CET-2024-10-exp-image]
939

[Biochemistry-INI-CET-2024-10-exp-image]

subject: Biochemistry
question:
Which of the following is not a neurotransmitter derived from aminoacids ?
926
explanation:
Creatine is not a neurotransmitter derived from amino acids.
Creatine is an intermediate product in the process of formation of creatinine. The steps involved
in the formation are as follows:
• The amidino group of arginine is transferred to glycine to form guanidoacetic acid. This step
occurs in the kidneys and pancreas.
• Guanido acetic acid is methylated by S-adenosyl methionine to form creatine. This step occurs in
the liver.
Creatine is phosphorylated to creatine phosphate by the enzyme creatine kinase, which is present
in the muscle, brain and liver. The reaction needs hydrolysis of ATP. The stored creatine
phosphate in the muscle serves as an immediate store of energy in the muscle. During muscle
contraction, the energy is first derived from ATP hydrolysis. Thereafter, the ATP is regenerated by
the hydrolysis of creatine phosphate. This is called the Lohmann's reaction.
The creatine phosphate may be converted to its anhydride, creatinine, which is excreted in the
urine. Creatinine level in the blood is an indicator of renal function.
Other options :
Option A - Gamma amino butyric acid (GABA) functions in brain tissue as an inhibitory
neurotransmitter. It is formed by decarboxylation of glutamate by L-glutamate decarboxylase.
Option B - Dopa is a precursor in the formation of the neurotransmitters dopamine,
norepinephrine and epinephrine. It is formed by the hydroxylation of tyrosine by tyrosine
hydroxylase.
Option C - 5 Hydroxytryptamine is an important neurotransmitter in the brain, where it functions
as an antidepressant. It is derived from the amino acid tryptophan, in a process of hydroxylation
followed by decarboxylation.
940

subject: Biochemistry
question:
Which of the following methods is used to test the blood taken from a neonate for metabolic
disease screening?
explanation:
Tandem mass spectrometry is used to screen a blood sample for metabolic diseases.
Mass spectrometry is a very sensitive quantitative analytical technique used to measure a wide
range of clinically relevant analytes. It identifies molecules based on their mass or molecular size.
A sample is vaporized such that a positive charge is produced on the molecule. An electric field is
applied so that particles move. At the same time, a magnetic field is applied, so the cations are
deflected and hit on the detector. For molecules of identical charge, the time required for the
molecule to reach the detector is inversely proportional to the mass of the molecule.
In tandem mass spectrometry, two mass spectrometers (MS) are arranged sequentially. The first
MS separates big peptides, based on their mass. From this observation, the parent ion can be
directed into the second MS. Here the ions collide with argon gas molecules and are broken into
smaller ions. These daughter ions are detected and quantified by their mass spectrum in the
second instrument.
Tandem MS is used to screen blood samples from newborns for the presence and concentrations
of amino acids, fatty acids and other metabolites. Abnormalities in metabolite levels can serve as
diagnostic indicators for a variety of genetic disorders like phenylketonuria.

subject: Biochemistry
question: Familial hypercholesterolemia is a result of a defect in which of the following?
explanation:
Familial hypercholesterolemia occurs due to a defect in the LDL (low-density lipoprotein)
receptor.
The primary function of LDL particles is to provide cholesterol to the peripheral tissues (or return
it to the liver). They do so by binding to plasma membrane LDL receptors that recognize apo
B-100.
After binding, the LDL–receptor complex is endocytosed. The pH of the endosome falls, which
allows the separation of the LDL from its receptor. The receptors can be recycled, whereas the
lipoprotein remnants in the vesicle are transferred to lysosomes and degraded by lysosomal acid
hydrolases, releasing free cholesterol, amino acids, fatty acids, and phospholipids. These
compounds can be reutilized by the cell.
Defects in the synthesis of functional LDL receptors cause a significant elevation in plasma
LDL-C. Patients with such deficiencies have type IIa hyperlipidemia /familial
hypercholesterolemia (FH). This autosomal dominant condition predisposes individuals to
premature atherosclerosis and coronary artery disease.
Patients with homozygous FH must be treated aggressively to delay the onset and progression of
CVD. Two drugs that reduce the hepatic production of VLDL and thus LDL, a small-molecule
inhibitor of the microsomal TG transfer protein (MTP) and an antisense oligonucleotide to apoB,
and an antibody that inhibits ANGPLT3 are approved and should be considered in patients who
have insufficient response to statins and PCSK9 inhibitors. LDL apheresis should be considered in
patients who have persistently elevated LDL-C levels despite drug therapy.
Other options :
941
Option B - Defects in apo-E lead to type III hyperlipoproteinemia / familial
dysbetalipoproteinemia.  It is characterized by defective clearance of chylomicron remnants and
intermediate-density lipoproteins by the liver. This leads to hypercholesterolemia, xanthomas,
and atherosclerosis.
Option C - Defective apo-C II results in type I hyperlipoproteinemia/ familial lipoprotein lipase
deficiency. The resulting inactive lipoprotein lipase leads to slow clearance of chylomicrons and
VLDL, and low levels of LDL and HDL. Patients present with eruptive xanthomas and acute
pancreatitis.
Option D - Apo-B 48 is involved in the assembly of chylomicrons. Defects in this protein are not
associated with familial hypercholesterolemia.

subject: Biochemistry
question:
In a patient with Diabetes Mellitus, the liver produces more TG and VLDL. What is the cause
of the same?

options.a: Decreased action of LPL with increased action of HSL
options.b: Decreased action of LPL with Decreased action of HSL
options.c: Increased action of LPL with increased action of HSL
options.d: Increased action of LPL with decreased action of HSL

explanation:
In a patient with Diabetes Mellitus, the liver produces more TG and VLDL, due to decreased
action of LPL with increased action of HSL.
• Lipoprotein lipase (LPL): Enzyme responsible for hydrolyzing triglycerides (TG) in VLDL and
chylomicrons into free fatty acids (FFAs) for storage or energy use in tissues.
• Hormone-sensitive lipase (HSL): Enzyme that breaks down stored triglycerides in adipocytes
into free fatty acids and glycerol.
Insulin has an inhibitory effect on hormone-sensitive lipase (HSL). In diabetes, low insulin
corresponds to an increase in HSL activity. This promotes the breakdown of lipids and a release of
TG and free fatty acids from the adipose tissue.
Insulin induces the activity of lipoprotein lipase (LPL) which is present on the capillary walls. LPL
promotes hydrolysis of TG and the degradation of the transport proteins, VLDL and
chylomicrons. In diabetes, low insulin leads to decreased LPL activity which corresponds to an
increase in VLDL and TG.
This results in increased production of triglycerides and VLDL in the liver.

subject: Biochemistry
question:
927
Nutritional deficiency of Copper, required by lysyl oxidase which is an importent key step for
formation for covalent cross linking in collagen synthesis, gives rise to which of the following
condition?
explanation:
Nutritional deficiency of copper, required by lysyl oxidase for formation for covalent cross linking
in collagen synthesis, gives rise to Menke's disease.
Menke's disease is an X-linked recessive condition caused due to a mutation in the ATP7A gene on
chromosome X. This causes impaired transport of dietary copper from the small intestine to the
liver leading to low levels of copper-containing enzymes.
The final step of collagen synthesis involves the formation of covalent cross-links. This is done by
the enzyme lysyl oxidase, a copper-containing protein. This is essential for the strength and
rigidity of collagen. As a result of deficiency of copper in Menkes disease, this step of collagen
synthesis is affected.
942
The patients presents with depigmented kinky hair, failure to thrive, neurodegenerative
symptoms (loss of developmental milestones, truncal hypotonia, epilepsy), multiple fractures,
anaemia due to ceruloplasmin deficiency, neutropenia, and recurrent infections.
Other Options:
Option B: Scurvy: Caused by vitamin C deficiency, leading to defective collagen synthesis due to
impaired hydroxylation of proline and lysine.
Option C: Ehlers-Danlos syndrome: A group of genetic disorders affecting collagen structure or
production.
Option D: Scoliosis: A structural deformity of the spine.

subject: Biochemistry
question: Match the following with appropriate precursors
explanation:
The correct matched sequence is : i-b, ii-d, iii-a, iv-c.
i. Melatonin – b. Tryptophan
Tryptophan is an essential amino acid and the precursor for the synthesis of melatonin, produced
by the pineal gland. The process begins with tryptophan being converted into serotonin, and then
serotonin is further converted into melatonin.
ii. Histamine – d. Histidine
Histamine is derived from the amino acid histidine. The conversion of histidine into histamine is
catalyzed by the enzyme histidine decarboxylase. Histamine is involved in local immune
responses, acts as a neurotransmitter, and plays a role in regulating gastric acid secretion.
iii. Norepinephrine – a. Tyrosine
Norepinephrine (noradrenaline) is synthesized from tyrosine. Tyrosine is first converted to
L-DOPA by the enzyme tyrosine hydroxylase, and then L-DOPA is converted into dopamine.
Finally, dopamine is hydroxylated to form norepinephrine. Norepinephrine is a neurotransmitter
involved in the sympathetic nervous system's fight-or-flight response.
iv. Glutathione – c. Cystine
Glutathione is a tripeptide composed of glutamate, cystine, and glycine. The cystine component is
crucial because it contains a thiol (-SH) group, which is responsible for the antioxidant properties
of glutathione.
i. Melatonin
b. Tryptoph
an
ii. Histamine
d. Histidine
iii. Norepinephrin
e
a. Tyrosine
iv. Glutathione
c. Cystine
943

subject: Biochemistry
question:
In the current scenario treatment for urea cycle disorder is available. Administration of
amino acid phenylbutyrate can decrease ammonia formation. Which amino acid provides
phenylbutyrate?
explanation:
The amino acid that provides phenylbutyrate in the treatment of urea cycle disorders is glutamate.
Ammonia to Urea cycle: Ammonia gets converted to glutamate (non toxic form) through
transamination.  Glutamate is transported to the liver as glutamine (by glutamine synthase). In
the liver, ammonia is released from glutamine, converting it to alpha keto glutatamte. The
released ammonia enters the urea cycle.
In acetylation therapy, Phenylbutyrate (a prodrug) which is metabolized to phenylacetate.
Phenylacetate combines with glutamine (a nitrogen-rich amino acid) to form
phenylacetylglutamine. Phenylacetylglutamine is water-soluble and is excreted in the urine. This
process helps trap excess nitrogen, bypassing the urea cycle.
Other drugs that can be used to achieve the same outcome (ammonia scavengers) include:
• Phenylacetate
• Benzoate
Other Options:
Option A and C: Arginine and Aspartate do not play a role in this specific reaction.
Option D: Glycine: The combination of benzoate and glycine forms hippuric acid, a non-toxic
compound. When Benzoate is administered to the body, it acts as a nitrogen-scavenging agent.
Hippuric acid is water-soluble and is excreted in the urine, effectively removing one nitrogen
molecule from the body per molecule of hippuric acid formed. This mechanism is similar to how
phenylacetate acts as a scavenger for glutamate.

subject: Biochemistry
question: Which among the following are appropriate techniques for chromosomal aneuploidy?
explanation:
In situ hybridization, Conventional cytogenetics, Sanger sequencing and Polymerase chain
reaction are appropriate techniques for chromosomal aneuploidy.
Techniques to detect aneuploidy:
• In situ hybridization: Fluorescent In Situ Hybridisation (FISH) helps in the detection of specific
genetic information using fluorescent probes. FISH is used to detect chromosomal aneuploidy,
subtle microdeletions and rearrangements, complex translocations, and gene amplification.
• Conventional cytogenetics: This technique involves the study of chromosomes under a
microscope, typically using methods like karyotyping, which arranges chromosomes by size,
shape, and number. The analysis allows the identification of large chromosomal abnormalities,
such as aneuploidy or structural changes.
• Sanger sequencing: DNA sequencing that uses chain-termination to identify the exact sequence
of nucleotides in a DNA molecule. Simple Senger sequencing can detect aneuploidy only with
massive parallel sequencing.
• Polymerase chain reaction: Polymerase chain reaction (PCR) is a rapid automated method for
the amplification of specific DNA sequences (or genes). It can accurately quantify the target
nucleic acid sequence within a sample. It is useful for determining somatic mutation with low
allele frequency. Quantitative Fluorescence (QF) PCR is a method of rapid aneuploidy detection. It
944
is less expensive and allows simultaneous processing of large number of samples of FISH.
• Array Comparitive Genomic Hybridization: microarray based technique which can reveal
imbalances across the genome.
• Multiplex Ligation Probe Amplification: to detect gene dosage.

subject: Biochemistry
question: Enzyme used in recombinant DNA technology are?
explanation:
Phosphatase, Terminal transferase and Crispr Cas9 are used in recombinant DNA technology.
Recombinant DNA technology is a technique where DNA from different sources is combined to
create new genetic material. Applications of recombinant DNA technology include producing
insulin, growth hormones, vaccines, gene therapy, and studying gene functions and disease
mechanisms.
Phosphatase dephosphorylates 5′ ends of RNA and DNA. It is primarily used for the removal of
phosphate groups pror to kinase labelling and it is also used to prevent self ligation.
Terminal transferase catalyzes the addition of nucleotides to the 3' terminus of a DNA molecule. It
is used in homopolymer tailing.
CRISPR-Cas9 acts as an RNA-directed nuclease. It is a DNA editing/gene regulatory system.  It
involves two essential components: Cas9, an endonuclease that causes a double-stranded DNA
break, allowing modifications to the genomic DNA to be made; and a synthetic single-guide RNA,
or sgRNA, responsible for guiding Cas9 to its target.
Isomerase is a class of enzymes that catalyze the structural rearrangement of molecules,
converting one isomer into another. Isomerase is not commonly used directly in recombinant
DNA technology, it has broader applications. They are used in metabolic pathways and as
biocatalysts in industrial processes.
945

subject: Biochemistry
question:
A patient underwent gastrectomy for gastric adenocarcinoma. Which of the following
vitamins needs to be supplemented in this patient?
explanation:
A patient who has undergone gastrectomy for gastric adenocarcinoma needs to be supplemented
with vitamin B12.
Vitamin B12 absorption is a complex process that relies on several factors. Intrinsic factor, a
glycoprotein secreted by the parietal cells in the stomach, is essential for this process. Gastric acid
and pepsin release vitamin B12 from dietary proteins, allowing it to bind with cobalophilin in the
saliva. In the jejunum, cobalophilin is broken down, freeing vitamin B12 to bind with intrinsic
factor. This complex is then absorbed in the distal ileum through specific receptors.  Following
gastrectomy, the production of intrinsic factor is impaired due to the removal of gastric tissue,
making vitamin B12 absorption difficult. Therefore, vitamin B12 supplementation is necessary
after gastrectomy to prevent deficiency.
Other complications of gastrectomy include:
• Nutritional deficiencies- Iron deficiency anemia (most common), Vitamin B12 deficiency, and
osteoporosis
• Dumping syndrome: early and late
• Diarrhea
• Duodenal stump leak/blow out
• Bile reflux gastritis and esophagitis
• Gall stones
• Roux syndrome

subject: Biochemistry
question:
A 10-year-old child from a southern Indian state, hailing from a poor family, presents with
rough and scaly skin. The child’s diet lacks vegetables and fruits. After treatment with linseed
oil, Vitamin A, and Vitamin B, the symptoms subsided. What is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation:
The cutaneous lesions with rough and scaly skin, with dietary history stating lack of vegetables
and fruits is suggestive of phrynoderma.
Phrynoderma are keratotic follicular papules commonly present over the elbows and knees. They
can also extend to the shoulders, neck, back, thighs, buttocks, and abdomen. It is also known
955
as toad skin, due to the rough cone-shaped elevated papules. It can caused by deficiency of
vitamins A, B complex, C, and E and essential fatty acids.
When it occurs due to vitamin A deficiency, it is associated with ophthalmic symptoms like
xerophthalmia, night blindness, and Bitot's spots and cutaneous symptoms like xerosis, scaling,
and acne.
Management includes nutritional improvement through diet, multivitamin supplementation, and
treatment of the underlying cause.
Note: Linseed oil is rich in alpha-linolenic acid, which is an essential fatty acid.
The toad skin appearance of a child with phrynoderma is given below:

[Biochemistry-NEET-2024-2-exp-image]
Other options:
Option A: Lichen planus is a papulosquamous lesion presenting as pruritic, purple, polygonal,
planar, papules and plaques. These papules are often highlighted by white dots or lines called
Wickham striae, which are created by areas of hypergranulosis. The characteristic lesions in lichen
planus are shown below:
956

[Biochemistry-NEET-2024-2-exp-image]
Option B: Erythroderma is a serious skin condition characterized by widespread redness
(erythema) and scaling affecting more than 90% of the body's surface area. It is also known as
exfoliative dermatitis and often results from underlying skin disorders, systemic diseases, or drug
reactions.
Option D: Keratosis pilaris is an inherited abnormality of keratinization. It affects the follicular
orifices leading to keratotic follicular plugging, perifollicular erythema, and follicular atrophy. It
usually occurs during puberty. They present as small, keratotic papules on the extensor aspects of
the limbs, particularly the arms which can lead to ingrowing of the hair. Treatment is purely
reserved for cosmetic purposes.

[Biochemistry-NEET-2024-2-exp-image]

subject: Biochemistry
question:
A patient presents to the emergency department with severe abdominal pain, nausea, and
vomiting. The laboratory results show serum triglyceride levels > 2000 mg/dl. Which
deficiency could be contributing to the patient's condition?
explanation:
957
The elevated triglyceride levels and symptoms of acute pancreatitis are likely related to a
deficiency in apolipoprotein C II.
Apolipoprotein C2 is a lipoprotein lipase cofactor that catalyzes cleavage of
chylomicrons. Defective apo C II results in type I hyperlipoproteinemia/ familial lipoprotein lipase
deficiency. The resulting inactive lipoprotein lipase leads to slow clearance of chylomicrons and
VLDL, and low levels of LDL and HDL. Patients present with eruptive xanthomas and acute
pancreatitis.
Lipoprotein lipase is an extracellular enzyme that is present in the capillary walls of most tissues.
It is chiefly synthesized by the adipose tissue, and cardiac and skeletal muscles. A deficiency of
lipoprotein lipase or apo C-II leads to type 1 hyperlipoproteinemia or familial chylomicronemia
which leads to an increased risk of acute pancreatitis.
Other options:
Option B: Apo B48 is involved in the assembly of chylomicrons. Defects in this protein are not
associated with familial hypercholesterolemia.
Option C: Apo B100 binds low-density lipoprotein receptors. For the uptake of LDL, apo B100
acts as a ligand for the low-density lipoprotein receptor.
Option D: Apo A1 activates lecithin cholesterol acyltransferase (LCAT). LCAT is the enzyme
present in HDL, which is activated by apo A1. LCAT converts free cholesterol (amphipathic) in
HDL to cholesterol ester(hydrophobic). The initial discoidal shape now becomes spherical. This
facilitates reverse cholesterol transport.

subject: Biochemistry
question:
946
After the age of 12, the boy had an intellectual disability. On examination, fingernail
abrasions, excessive biting of lips, and increased levels of serum uric acid were observed.
What is the likely diagnosis?
explanation:
The above clinical scenario with findings showing self-mutilation and laboratory values of
hyperuricemia is most likely suggestive of Lesch-Nyhan disease.
Lesch-Nyhan syndrome is an X-linked recessive disorder due to a complete deficiency of the
hypoxanthine-guanine phosphoribosyltransferase (HGPRT). This deficiency results in an inability
to salvage hypoxanthine or guanine leading to increased levels of phospho-ribosyl pyrophosphate
(PRPP). Increased PRPP leads to increased purine synthesis, which is later degraded to uric acid.
Increased hypoxanthine is also converted to uric acid.
The patients have intellectual disability, behaviors of self-injury like lip and finger biting,
developmental delay, muscle dystonia, and extrapyramidal symptoms.
Diagnosis is made by the elevated levels of uric acid and by HGPRT enzyme assay.
The image given below shows the metabolism of hypoxanthine and guanine by HGPRT.
958

[Biochemistry-NEET-2024-4-exp-image]
Orange diaper syndrome can be an early indicator of Lesch-Nyhan syndrome. At birth, individuals
with Lesch-Nyhan syndrome typically appear asymptomatic, with normal prenatal growth and
development. The condition is often first detected through the presence of orange-coloured
crystals in the diaper, resulting from hyperuricemia. Since hyperuricemia is a common feature
across all variants of HGPRT deficiency, which causes Lesch-Nyhan syndrome, predicting
associated neurodevelopmental abnormalities requires enzyme activity testing.

subject: Biochemistry
question:
A neonate was brought with features of lethargy, and poor oral intake. On examination, a
cataract was seen. Urine Benedict's test was positive but negative for Glucose. What will be
the enzyme deficiency in this condition?
explanation:
The given clinical scenario of a neonate presenting with lethargy, and poor oral intake, with
examination revealing a cataract and urine benedict's test was positive but negative for glucose, is
suggestive of classical galactosemia and enzyme deficient is galactose-1-phosphate uridyl
transferase.
Galactose-1-phosphate uridyl transferase deficiency leads to classical galactosemia. It is
an autosomal recessive disorder. Normally, newborns receive up to 40% of their intake as lactose
(glucose + galactose). The deficiency of this enzyme leads to an inability to convert
galactose-1-phosphate to uridine diphosphate galactose. This leads to the accumulation
of galactose 1-phosphate and causes injury to the kidney, liver, and brain.
Clinical features:
• Accumulation of galactose-1-phosphate in the liver causes liver damage & leads to
hepatomegaly, vomiting, and convulsions.
• Accumulation of galactose-1-phosphate depletes the liver of inorganic phosphate leading to
diminished ATP synthesis which affects gluconeogenesis resulting in hypoglycemia.
• Accumulation of galactose-1-phosphate in the nerves causes intellectual disability.
• Accumulation of galactitol in the lens causes oil-drop cataracts.
• Patients with galactosemia are at an increased risk of developing Escherichia coli neonatal
sepsis.
959
In uridyl transferase deficiency, the epimerase is present in adequate amounts, so that the
galactosemic individual can still form UDPGal from glucose. This explains how normal growth
and development of affected children can occur despite the galactose-free diets.
Urinary reducing sugar (Benedict's test) and mucic acid test are positive. Direct enzyme assay
using erythrocytes establishes the diagnosis.
Treatment is a galactose-free diet.

[Biochemistry-NEET-2024-5-exp-image]
Other options:
Option A: Hexokinase is a glycolytic enzyme, involved in maintaining intracellular glucose
concentration by catalysing the first step in glucose metabolism, i.e., phosphorylation of
intracellular glucose.  Its deficiency leads to hemolytic anaemia. Splenomegaly may be another
presenting feature.
Option B: Galactokinase is the enzyme responsible for the conversion of galactose to galactose
1-phosphate. Only cataract is seen in the deficiency of this enzyme because galactose-1-phosphate
is not formed. Hence no toxic effects of this compound are manifested.
Option D: Phosphofructokinase is a key regulatory enzyme of glycolysis and is necessary for the
ATP-dependent conversion of fructose-6-phosphate to fructose-1,6-diphosphate. Tauri's disease is
type VII glycogen storage disorder that results due to the deficiency of phosphofructokinase.

subject: Biochemistry
question:
A patient diagnosed with a rare homozygous recessive condition presented with ectopia
lentis. In the peripheral smear, there was no megaloblastic anemia. Later, he was found to
have elevated homocysteine levels in the blood. Which of the following supplementations will
help the patient?
explanation:
The vitamin that can be used as supplementation in this patient with homocystinuria is
pyridoxine (vitamin B6).
Classical homocystinuria is an autosomal recessive disease most commonly caused by a deficiency
of the cystathionine β-synthase. The deficiency of this enzyme leads to an accumulation of
homocysteine in the blood and a clinical syndrome of intellectual disability, subluxated ocular
lenses, long and slender extremities, arachnodactyly, and arterial and venous thrombi. The
skeletal features of homocystinuria often mimic Marfan syndrome. Folic acid and vitamin B12
960
deficiency can also lead to homocystinuria with megaloblastic anemia.

[Biochemistry-NEET-2024-6-exp-image]
Demonstration of elevated total homocysteine in the blood confirms the diagnosis. 50% of the
cases of homocystinuria are termed pyridoxine-responsive because large doses of pyridoxine can
enhance the activity of deficient enzymes in these patients. Administration of large doses of
pyridoxine (vitamin B6) restores the activity of the deficient enzyme in half of all cases of
homocystinuria.
Folate supplementation is also added to overcome any folate deficiency that might occur due to
increased conversion of homocysteine to methionine. For the other 50% of the patients, the
treatment consists of methionine restriction and cysteine supplementation. In addition, betaine
(methyl group donor), folate and vitamin B12 may be added.

subject: Biochemistry
question:
You received a text message from your friend JOHN DOE about occupational exposure after
he visited his doctor as shown below. What is the test used to diagnose?
947

[Biochemistry-NEET-2024-7-image]
explanation:
The test for coproporphyrinogen levels is used as a screening test to diagnose lead poisoning.
Lead inhibits the enzymes delta-aminolevulinic acid (ALA) dehydratase and ferrochelatase, which
are involved in heme synthesis. ALA is converted to porphobilinogen by ALA dehydratase,
inhibited by lead. So, serum porphobilinogen levels are decreased in lead poisoning.
Laboratory findings include the following:
• An elevated serum lead level
• An elevated level of protoporphyrin in blood and elevated ALA and coproporphyrinogen in urine
• Microcytic anemia with basophilic stippling in peripheral smear due to ineffective heme
synthesis
Clinical features of chronic lead poisoning include facial pallor, anemia, basophilic stippling of red
cells, blue lines in the gums, and retinal stippling. In the later phase, the findings are colic
abdominal pain, constipation, nerve palsy, encephalopathy, and disturbance of the genito-urinary
and cardiovascular systems.
961

g/L
